Role of Thermodynamic Laws. Energy Targeting Methods of Heat Exchanger Networks: Composite Curve Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role of Thermodynamic Laws. Energy Targeting Methods of Heat Exchanger Networks: Composite Curve Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Role of Thermodynamic Laws in Process Integration

### Formal Academic Definition
**Process Integration** is a holistic design philosophy that emphasizes the *unified* analysis and synthesis of a complete chemical process, with the primary objective of optimizing material and energy usage across the entire flowsheet rather than at individual unit operations. It treats the plant as a single integrated system whose components are interdependent.

The entire theoretical edifice of Process Integration rests upon the two pillars of classical thermodynamics:

**First Law of Thermodynamics (Law of Energy Conservation / The First Pillar):**
> *"Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another."*

In the language of Process Integration, this translates to: **For any process or heat exchanger network (HEN), the total energy released by hot streams must equal the total energy absorbed by cold streams plus any net work or utility duty.** Mathematically:

$$\sum_{i} Q_{Hot,i} = \sum_{j} Q_{Cold,j} + Q_{Utility}$$

This law dictates the **energy balance** of the network and is the foundation for **Energy Targeting** — the computation of the minimum theoretical energy requirement before any network is drawn.

**Second Law of Thermodynamics (Law of Entropy / The Second Pillar):**
> *"The total entropy of an isolated system can never decrease over time; it is at best constant (reversible) and typically increases (irreversible)."*

In Process Integration, the Second Law imposes a fundamental restriction: **heat cannot flow spontaneously from a colder body to a hotter body.** This is the conceptual origin of the **Pinch** — a temperature bottleneck where minimum temperature driving force ($\Delta T_{min}$) governs the entire network. The Second Law therefore dictates the **feasibility** of heat exchange.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The First Law sets the *quantitative target* (how much energy is required), while the Second Law sets the *qualitative feasibility boundary* (what is thermodynamically achievable). The **Composite Curve Method** is a graphical tool that visually marries both laws on a single temperature–enthalpy ($T$–$H$) diagram.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Shower Mixer Analogy:** Imagine you are adjusting a shower. Turning on only hot water gives scalding water; only cold water gives a chilling experience. A good plumber installs a *thermostatic mixing valve* that automatically balances hot and cold streams to deliver a *target temperature* using the minimum extra heating. **Process Integration does the same thing for an entire chemical plant** — it asks, "What is the minimum external (utility) heating and cooling we must add to make all the hot and cold streams in the plant self-balance to a target temperature (the pinch)?" The Composite Curve is essentially a plot showing the "supply" of hot energy vs. the "demand" of cold energy, where the overlap is what we recover, and the gap at the ends is what we pay for with utilities.

### The Two Conceptual Outcomes
1. **Quantitative Outcome (1st Law):** Determination of the **Minimum Energy Requirement (MER)** — the absolute theoretical minimum external hot utility ($Q_{H,min}$) and cold utility ($Q_{C,min}$) the plant must consume.
2. **Qualitative Outcome (2nd Law):** Identification of the **Pinch Point** — the location in temperature space where the network is most constrained.

Standard Metrics Used in This Module:
- **$\Delta T_{min}$**: Minimum allowable temperature approach (typically **10 °C** for liquids, **20 °C** for gases).
- **MER**: Minimum Energy Requirement (in **kW** or **MW**).
- **Pinch Temperature ($T_{pinch}$)**: The point of zero temperature driving force.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stream Identification — The Pre-requisite

Before any composite curve can be drawn, all process streams in the plant must be classified:

| Stream Type | Symbol | Energy Role | Utility Required if Not Integrated |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot Stream** | Requires cooling from $T_s$ to $T_t$ | Heat **donor** | Cooling water / Refrigerant |
| **Cold Stream** | Requires heating from $T_s$ to $T_t$ | Heat **acceptor** | Steam / Hot oil / Furnace |
| **Utility Stream** | External heating/cooling medium | Energy import | — |

For each stream, the heat duty (load) is computed as:

$$Q_i = m_i \cdot C_{p,i} \cdot \Delta T_i = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{supply} - T_{target})$$

where $C_p$ is the specific heat capacity in **kJ/(kg·K)** and $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate in **kg/s**.

## 2.2 The Composite Curve — Conceptual Architecture

A **Composite Curve** is constructed by *stacking* the individual stream enthalpy–temperature ($H$–$T$) profiles in a temperature interval. There are two composite curves:

- **Hot Composite Curve (HCC):** The cumulative enthalpy released by all hot streams over the plant's temperature range.
- **Cold Composite Curve (CCC):** The cumulative enthalpy absorbed by all cold streams over the plant's temperature range.

When plotted on the same $T$–$H$ axis:
- The **horizontal overlap** between the HCC and CCC represents **heat recovery** (process-to-process exchange).
- The **left-end gap** (CCC extending beyond HCC) at the top represents **minimum hot utility $Q_{H,min}$**.
- The **right-end gap** (HCC extending beyond CCC) at the bottom represents **minimum cold utility $Q_{C,min}$**.
- The **point of closest vertical approach** is the **Pinch Point**, equal to $\Delta T_{min}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A composite curve is to a chemical plant what an "energy supply-demand curve" is to an economy. By integrating locally, we maximize the overlap and minimize imports.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_i = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q_i$ | Stream heat duty | kW |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | $\Delta T_{LM}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | °C or K |
| 3 | $T^*_{hot} = T_{hot}$ | $T^*$ | Shifted (interval) temperature, hot side | °C |
| 4 | $T^*_{cold} = T_{cold} - \Delta T_{min}$ | $T^*$ | Shifted temperature, cold side (offset by $\Delta T_{min}/2$) | °C |
| 5 | $\sum Q_{released} = \sum Q_{absorbed}$ | — | Energy balance (1st Law) | kW |
| 6 | $Q_{H,min} = Q_{cold,total} - Q_{rec,max}$ | $Q_{H,min}$ | Minimum hot utility | kW |
| 7 | $Q_{C,min} = Q_{hot,total} - Q_{rec,max}$ | $Q_{C,min}$ | Minimum cold utility | kW |
| 8 | $Pinch = $ closest approach $= \Delta T_{min}$ | — | Zero driving force location | °C |
| 9 | $\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{Q_{rec}}{Q_{hot,total}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$ | Energy recovery efficiency | % |
| 10 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}}$ | $A$ | Heat exchanger area | m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder for Tables:** The notation $\vert \Delta T \vert$ should be read as the absolute value of $\Delta T$. **Never** use the vertical bar `|` character inside the markdown table; it breaks the table parser. Use the word "abs" or the LaTeX `\vert` command in such expressions.

## 2.4 The Pinch Design Rules (Gold Rules of HEN Design)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** These three rules are worth **3–5 marks** if asked as a short question.

1. **Do not transfer heat across the Pinch.** Heat exchange must occur *only* on the same side of the pinch.
2. **Do not use external utilities across the Pinch.** A hot utility must not be placed below the pinch on the cold side, and vice versa.
3. **Minimize the number of units:** $N_{min} = N_{streams} - 1$, where $N_{streams}$ = total number of streams (excluding utilities) on one side of the pinch.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil Refineries:** Used to design crude preheat trains (e.g., reducing furnace fuel by 30–50%).
- **Ethylene Plants:** Optimize quench and cracking furnace integration.
- **LNG Plants:** Minimize the enormous cold energy demand in liquefaction trains.
- **Pulp & Paper:** Recovery boiler integration with drying and evaporation sections.
- **Ammonia Plants:** Syngas cooling and ammonia synthesis heat recovery.

The composite curve method is the **first and most fundamental** tool taught in any Pinch Analysis course, forming the gateway to the **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** and **Total Site Integration**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stream Heat Duty

The fundamental heat balance on a single stream undergoing sensible temperature change (no phase change) is derived from the First Law of Thermodynamics for an open, steady-state system with no work and negligible kinetic/potential energy changes:

$$\frac{dQ}{dt} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot dT$$

Integrating between the supply temperature $T_s$ and target temperature $T_t$:

$$\int_{0}^{Q_i} dQ = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \int_{T_s}^{T_t} dT$$

Evaluating the integral (assuming $C_p$ constant over the interval, valid for moderate temperature spans):

$$Q_i = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_t - T_s)$$

For a hot stream ($T_s > T_t$), the released heat is taken as positive and conventionally defined as a heat load that must be removed. For a cold stream ($T_t > T_s$), the absorbed heat is the load to be supplied.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Composite Curve Construction

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A process has the following streams. Construct the composite curves and determine $Q_{H,min}$, $Q_{C,min}$, and the pinch point. Take $\Delta T_{min} = 20$ °C.

| Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $C_p$ (kW/K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Hot | 250 | 60 | 10 |
| H2 | Hot | 200 | 100 | 15 |
| C1 | Cold | 40 | 230 | 12 |
| C2 | Cold | 120 | 250 | 8 |

### Step 1: Compute individual stream heat duties
$$Q_{H1} = 10 \times (250 - 60) = 1900 \text{ kW}$$
$$Q_{H2} = 15 \times (200 - 100) = 1500 \text{ kW}$$
$$Q_{C1} = 12 \times (230 - 40) = 2280 \text{ kW}$$
$$Q_{C2} = 8 \times (250 - 120) = 1040 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 2: Determine interval boundaries (true temperatures)
Sorted temperatures: 40, 60, 100, 120, 200, 230, 250 °C.
This creates **6 temperature intervals**.

### Step 3: Apply the shift transformation for interval temperatures
For the **hot composite**, use $T$ as-is. For the **cold composite**, shift the cold temperature down by $\Delta T_{min}/2$ to create an equivalent "interval temperature" basis:

| Interval (True) | Interval (Shifted Cold) | $T_{int}$ (°C) | $\Delta T$ (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1: 40 → 60 | 30 → 50 | 40 | 20 |
| H1: 60 → 100 | 50 → 90 | 70 | 40 |
| C2: 100 → 120 | 90 → 110 | 100 | 20 |
| H2: 120 → 200 | 110 → 190 | 150 | 80 |
| H1+H2: 200 → 230 | 190 → 220 | 205 | 30 |
| C1+C2: 230 → 250 | 220 → 240 | 230 | 20 |

### Step 4: Cumulative enthalpy cascade (Hot Composite)
Start at $T = 40$ °C with $H = 0$ and integrate downward:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(60) &= 0 + 10 \times (250-60)\big|_{60\to 40} = 0 \\
H(100) &= H(60) + 10 \times (100-60) = 400 \text{ kW} \\
H(120) &= H(100) + (10+15) \times (120-100) = 900 \text{ kW} \\
H(200) &= H(120) + (10+15) \times (200-120) = 2100 \text{ kW} \\
H(230) &= H(200) + 15 \times (230-200) = 2550 \text{ kW} \\
H(250) &= H(230) + 10 \times (250-230) = 2750 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Cumulative enthalpy cascade (Cold Composite)
Start at the top interval and integrate the cold loads upward:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(250) &= 0 \\
H(230) &= 0 + 12 \times (250-230) = 240 \text{ kW} \\
H(200) &= H(230) + (12+8) \times (230-200) = 840 \text{ kW} \\
H(120) &= H(200) + 8 \times (200-120) = 1480 \text{ kW} \\
H(100) &= H(120) + 12 \times (120-100) = 1720 \text{ kW} \\
H(60) &= H(100) + 12 \times (100-60) = 2200 \text{ kW} \\
H(40) &= H(60) + 12 \times (60-40) = 2440 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Identify the minimum energy targets
The horizontal separation of the two composite curves in the **shifted temperature domain** gives the pinch. The pinch point is identified where the two curves are closest (separation = $\Delta T_{min} = 20$ °C).

From the cascade above, evaluating net surplus/deficit at each interval:

| Interval | Hot release | Cold demand | Net (Hot – Cold) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 40–60 | 200 | 240 | $-40$ |
| 60–100 | 400 | 480 | $-80$ |
| 100–120 | 200 | 80 | $+120$ |
| 120–200 | 800 | 640 | $+160$ |
| 200–230 | 450 | 60 | $+390$ |
| 230–250 | 200 | 240 | $-40$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **pinch region** is at the interface where the net changes from negative to positive, located in the **100–120 °C** true-temperature interval. The exact pinch temperature in the shifted domain is **$T^*_{pinch} = 100$ °C**, corresponding to **$T_{hot,pinch} = 110$ °C** and **$T_{cold,pinch} = 90$ °C** (separated by exactly $\Delta T_{min} = 20$ °C).

**Minimum Energy Requirements:**
$$Q_{H,min} = 440 \text{ kW} \quad \text{(cumulative deficit at the top)}$$
$$Q_{C,min} = 440 \text{ kW} \quad \text{(cumulative surplus at the bottom, by First-Law balance)}$$

## 3.3 Verification via 1st Law Energy Balance

Total hot stream heat available:
$$Q_{H,total} = Q_{H1} + Q_{H2} = 1900 + 1500 = 3400 \text{ kW}$$

Total cold stream heat required:
$$Q_{C,total} = Q_{C1} + Q_{C2} = 2280 + 1040 = 3320 \text{ kW}$$

Maximum possible heat recovery (limited by the smaller value):
$$Q_{rec} = \min(3400, 3320) = 3320 \text{ kW}$$

Therefore:
$$Q_{H,min} = Q_{C,total} - Q_{rec} = 3320 - 3320 = 0 \text{ kW (in best case)}$$

But the **2nd-Law pinch constraint** enforces additional utility duty beyond the trivial 1st-Law minimum. The 440 kW value above represents the *2nd-Law-corrected* minimum — a perfect illustration of why both laws are needed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Process Integration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plant Stream Data Collection] --> B[Classify Hot Cold Streams]
    B --> C[Compute Stream Heat Duties Q = m.Cp.dT]
    C --> D[Define Temperature Intervals]
    D --> E[Construct Hot Composite Curve HCC]
    D --> F[Construct Cold Composite Curve CCC]
    E --> G[Shift Cold Curve by DeltaTmin]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Locate Pinch Point}
    H --> I[Read Q H min from End Gap]
    H --> J[Read Q C min from Other End Gap]
    I --> K[Energy Targeting Complete]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Apply Three Pinch Design Rules]
    L --> M[Design HEN Above Pinch]
    L --> N[Design HEN Below Pinch]
    M --> O[Merge Networks]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Validate 1st and 2nd Laws]
    P --> Q[Final Heat Exchanger Network]
```

## 4.2 Composite Curve Architecture (T–H Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T_H_Plot[T vs H Coordinate System]
        direction LR
        X1[H kW] --- P1
    end
    P1[Pinch Point - Tpinch 110 C] 
    P2[QH min Top End] 
    P3[QC min Bottom End]
    P4[Heat Recovery Overlap Zone]
    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P1 --> P4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** $P_1$ is the pinch point — the *neck* of the composite curves. $P_2$ is the top-end gap filled by hot utility (e.g., steam). $P_3$ is the bottom-end gap filled by cold utility (e.g., cooling water). $P_4$ is the body of the curve where process-to-process heat exchange takes place.

## 4.3 Pinch Decomposition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Main[Complete Process System] --> Above[Above Pinch Subsystem]
    Main --> Below[Below Pinch Subsystem]
    Above --> A1[Heat Sink Net]
    Above --> A2[Requires Q H min]
    Below --> B1[Heat Source Net]
    Below --> B2[Requires Q C min]
    A1 -. NO CROSS-PINCH HEAT TRANSFER .-> B1
    A2 -. NO CROSS-PINCH UTILITY USE .-> B2
```

## 4.4 Stream Categorization Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Process Stream] --> Q1{Is stream temperature higher than pinch?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| H[Hot Stream - Heat Donor]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is stream temperature lower than pinch?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| C[Cold Stream - Heat Acceptor]
    Q2 -->|No| U[Utility Stream]
    H --> Cool[Needs Cooling Utility]
    C --> Heat[Needs Heating Utility]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**State the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics and explain their significance in Process Integration.**

**Model Answer:**
**First Law:** Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it is conserved. In Process Integration, this gives the **energy balance** for the entire network, enabling the calculation of total utility requirements and heat recovery potentials. $\sum Q_{hot} = \sum Q_{cold} + Q_{utility}$.

**Second Law:** Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a colder to a hotter body; entropy of the universe increases. In Process Integration, this **imposes a minimum temperature driving force ($\Delta T_{min}$)** at the *Pinch Point*, defining the theoretical minimum external utilities the plant needs. It prevents the design of thermodynamically infeasible networks.

**Significance:** Together, the First Law tells us *how much* energy must be transferred, and the Second Law tells us *whether such transfer is feasible*. The Composite Curve Method is the tool that visually integrates both laws on a $T$–$H$ diagram.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define the Pinch Point and explain how it is identified on a Composite Curve.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Pinch Point** is the location in the process where the **temperature driving force between the Hot and Cold Composite Curves is at its minimum**, equal to the specified $\Delta T_{min}$ (typically 10–20 °C). At the pinch, heat transfer is most constrained, and any further reduction in $\Delta T_{min}$ would demand disproportionately higher exchanger area or be thermodynamically infeasible.

**Identification Procedure:**
1. Draw the HCC and CCC on the same $T$–$H$ axes.
2. Shift the cold curve to the right by $\Delta T_{min}/2$ to create the *shifted temperature* domain.
3. The pinch appears as the **point of closest vertical approach** between the two curves in this shifted domain.
4. Read the **hot pinch temperature** $T_{hot,pinch}$ and **cold pinch temperature** $T_{cold,pinch}$ such that $T_{hot,pinch} - T_{cold,pinch} = \Delta T_{min}$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

(a) **Explain in detail the construction of Hot and Cold Composite Curves with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of $\Delta T_{min}$ on the energy targets. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction Procedure:**

1. **Step 1 — Data Collection:** List all hot and cold streams with $T_s$, $T_t$, and $\dot{m} C_p$.
2. **Step 2 — Heat Duty Calculation:** Compute $Q_i = \dot{m} C_p (T_t - T_s)$ for each stream.
3. **Step 3 — Interval Definition:** Sort all $T_s$ and $T_t$ values in ascending order; each unique value creates a temperature boundary.
4. **Step 4 — Hot Composite Construction:** For each interval, sum the $C_p$ values of all hot streams passing through that interval. Plot $H$ (cumulative enthalpy change) vs $T$ (interval temperature) as a staircase.
5. **Step 5 — Cold Composite Construction:** Repeat Step 4 for cold streams, but using **shifted temperatures** $T^* = T - \Delta T_{min}/2$.
6. **Step 6 — Plot Together:** Overlay both curves on the same $T$–$H$ axes.

**Sketch (schematic):**
- $Y$-axis: Temperature $T$ (°C). $X$-axis: Enthalpy $H$ (kW).
- HCC: descending staircase from top-left to bottom-right.
- CCC: ascending staircase from bottom-left to top-right.
- Vertical gap at the top = $Q_{H,min}$. Vertical gap at the bottom = $Q_{C,min}$. Closest vertical touch point = **Pinch**.

**Role of $\Delta T_{min}$:**
- **Larger $\Delta T_{min}$** $\Rightarrow$ Smaller heat recovery, **larger $Q_{H,min}$ and $Q_{C,min}$**, **smaller capital cost** (smaller area).
- **Smaller $\Delta T_{min}$** $\Rightarrow$ Larger heat recovery, **smaller utility demand**, **larger capital cost** (larger area).
- The optimum $\Delta T_{min}$ is determined by **total cost = utility cost + capital cost** minimization (cost targeting).

(b) **A refinery has the following streams. Construct the Composite Curves, identify the Pinch Point, and compute $Q_{H,min}$ and $Q_{C,min}$. Take $\Delta T_{min} = 10$ °C. (7 marks)**

| Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $C_p$ (kW/K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Hot | 180 | 40 | 20 |
| H2 | Hot | 150 | 50 | 30 |
| C1 | Cold | 30 | 170 | 25 |
| C2 | Cold | 100 | 200 | 15 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Heat duties:**
$$Q_{H1} = 20 \times (180-40) = 2800 \text{ kW}$$
$$Q_{H2} = 30 \times (150-50) = 3000 \text{ kW}$$
$$Q_{C1} = 25 \times (170-30) = 3500 \text{ kW}$$
$$Q_{C2} = 15 \times (200-100) = 1500 \text{ kW}$$

Total hot $= 5800$ kW; total cold $= 5000$ kW. By 1st Law, $Q_{H,min} \geq 5800 - 5000 = 800$ kW (theoretical lower bound).

**Step 2: Intervals (sorted unique temperatures):**
30, 40, 50, 100, 150, 170, 180, 200 °C → **7 intervals**.

**Step 3: Shifted cold temperatures (subtract $\Delta T_{min}/2 = 5$ °C):**
25, 35, 45, 95, 145, 165, 175, 195 °C.

**Step 4: Heat cascade:**

| Interval (°C) | $T^*$ (°C) | Hot $C_p$ | Cold $C_p$ | Hot Release | Cold Demand | Net |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 30–40 | 30 | 0 | 25 | 0 | 250 | $-250$ |
| 40–50 | 45 | 20 | 25 | 200 | 250 | $-50$ |
| 50–100 | 75 | 20 | 25 | 1000 | 1250 | $-250$ |
| 100–150 | 125 | 50 | 25 | 2500 | 1250 | $+1250$ |
| 150–170 | 160 | 30 | 25 | 600 | 500 | $+100$ |
| 170–180 | 175 | 0 | 25 | 0 | 250 | $-250$ |
| 180–200 | 190 | 0 | 15 | 0 | 300 | $-300$ |

**Step 5: Pinch Location (where net changes sign):**
- Running deficit/surplus cascade from top (180 °C): starting deficit accumulates, then a large surplus appears in the 100–150 °C range. The pinch sits at the **boundary between intervals where the cumulative balance reaches zero from above**.
- Computing cumulative from the top (deficit-positive convention):
  - 180–170: deficit 250 kW
  - 170–150: deficit 250 + 500 = 750 kW
  - 150–100: surplus 750 – 1250 = $-500$ (means 500 kW recovered at this point)
  - The pinch is at **$T^* = 100$ °C** (interface of 50–100 and 100–150).

**Step 6: Final Energy Targets:**
$$Q_{H,min} = 800 \text{ kW} \quad (\text{higher than the 1st-Law lower bound of 800 kW — i.e., this is the optimum})$$
$$Q_{C,min} = 800 \text{ kW}$$

Pinch temperatures: $T_{hot,pinch} = 105$ °C, $T_{cold,pinch} = 95$ °C (separated by 10 °C).

---

### Question B — Choice Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

(a) **Discuss the three "Golden Rules" of Pinch-based HEN design. Why must heat not be transferred across the Pinch? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three Golden Rules are:
1. **No heat transfer across the Pinch.**
2. **No use of external utilities across the Pinch.**
3. **Maximum feasible heat exchange (minimum number of units).**

**Why No Cross-Pinch Heat Transfer:**
The process is divided by the Pinch into two thermodynamically distinct regions:
- **Above the Pinch (Hot Region):** Net heat *sink*. Requires external hot utility.
- **Below the Pinch (Cold Region):** Net heat *source*. Requires external cold utility.

If heat is transferred *across* the pinch (e.g., a hot stream above the pinch heats a cold stream below the pinch), it creates an additional **driving force imbalance**: more hot utility must be added above the pinch to compensate for the heat removed by the cross-pinch exchanger. This *increases* $Q_{H,min}$ and $Q_{C,min}$ beyond the true minimum — a direct violation of energy targeting. The Pinch therefore acts as a **thermodynamic firewall**.

**Why No Cross-Pinch Utility Use:**
If a cold utility is used above the pinch, it absorbs heat that should have been recovered by the cold streams above the pinch. Similarly, a hot utility below the pinch would add heat that should have been removed by the hot streams below. Both inflate utility consumption.

(b) **For a process with the following data, perform energy targeting and design the minimum utility HEN using the Composite Curve Method. Take $\Delta T_{min} = 15$ °C. (7 marks)**

| Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $C_p$ (kW/K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Hot | 250 | 80 | 12 |
| H2 | Hot | 180 | 60 | 8 |
| C1 | Cold | 50 | 200 | 10 |
| C2 | Cold | 140 | 230 | 6 |

**Model Solution Outline (expected steps for full marks):**

1. Compute all four $Q_i$ values.
2. Sort temperatures and identify 6 intervals.
3. Apply the temperature shift ($\Delta T_{min}/2 = 7.5$ °C) to the cold curve.
4. Build the enthalpy cascade for HCC and CCC.
5. Identify the pinch at the sign-change boundary of the cascade.
6. Read $Q_{H,min}$ and $Q_{C,min}$ from the end gaps.
7. Sketch the composite curves and label the pinch.
8. Apply the Golden Rules to obtain the HEN design — a recommended examiner expectation is a **3-utility / 4-stream** minimum network with **stream splitting** at the pinch.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the temperature shift** by $\Delta T_{min}/2$ on the cold curve. Without this shift, the pinch cannot be located correctly.
> 2. **Summing enthalpies instead of cascades.** Composite curves are *cumulative* — students often confuse interval enthalpies with cumulative ones.
> 3. **Ignoring stream $C_p$ differences.** Each stream's slope in $T$–$H$ is $1/C_p$, not 1. Draw proportional slopes.
> 4. **Reporting only the 1st-Law minimum** and ignoring the 2nd-Law pinch penalty.
> 5. **Not labelling units** (kW vs MW) on the enthalpy axis.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **First Law** = Energy conservation $\Rightarrow$ **Quantitative targets** (heat balance).
- **Second Law** = Entropy non-decrease $\Rightarrow$ **Qualitative feasibility** + **Pinch constraint**.
- **Composite Curve** = a $T$–$H$ plot of *cumulative* hot and cold enthalpy loads, used for energy targeting.
- **Pinch Point** = closest vertical approach of the two curves, equal to $\Delta T_{min}$.
- **$Q_{H,min}$** = top-end horizontal gap (deficit that must be supplied by hot utility like steam).
- **$Q_{C,min}$** = bottom-end horizontal gap (surplus that must be removed by cold utility like cooling water).
- **Three Golden Rules** for HEN design: (i) no cross-pinch heat transfer, (ii) no cross-pinch utility use, (iii) minimum units.
- **$N_{min}$ formula** (Stream-based): Above pinch: $U_{H,min} + N_{hot,above} + N_{cold,above} - 1$; analogously for below.
- **$\Delta T_{min}$ trade-off:** Larger value $\Rightarrow$ less recovery, lower capital. Smaller value $\Rightarrow$ more recovery, higher capital. Optimize by **total cost targeting**.
- **Shifted temperature domain** is the *operating* domain where the hot and cold curves must not intersect.
- **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** is the next-level tool (Module 2/3 territory) that incorporates utility placement.
- Always **state units** (kW, °C) explicitly — KTU examiners routinely deduct marks for missing units.
- **Sign convention:** Hot utility $Q_H > 0$ (imported); Cold utility $Q_C > 0$ (exported to environment).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grand Composite Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Grand Composite Curve # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) The **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** is a fundamental graphical tool in **Pinch Analysis** (a core methodology of Process Integration) that represents the **net enthalpy profile** of a process as a function of **shifted temperature** ($T^*$). It is constructed by plotting the cumulative heat availability (or demand) at each shifted temperature level after accounting for all hot and cold streams in the process. $$T^* = T - \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2} \quad \text{(for hot streams)}$$ $$T^* = T + \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2} \quad \text{(for cold streams)}$$ The GCC is a derived representation that simplifies the **Composite Curves (CC)** into a single, clean load-versus-temperature profile, making it the standard visual for targeting minimum utility consumption. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The GCC is the primary tool used to identify the **Pinch Point** (the point of zero net heat flow) and to set targets for **minimum hot utility ($Q_{H,min}$)**, **minimum cold utility ($Q_{C,min}$)**, and the **minimum number of heat exchanger units**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a multi-storey building where each floor represents a temperature range. On each floor, some rooms (cold streams) need heating, while others (hot streams) need cooling. Now think of a moving elevator (the "heat cascade") that starts at the top floor (highest temperature) and descends floor by floor, carrying a certain amount of heat energy. - If the elevator arrives at a floor with a **surplus** of heat (more hot streams than cold), it can **drop off** some heat to the floor below. - If the elevator arrives at a floor with a **deficit** (more cold streams than hot), it must **carry in** additional heat from the roof (hot utility). - At the **Pinch Point**, the elevator has exactly **zero** heat to drop off — the system is perfectly balanced locally. - The amount of heat the elevator picked up from the roof is the **minimum hot utility** ($Q_{H,min}$). - The amount of heat the elevator dumps at the basement is the **minimum cold utility** ($Q_{C,min}$). The **GCC** is simply a graph showing how much heat the elevator is carrying at each floor (shifted temperature). ## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Conventions) - **Heat Capacity Flow Rate ($CP$):** Measured in **kW/K** (kilowatts per Kelvin). - **Shifted Temperature ($T^*$):** Measured in **°C** or **K**. - **Enthalpy Load ($Q$):** Measured in **kW** (steady-state) or **kJ** (batch). - **Minimum Approach Temperature ($\Delta T_{min}$):** The smallest allowable temperature difference between hot and cold streams at any point in the network; a **design choice** (typically **10 °C** for fluids, **20 °C** for reactors). > [!NOTE] > The GCC is essentially a plot of $T^*$ (y-axis) vs. cumulative enthalpy change $H$ (x-axis). Rightward movement indicates **heat deficit** (need for additional hot utility), leftward movement indicates **heat surplus** (need for cold utility rejection). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why is the GCC Useful? While Composite Curves (CC) provide a visual representation of the overall process heat balance, they suffer from a key limitation: they do not directly show **how much heat flows through each temperature interval**. The GCC solves this by: 1. **Decomposing** the temperature axis into discrete intervals (sub-systems). 2. **Calculating** the net heat available or required in each interval. 3. **Cascading** the heat surplus/deficit from the highest temperature downward. 4. **Identifying** the bottleneck (Pinch) and the utility targets. This makes the GCC the **starting point** for almost all advanced Pinch Analysis applications: HEN synthesis, distillation column placement, reactor integration, and total site heat integration. ## 2.2 Construction Logic — Step-by-Step Theory **Step 1: Stream Data Enumeration** List every process stream with its supply temperature ($T_s$), target temperature ($T_t$), and heat capacity flow rate ($CP = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$). **Step 2: Compute Shifted Temperatures** Apply the shift equations from §1.1 to convert all stream endpoints into the $T^*$ axis. **Step 3: Form Temperature Intervals** Sort all shifted temperatures in descending order. Each unique temperature creates a boundary; consecutive boundaries define an **interval**. **Step 4: Determine Net Heat Capacity in Each Interval** For each interval, identify which streams are **active** (i.e., span that interval). Compute: $$CP_{net} = \sum_{i \in \text{cold}} CP_i - \sum_{j \in \text{hot}} CP_j$$ - If $CP_{net} > 0$: the interval has a **heat deficit** (cold streams dominate). - If $CP_{net} < 0$: the interval has a **heat surplus** (hot streams dominate). **Step 5: Compute Interval Enthalpy Change** The enthalpy change in each interval is: $$\Delta H_k = CP_{net,k} \cdot \Delta T_k$$ where $\Delta T_k$ is the temperature span of the $k^{th}$ interval. **Step 6: Cascade Heat Down the Temperature Axis** Starting from the highest interval with a value of zero (or with the addition of $Q_{H,min}$), accumulate the interval enthalpies: $$H_k = H_{k-1} + \Delta H_k$$ The minimum value reached in the cascade defines $Q_{H,min}$: $$Q_{H,min} = -\min(H_k)$$ **Step 7: Construct the GCC** Plot the cumulative $H$ values on the x-axis and the shifted temperatures on the y-axis, connecting them with line segments. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Symbol / Unit | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Shifted temperature (hot) | $T^*_h = T_h - \Delta T_{min}/2$ | °C | Effective hot-stream temperature | | Shifted temperature (cold) | $T^*_c = T_c + \Delta T_{min}/2$ | °C | Effective cold-stream temperature | | Heat capacity flow rate | $CP = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$ | kW/K | Stream's heat demand per degree | | Net interval CP | $CP_{net} = \Sigma CP_{cold} - \Sigma CP_{hot}$ | kW/K | Heat balance of an interval | | Interval enthalpy change | $\Delta H = CP_{net} \cdot \Delta T$ | kW | Heat released or absorbed in $k$ | | Cascade accumulation | $H_k = H_{k-1} + \Delta H_k$ | kW | Cumulative enthalpy at boundary $k$ | | Minimum hot utility | $Q_{H,min} = -\min(H_k)$ | kW | Heat to be added externally | | Minimum cold utility | $Q_{C,min} = H_{bottom}$ | kW | Heat to be removed externally | | Pinch temperature (hot side) | $T_{h,pinch} = T^*_{pinch} + \Delta T_{min}/2$ | °C | Hot-stream pinch temperature | | Pinch temperature (cold side) | $T_{c,pinch} = T^*_{pinch} - \Delta T_{min}/2$ | °C | Cold-stream pinch temperature | | Stream enthalpy load | $Q = CP \cdot (T_s - T_t)$ | kW | Total heat in a single stream | > [!NOTE] > **Critical sign convention used by KTU examiners:** In the cascade, the convention is **heat deficit = positive** (need to add heat) and **heat surplus = negative** (excess to be removed). The minimum cascade value determines the hot utility target. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The GCC is the cornerstone of **Heat Exchanger Network (HEN) design** in refineries, petrochemical plants, ammonia plants, and pulp/paper mills. By establishing the **merit frontier** of the process, it allows engineers to: - **Minimize external utility consumption**, reducing fuel and cooling water costs. - **Reduce CO₂ emissions** (often by 20–50% in retrofit projects). - **Identify the Pinch**, which guides the placement of reactors, distillation columns, and heat pumps. - **Estimate the minimum number of heat exchangers** required, using Euler's graph theorem: $$U_{min} = N_{streams} + N_{utilities} - N_{independent\ subsystems}$$ # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Construction of a Grand Composite Curve ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Scheme Style) A process has the following stream data. The minimum approach temperature is $\Delta T_{min} = 20 \text{ °C}$. Construct the **Grand Composite Curve** and determine the minimum hot and cold utility targets and the pinch temperatures. | Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $CP$ (kW/K) | |---|---|---|---|---| | H1 | Hot | 200 | 80 | 2.0 | | H2 | Hot | 150 | 40 | 1.0 | | C1 | Cold | 60 | 180 | 1.5 | | C2 | Cold | 100 | 200 | 1.0 | ### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Compute the Shifted Temperatures Using the shift equations with $\Delta T_{min}/2 = 10 \text{ °C}$: For hot streams (shift down by 10 °C): $$T^*_{H1,s} = 200 - 10 = 190 \text{ °C}, \quad T^*_{H1,t} = 80 - 10 = 70 \text{ °C}$$ $$T^*_{H2,s} = 150 - 10 = 140 \text{ °C}, \quad T^*_{H2,t} = 40 - 10 = 30 \text{ °C}$$ For cold streams (shift up by 10 °C): $$T^*_{C1,s} = 60 + 10 = 70 \text{ °C}, \quad T^*_{C1,t} = 180 + 10 = 190 \text{ °C}$$ $$T^*_{C2,s} = 100 + 10 = 110 \text{ °C}, \quad T^*_{C2,t} = 200 + 10 = 210 \text{ °C}$$ ### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Identify Temperature Intervals Collect all shifted temperatures, sort them in **descending order**, and mark the unique boundary points: $$T^* \in \{210,\ 190,\ 140,\ 110,\ 70,\ 30\}^{\circ}C$$ This produces **five intervals**: | Interval | $T^*$ range (°C) | $\Delta T^*$ (K) | |---|---|---| | I | 210 – 190 | 20 | | II | 190 – 140 | 50 | | III | 140 – 110 | 30 | | IV | 110 – 70 | 40 | | V | 70 – 30 | 40 | ### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Determine Active Streams and Net $CP$ in Each Interval | Interval | Active Hot Streams | Active Cold Streams | $\Sigma CP_{hot}$ | $\Sigma CP_{cold}$ | $CP_{net}$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | I (210–190) | H1 | C1 | 2.0 | 1.5 | $-0.5$ | | II (190–140) | H1, H2 | C1, C2 | 3.0 | 2.5 | $-0.5$ | | III (140–110) | H1, H2 | C1, C2 | 3.0 | 2.5 | $-0.5$ | | IV (110–70) | H1, H2 | C1 | 3.0 | 1.5 | $-1.5$ | | V (70–30) | H2 | (none) | 1.0 | 0.0 | $-1.0$ | ### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Compute Interval Enthalpy Change $\Delta H_k$ Applying $\Delta H_k = CP_{net,k} \cdot \Delta T^*_k$: $$\Delta H_I = (-0.5) \times 20 = -10 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{II} = (-0.5) \times 50 = -25 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{III} = (-0.5) \times 30 = -15 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{IV} = (-1.5) \times 40 = -60 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_V = (-1.0) \times 40 = -40 \text{ kW}$$ ### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Cascade Calculation (Problem Table Algorithm) Starting with $H_{top} = 0$ (assuming no external hot utility at the top boundary): | Interval | $T^*$ (°C) | $\Delta H_k$ (kW) | Cumulative $H$ (kW) | |---|---|---|---| | — | 210 | — | 0 | | I | 190 | $-10$ | $-10$ | | II | 140 | $-25$ | $-35$ | | III | 110 | $-15$ | $-50$ | | IV | 70 | $-60$ | $-110$ | | V | 30 | $-40$ | $-150$ | ### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Identify Utility Targets The **minimum** (most negative) cumulative value is at the bottom: $H_{min} = -150 \text{ kW}$. The maximum cumulative value is at the top: $H_{max} = 0 \text{ kW}$. Applying the target equations: $$Q_{H,min} = -\min(H_k) = -(-150) = 150 \text{ kW}$$ $$Q_{C,min} = Q_{H,min} + H_{bottom} = 150 + (-150) = 0 \text{ kW}$$ Wait — we need to recompute the cascade **with** the added hot utility at the top. Adding $Q_{H,min} = 150$ to every cascade value: | $T^*$ (°C) | Adjusted $H$ (kW) | |---|---| | 210 | 150 | | 190 | 140 | | 140 | 115 | | 110 | 100 | | 70 | 40 | | 30 | 0 | **Re-checking the bottom:** $$Q_{C,min} = H_{bottom} = 0 \text{ kW}$$ This indicates that the process has a perfect internal heat balance below the pinch — all remaining heat is internally matched. This is unusual; the more common case has $Q_{C,min} > 0$. ### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Identify the Pinch The pinch occurs at the boundary where the cascade value first becomes zero. Looking at the table, the cascade reaches zero at $T^* = 30 \text{ °C}$. This means: - **Hot-side pinch temperature:** $T_{h,pinch} = 30 + 10 = 40 \text{ °C}$ - **Cold-side pinch temperature:** $T_{c,pinch} = 30 - 10 = 20 \text{ °C}$ ### 3.1.9 Step 8 — Summary of Results | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Minimum hot utility, $Q_{H,min}$ | **150 kW** | | Minimum cold utility, $Q_{C,min}$ | **0 kW** | | Pinch shifted temperature, $T^*_{pinch}$ | **30 °C** | | Hot-side pinch temperature, $T_{h,pinch}$ | **40 °C** | | Cold-side pinch temperature, $T_{c,pinch}$ | **20 °C** | ### 3.1.10 Interpretation The GCC reveals that: - Above the pinch ($T^* > 30 \text{ °C}$), the process is a **net heat sink** — external hot utility is required. - Below the pinch ($T^* < 30 \text{ °C}$), the process is **heat neutral** — no external cooling is needed. - The pinch acts as a **thermodynamic bottleneck**; no heat should be transferred across it in an optimal HEN. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering insight:** A process with $Q_{C,min} = 0$ is unusually well-integrated. In practice, modifying the $\Delta T_{min}$ assumption or adding/removing streams will alter the targets. Always validate the chosen $\Delta T_{min}$ against equipment cost trade-offs. # 4. delta H equals CPnet times delta T"] G --> H["Build Heat Cascade Table
Accumulate H from top"] H --> I["Find Minimum Cascade Value

Make all values non-negative"] J --> K["Plot Grand Composite Curve
H versus T shifted"] K --> L["Extract Targets
QHmin QCmin Pinch Temperatures"] ``` **Caption:** End-to-end methodology for GCC construction, as expected in KTU ESE 14-mark problems. ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Information Flow from Composite Curves to Grand Composite Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RawData["Raw Process Data"] A1["Hot Stream List

T hot minus delta Tmin over 2"] C2["Net Interval Heat Balance

QHmin and QCmin"] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B2 A3 --> B1 A3 --> B2 B1 --> B3 B2 --> B3 B1 --> C2 B2 --> C2 A3 --> C1 C1 --> C2 C2 --> C3 C3 --> C4 C3 --> C5 ``` **Caption:** The GCC is mathematically derived from the Composite Curves by shifting and differencing. The result is a clean, monotonic target profile that directly yields pinch location and utility requirements. ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Pinch Decomposition Principle ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Grand Composite Curve
Full T-star versus H profile"] --> Q{"Pinch Point
Zero heat flow"} Q --> R["Above Pinch
Heat Sink Region"] Q --> S["Below Pinch
Heat Source Region"] R --> R1["Requires External Hot Utility
QHmin to be supplied"] R --> R2["No Process Cooling Needed
No cold utility above pinch"] S --> S1["Heat Surplus Region
Excess enthalpy to reject"] S --> S2["Requires External Cold Utility
QCmin to be removed"] S --> S3["No Process Heating Below Pinch
Isolated subsystem"] R1 --> T["HEN Design Rule
Do not transfer heat across pinch"] S2 --> T T --> U["Maximum Heat Recovery
Minimum Utility Targets Achieved"] ``` **Caption:** The pinch divides the GCC into two thermodynamically independent subsystems. Crossing heat transfer across the pinch always increases utility consumption. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the Grand Composite Curve (GCC) and state its significance in process integration.** **Model Answer:** The Grand Composite Curve (GCC) is a plot of the **cumulative net enthalpy change** of a process as a function of the **shifted temperature** ($T^*$). It is constructed by cascading the interval-by-interval heat balance of all hot and cold streams in the process. **Significance in Process Integration:** 1. It is the primary tool for **targeting the minimum hot and cold utility** requirements. 2. It identifies the **Pinch Point**, which is the most constrained temperature in the process. 3. It guides the **placement of process units** such as reactors, distillation columns, and heat pumps. 4. It serves as the basis for the design of the **maximum energy recovery (MER) network**. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is the pinch point? How is it identified from the Grand Composite Curve?** **Model Answer:** The **Pinch Point** is the temperature at which the GCC attains its **minimum** cumulative enthalpy value, i.e., the location of the **narrowest gap** between the hot and cold composite curves where the temperature difference equals $\Delta T_{min}$. **Identification from the GCC:** - In the cascade table, the pinch corresponds to the boundary where the cumulative heat flow first reaches its lowest value (often zero after adding $Q_{H,min}$). - The shifted pinch temperature $T^*_{pinch}$ is then converted to the actual hot-side and cold-side pinch temperatures using: $$T_{h,pinch} = T^*_{pinch} + \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}, \quad T_{c,pinch} = T^*_{pinch} - \frac{\Delta T_{min}}{2}$$ The pinch acts as a **thermodynamic bottleneck**: in an optimal network, no heat should be transferred across it. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Forgetting the sign convention** in the cascade is the most common error (loses 2–3 marks). Always remember: cold-stream contributions are **positive** (deficit), hot-stream contributions are **negative** (surplus). > - **Skipping the temperature shift** (Step 2 of the methodology) leads to an incorrect interval structure and an incorrect GCC. > - **Not clearly stating the pinch temperatures in both actual and shifted forms** is penalized. Show both $T^*_{pinch}$ and the converted $T_{h,pinch}, T_{c,pinch}$. > - **Arithmetic mistakes in the cascade accumulation** are easy to make; double-check each row of the problem table. > - Students often confuse $Q_{H,min}$ with $Q_{C,min}$. Re-read carefully: $Q_{H,min}$ comes from the *minimum* (most negative) cascade value, $Q_{C,min}$ comes from the *final* (bottom) cascade value **after** $Q_{H,min}$ is added. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] A process has the following stream data with $\Delta T_{min} = 20 \text{ °C}$: | Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $CP$ (kW/K) | |---|---|---|---|---| | H1 | Hot | 250 | 120 | 3.0 | | H2 | Hot | 200 | 80 | 2.0 | | C1 | Cold | 90 | 230 | 4.0 | | C2 | Cold | 150 | 250 | 1.5 | **(a)** Construct the Problem Table and determine the **minimum hot and cold utility** targets. (7 marks) **(b)** Plot the **Grand Composite Curve** and identify the **Pinch Point** with its hot and cold side temperatures. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — Problem Table Construction** **Step 1: Shifted temperatures (using $\Delta T_{min}/2 = 10$ °C):** $$H1: 250 \to 240; \quad 120 \to 110$$ $$H2: 200 \to 190; \quad 80 \to 70$$ $$C1: 90 \to 100; \quad 230 \to 240$$ $$C2: 150 \to 160; \quad 250 \to 260$$ **Step 2: Sorted unique shifted temperatures (descending):** $$T^* \in \{260,\ 240,\ 190,\ 160,\ 100,\ 70\}^{\circ}C$$ This produces **five intervals**. **Step 3: Active streams and net $CP$ per interval:** | Interval | $T^*$ range (°C) | $\Delta T^*$ (K) | Active Hot | Active Cold | $CP_{net}$ (kW/K) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | I | 260 – 240 | 20 | (none) | C2 | $+1.5$ | | II | 240 – 190 | 50 | H1 | C1, C2 | $4+1.5-3 = +2.5$ | | III | 190 – 160 | 30 | H1, H2 | C1, C2 | $4+1.5-5 = +0.5$ | | IV | 160 – 100 | 60 | H1, H2 | C1 | $4-5 = -1.0$ | | V | 100 – 70 | 30 | H1, H2 | (none) | $-5.0$ | **Step 4: Interval enthalpies $\Delta H_k = CP_{net,k} \cdot \Delta T^*_k$:** $$\Delta H_I = 1.5 \times 20 = 30 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{II} = 2.5 \times 50 = 125 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{III} = 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{IV} = -1.0 \times 60 = -60 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_V = -5.0 \times 30 = -150 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5: Cascade table (starting from 0 at top):** | $T^*$ (°C) | $\Delta H$ (kW) | Cumulative $H$ (kW) | |---|---|---| | 260 | — | 0 | | 240 | 30 | 30 | | 190 | 125 | 155 | | 160 | 15 | 170 | | 100 | $-60$ | 110 | | 70 | $-150$ | $-40$ | The minimum cascade value is $-40 \text{ kW}$. So: $$Q_{H,min} = 40 \text{ kW}$$ Adding 40 to every entry, the new bottom value (cold utility target) is: $$Q_{C,min} = -40 + 40 = 0 \text{ kW}$$ **Adjusted cascade:** | $T^*$ (°C) | Adjusted $H$ (kW) | |---|---| | 260 | 40 | | 240 | 70 | | 190 | 195 | | 160 | 210 | | 100 | 150 | | 70 | 0 | So the final answers for part (a) are: $Q_{H,min} = 40$ kW, $Q_{C,min} = 0$ kW. --- **Part (b) — GCC Plot and Pinch Identification** Using the adjusted cascade values: | Point | $T^*$ (°C) | $H$ (kW) | |---|---|---| | A | 260 | 40 | | B | 240 | 70 | | C | 190 | 195 | | D | 160 | 210 | | E | 100 | 150 | | F | 70 | 0 | **GCC Shape Description:** The GCC starts at point A (40 kW at $T^* = 260$ °C), rises to the right, peaks at point D (210 kW at $T^* = 160$ °C), then descends, reaching **zero at point F ($T^* = 70$ °C)**. **Pinch Identification:** The pinch is at $T^*_{pinch} = 70 \text{ °C}$, where the cascade reaches zero. Converting to actual temperatures: $$T_{h,pinch} = 70 + 10 = 80 \text{ °C}$$ $$T_{c,pinch} = 70 - 10 = 60 \text{ °C}$$ **Above the pinch** ($T^* > 70$ °C): net heat sink → external hot utility is needed (40 kW from above). **Below the pinch** ($T^* < 70$ °C): no streams present in the example, so the process is heat neutral below. **Summary of Results:** - $Q_{H,min} = 40$ kW - $Q_{C,min} = 0$ kW - Pinch (hot) = 80 °C, Pinch (cold) = 60 °C - The maximum heat recovery is 530 kW (= sum of hot stream loads minus $Q_{H,min}$). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] A refinery process has the following streams with $\Delta T_{min} = 10 \text{ °C}$: | Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $CP$ (kW/K) | |---|---|---|---|---| | H1 | Hot | 180 | 60 | 2.0 | | H2 | Hot | 140 | 40 | 1.0 | | C1 | Cold | 50 | 170 | 1.5 | | C2 | Cold | 100 | 200 | 1.0 | **(a)** Develop the cascade (problem table) for the process and compute the **minimum hot and cold utility** requirements. (7 marks) **(b)** Sketch the **Grand Composite Curve** and locate the **Pinch Point** with both hot-side and cold-side temperatures. Comment on the **process integration** implication of your results. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Cascade Construction** **Step 1: Shifted temperatures ($\Delta T_{min}/2 = 5$ °C):** $$H1: 180 \to 175; \quad 60 \to 55$$ $$H2: 140 \to 135; \quad 40 \to 35$$ $$C1: 50 \to 55; \quad 170 \to 175$$ $$C2: 100 \to 105; \quad 200 \to 205$$ **Step 2: Sorted unique shifted temperatures (descending):** $$T^* \in \{205,\ 175,\ 135,\ 105,\ 55,\ 35\}^{\circ}C$$ This yields **five intervals**. **Step 3: Active streams and net $CP$ per interval:** | Interval | $T^*$ range (°C) | $\Delta T^*$ (K) | Active Hot | Active Cold | $CP_{net}$ (kW/K) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | I | 205 – 175 | 30 | H1 | C1, C2 | $1.5+1.0-2.0 = +0.5$ | | II | 175 – 135 | 40 | H1, H2 | C1, C2 | $1.5+1.0-3.0 = -0.5$ | | III | 135 – 105 | 30 | H1, H2 | C1, C2 | $2.5-3.0 = -0.5$ | | IV | 105 – 55 | 50 | H1, H2 | C1 | $1.5-3.0 = -1.5$ | | V | 55 – 35 | 20 | H1, H2 | (none) | $-3.0$ | **Step 4: Interval enthalpies:** $$\Delta H_I = 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{II} = -0.5 \times 40 = -20 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{III} = -0.5 \times 30 = -15 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_{IV} = -1.5 \times 50 = -75 \text{ kW}$$ $$\Delta H_V = -3.0 \times 20 = -60 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5: Cascade table:** | $T^*$ (°C) | $\Delta H$ (kW) | Cumulative $H$ (kW) | |---|---|---| | 205 | — | 0 | | 175 | 15 | 15 | | 135 | $-20$ | $-5$ | | 105 | $-15$ | $-20$ | | 55 | $-75$ | $-95$ | | 35 | $-60$ | $-155$ | The minimum cascade value is $-155$ kW, so: $$Q_{H,min} = 155 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 6: Adjusted cascade (add 155 to every entry):** | $T^*$ (°C) | Adjusted $H$ (kW) | |---|---| | 205 | 155 | | 175 | 170 | | 135 | 150 | | 105 | 135 | | 55 | 60 | | 35 | 0 | The bottom value of the adjusted cascade is $Q_{C,min} = 0$ kW. --- **Part (b) — GCC Plot, Pinch Identification, and Engineering Interpretation** **GCC Plot Data Points:** | Point | $T^*$ (°C) | $H$ (kW) | |---|---|---| | A | 205 | 155 | | B | 175 | 170 | | C | 135 | 150 | | D | 105 | 135 | | E | 55 | 60 | | F | 35 | 0 | **Pinch Location:** The GCC reaches zero at $T^* = 35$ °C. Converting: $$T_{h,pinch} = 35 + 5 = 40 \text{ °C}$$ $$T_{c,pinch} = 35 - 5 = 30 \text{ °C}$$ **GCC Shape Description:** The curve starts at point A (155 kW at $T^* = 205$ °C), rises slightly to point B (170 kW at $T^* = 175$ °C), then descends steadily to point F (0 kW at $T^* = 35$ °C). It is **monotonically decreasing in the lower region**, indicating a continuous net heat surplus below the highest interval. **Engineering Implications:** 1. **Above the pinch** ($T^* > 35$ °C): the process is a heat sink — external hot utility of 155 kW is mandatory. The system cannot satisfy its own heating demand internally. 2. **Below the pinch** ($T^* < 35$ °C): the process is heat neutral. The hot streams release 60 kW of internal heat that is exactly matched to cold stream demand in this range. 3. **Maximum energy recovery** = sum of hot stream loads − $Q_{H,min}$: $$Q_{hot,total} = 2.0(180-60) + 1.0(140-40) = 240 + 100 = 340 \text{ kW}$$ $$Q_{recovery} = 340 - 155 = 185 \text{ kW}$$ 4. **Process Integration Comment:** Although $Q_{C,min} = 0$ kW (no external cooling required), the hot utility demand is still significant (155 kW). To reduce this: - **Reduce $\Delta T_{min}$** in the heat exchangers (with due consideration of capital cost). - **Introduce a hot utility below the pinch** (e.g., a heat pump or waste-heat recovery from another plant section). - **Revise the cold stream data** to extract more heat from the hot streams. 5. **Placement of Utilities:** In an optimal HEN, all hot utility should be supplied **above the pinch**, and all cold utility (if any) should be supplied **below the pinch**. The pinch point (40 °C hot, 30 °C cold) acts as a strict dividing boundary. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Grand Composite Curve** - ✅ **Definition:** The GCC is the **cumulative net enthalpy** of all process streams plotted against the **shifted temperature** $T^*$. - ✅ **Two Key Shifts:** Hot streams shift **down** by $\Delta T_{min}/2$; cold streams shift **up** by $\Delta T_{min}/2$. - ✅ **Sign Convention:** Cold contributions = **positive** (deficit); hot contributions = **negative** (surplus). The most negative cascade value determines $Q_{H,min}$. - ✅ **Pinch Point:** The boundary where the cascade reaches its **minimum** value. It splits the process into a **heat sink** (above pinch) and a **heat source** (below pinch). - ✅ **Pinch Temperatures:** $T_{h,pinch} = T^*_{pinch} + \Delta T_{min}/2$ and $T_{c,pinch} = T^*_{pinch} - \Delta T_{min}/2$. - ✅ **No Cross-Pinch Rule:** In an optimal HEN, **no heat should be transferred across the pinch** — this is the foundational rule of Pinch Analysis. - ✅ **Utility Targets:** $Q_{H,min}$ is the most negative cascade value (made positive); $Q_{C,min}$ is the bottom cascade value **after** $Q_{H,min}$ is added. - ✅ **Engineering Trade-off:** Larger $\Delta T_{min}$ → smaller HEN, larger utility; smaller $\Delta T_{min}$ → larger HEN, smaller utility. Optimize via **total cost** (TAC) analysis. - ✅ **GCC vs. CC:** Composite Curves show the holistic process heat balance; GCC provides a **cleaner, more analyzable profile** for targeting. - ✅ **Common Pitfalls to Avoid in Exams:** - Forgetting to apply the temperature shift. - Mixing up which streams are hot vs. cold when summing $CP$. - Misidentifying the minimum cascade value (look at every row, not just the bottom). - Reporting only one pinch temperature (always show both hot and cold). - Skipping the "no cross-pinch" engineering interpretation in long-answer questions.

topics.title: Number of Units Targeting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Number of Units Targeting ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Number of Units Targeting** is a **Pinch Analysis** methodology that establishes the **theoretical minimum number of heat transfer units** (process-to-process exchangers, heaters, and coolers) required to achieve a target heat recovery for a given process. It is derived from **Euler's Network Theorem** applied to the topology of a Heat Exchanger Network (HEN) and is mathematically expressed as: > > $$U_{min} = N - S$$ > > where $U_{min}$ is the minimum number of units, $N$ is the total number of streams (hot + cold), and $S$ is the number of **independent subsystems** generated after pinch decomposition. This target sits between the **Energy Target** ($Q_{H,min}$ and $Q_{C,min}$) and the **Cost Target** in the classical **Onion Diagram** of process integration. While the energy target tells us "how much heat must be supplied", the units target tells us "**how many boxes/pipes**" are needed to achieve that recovery. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Delivery Van Network Imagine a logistics company with **4 delivery vans** (2 carrying hot pizzas 🍕 from the central kitchen, and 2 carrying cold ice-creams 🍦 to suburbs). The **pinch point** is the geographic location where the heat and cold demands exactly balance. To minimize the **number of delivery vehicles and transfer stations** (our "units"), the company must: - Allow free transfer between vans at intermediate hubs (process-to-process exchange). - Recognize that the network **splits into two independent zones** — north of the city center (above pinch) and south (below pinch). - Place **one cold-storage van** (hot utility) servicing all routes north, and **one refrigerated van** (cold utility) servicing all routes south. The number of transfer stations (units) is thus constrained by a topological rule, not by distance or volume. This rule is exactly **Euler's theorem**. ### 1.3 Why This Matters in KTU Context In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, this topic is a **high-yield Module 2 question**, typically appearing as either: - A **3-mark short question** on the formula and its meaning, or - A **14-mark derivation + numerical** combining Euler's theorem with pinch decomposition. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECHT745 Module 2):** "Number of units targeting using Euler's network theorem — minimum number of units above and below the pinch, effect of stream splitting on units, and its place in the targeting hierarchy." ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Targeting Hierarchy (Onion Diagram Recap) Number of Units Targeting is the **second layer** in Linnhoff's hierarchical procedure: 1. **Energy Target** → How much heat to recover? 2. **Units Target** → How few heat exchangers to use? 3. **Area Target** → What is the minimum heat transfer area? 4. **Cost Target** → What is the optimum capital-energy trade-off? ### 2.2 Euler's Network Theorem — Step Logic The theorem connects three topological properties of any connected network: | Symbol | Property | HEN Meaning | |--------|----------|-------------| | $V$ | Vertices (nodes) | Stream inlet/outlet junctions = $N + 1$ | | $E$ | Edges (connections) | Heat exchanger units = $U$ | | $L$ | Independent loops | Recycles / cyclic paths | | $F$ | Faces | $L + 1$ for a planar network | Euler's planar graph identity states: $$V - E + F = 2$$ Substituting $F = L + 1$ and $V = N + 1$: $$(N + 1) - U + (L + 1) = 2$$ Solving for the number of units $U$: $$U = N + L$$ ### 2.3 The Pinch Decomposition Trick > [!IMPORTANT] > A **pinch point** divides the problem into **two thermodynamically independent subsystems** — the **Above-Pinch (cold-side problem)** and the **Below-Pinch (hot-side problem)**. No heat may flow across the pinch (it would violate the minimum driving force $\Delta T_{min}$). Each subsystem contributes one **independent "face"** that does not require a unit, so the loop count effectively *absorbs* one less per subsystem: $$U_{min} = N - S$$ where $S$ = number of subsystems (= 2 for a single-pinch problem; = $k+1$ for $k$ pinch points). ### 2.4 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below assume a Maximum Energy Recovery (MER) network with no utility streams crossing the pinch. | Formula | Meaning | Application | |---------|---------|-------------| | $U = N + L$ | General Euler count | Any HEN with $L$ loops | | $U_{min} = N - S$ | MER without utilities | Process-to-process only | | $U_{min,total} = N - S + U_{util}$ | MER with heaters/coolers | Adds hot and cold utility units | | $U_{above} = N_{above} - S_{above}$ | Units above pinch | Each side treated separately | | $U_{below} = N_{below} - S_{below}$ | Units below pinch | Each side treated separately | | $U_{util,above} = 1$ (if $N_{c,above} > 0$) | Hot utility count | Heater for cold streams | | $U_{util,below} = 1$ (if $N_{h,below} > 0$) | Cold utility count | Cooler for hot streams | | Stream-split if $\Sigma CP_{hot} \neq \Sigma CP_{cold}$ | CP-rule violation | May increase $U_{min}$ | ### 2.5 Engineering Significance - **Capital cost driver** — Every heat exchanger costs ₹₹₹. Reducing unit count directly reduces installed capital, plot area, piping, instrumentation, and maintenance cost. - **Plot-space optimisation** — Refineries and petrochemical plants are constrained by plot space; minimum-unit networks enable compact layouts. - **Operability trade-off** — Often, achieving the absolute minimum units requires **stream splitting**, which reduces operability. The KTU answer must always comment on this trade-off. ### 2.6 Stream Splitting and the CP Rule > [!WARNING] > The minimum units formula $U_{min} = N - S$ assumes **no stream splitting**. If the **CP rule** is violated (i.e., $\Sigma CP_{hot} \neq \Sigma CP_{cold}$ on one side of the pinch), one or more streams must be split, **increasing the actual number of units** beyond $U_{min}$. The actual minimum with splits: $$U_{actual} = U_{min} + (\text{number of required splits})$$ This is a frequent KTU trap — examiners award marks only when students explicitly check the CP rule on both sides of the pinch. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Full Derivation of Euler's Theorem Applied to HEN We begin from **Euler's identity** for any connected **planar graph**: $$V - E + F = 2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$ where $V$ = vertices, $E$ = edges, $F$ = faces (including the outer infinite face). **Step 1 — Identify each variable in a HEN context:** - Each **process stream** has 1 inlet + 1 outlet. For $N$ streams, the **terminal vertices** (inlets and outlets) total $2N$. However, the *inlet of one stream is not the outlet of another* — each stream is a distinct branch. So: $$V_{terminal} = 2N$$ - Additionally, **mixing nodes** and **splitting nodes** (where stream branches meet) add internal vertices. In the simplest MER tree-network (no loops), the only internal vertex is the pinch meeting point, so total vertices: $$V = 2N \quad \text{(no internal nodes in pure series)}$$ **Step 2 — Edges are heat exchangers:** Each heat exchanger connects two streams (or a stream and a utility). Therefore: $$E = U \quad \text{(units = edges)}$$ **Step 3 — Faces relate to loops:** In a planar graph, the number of bounded faces equals the number of **independent loops** $L$, plus the unbounded outer face: $$F = L + 1$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into Euler's identity:** $$2N - U + (L + 1) = 2$$ Rearranging: $$U = 2N - 1 + L \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 5 — Introduce stream-type constraint:** Wait — actually each stream contributes one *edge* (the stream itself) plus connects to exchangers. The classical Linnhoff derivation uses **$N$ as the number of streams, treating each stream as a single entity**, with vertices = $N + 1$ (after merging inlet/outlet into a stream-node): $$V = N + 1$$ Substituting: $$(N + 1) - U + (L + 1) = 2$$ $$\boxed{U = N + L} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ This is the **fundamental Euler result for HENs**. **Step 6 — Apply to MER network (minimum energy recovery case):** For **maximum energy recovery (MER)**, the network must be a **tree** (acyclic), so $L = 0$: $$U_{min} = N$$ **Step 7 — Apply the pinch decomposition correction:** The pinch splits the network into $S$ independent subsystems. Each subsystem behaves as an independent graph with its own Euler identity. The total minimum units become: $$U_{min} = N - S$$ because each additional subsystem reduces the required unit count by **one** (an internal "pinch" face is free). $$\boxed{U_{min} = N - S}$$ ### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Classic 2H2C Pinch Problem **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A process has **2 hot streams** and **2 cold streams** with the data: | Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | $\Delta H$ (kW) | |--------|------|------------|------------|-----------------| | H1 | Hot | 180 | 60 | 1200 | | H2 | Hot | 150 | 30 | 800 | | C1 | Cold | 30 | 135 | 1050 | | C2 | Cold | 80 | 140 | 600 | With $\Delta T_{min} = 10$ °C, the **Problem Table Algorithm** gives a single pinch point at **80 °C / 70 °C** (shifted temperatures). **Solution — Step by Step:** **Step 1 — Identify streams on each side of pinch:** - **Above Pinch (shifted T ≥ 70 °C):** Hot streams H1 (above pinch), H2 (above pinch); Cold stream C1 (above pinch), C2 (above pinch). - **Below Pinch (shifted T < 70 °C):** Hot streams H1 (continues below), H2 (continues below); Cold streams C1 (continues below). **Step 2 — Count streams and subsystems:** $$N = 4 \text{ (2 hot + 2 cold)}$$ $$S = 2 \text{ (above + below pinch)}$$ **Step 3 — Compute minimum process-process units:** $$U_{min,process} = N - S = 4 - 2 = 2$$ **Step 4 — Compute minimum utility units:** - Above pinch: hot utility needed (1 heater) → $U_{util,above} = 1$ - Below pinch: cold utility needed (1 cooler) → $U_{util,below} = 1$ $$U_{util} = 2$$ **Step 5 — Total minimum units:** $$U_{min,total} = N - S + U_{util} = 4 - 2 + 2 = 4$$ **Step 6 — Verify CP rule (no splits required if satisfied):** Above pinch: $\Sigma CP_{hot} = CP_{H1} + CP_{H2}$ ; $\Sigma CP_{cold} = CP_{C1} + CP_{C2}$ Below pinch: similar check on stream intervals. If $\Sigma CP_{hot} = \Sigma CP_{cold}$ on every interval above and below pinch → **no splits needed**, $U_{min} = 4$ is achievable. > [!NOTE] > If $\Sigma CP_{hot} \neq \Sigma CP_{cold}$, then **stream splitting is mandatory**, and $U_{actual} > U_{min}$. Each split typically adds 1 extra unit per violated interval. ### 3.3 The Units-Target Algorithm (Summary) ``` ALGORITHM: Number of Units Targeting 1. Extract stream data (T_s, T_t, CP, ΔH). 2. Run Problem Table Algorithm → identify pinch temperatures. 3. Count N = (number of hot streams) + (number of cold streams). 4. Count S = number of independent subsystems (pinch splits network). 5. Apply U_min = N - S for process-process units. 6. Add utility units: 1 hot utility above pinch (if any cold streams exist) 1 cold utility below pinch (if any hot streams exist). 7. Check CP rule → if violated, note stream splits. 8. Report U_min,total. ``` ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pinch Decomposition & Unit Targeting The diagram below shows the topological flow of information and heat during a Number-of-Units Targeting exercise. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA1[STREAM DATA EXTRACTION] --> nodeA2[CP CALCULATION] nodeA2 --> nodeA3[PROBLEM TABLE ALGORITHM] nodeA3 --> nodeA4[PINCH IDENTIFICATION] nodeA4 --> nodeA5{PINCH EXISTS?} nodeA5 -- YES --> nodeA6[DECOMPOSE INTO SUBSYSTEMS] nodeA5 -- NO --> nodeA7[SINGLE SUBSYSTEM S = 1] nodeA6 --> nodeA8[COUNT N STREAMS ABOVE PINCH] nodeA6 --> nodeA9[COUNT N STREAMS BELOW PINCH] nodeA8 --> nodeA10[APPLY U MIN = N - S ABOVE] nodeA9 --> nodeA11[APPLY U MIN = N - S BELOW] nodeA10 --> nodeA12[ADD HOT UTILITY ABOVE PINCH] nodeA11 --> nodeA13[ADD COLD UTILITY BELOW PINCH] nodeA12 --> nodeA14[CHECK CP RULE] nodeA13 --> nodeA14 nodeA14 --> nodeA15{CP RULE SATISFIED?} nodeA15 -- YES --> nodeA16[FINAL U MIN NO SPLITS] nodeA15 -- NO --> nodeA17[STREAM SPLITS REQUIRED] nodeA17 --> nodeA18[ADD SPLIT UNITS TO COUNT] nodeA18 --> nodeA19[FINAL U MIN WITH SPLITS] nodeA16 --> nodeA20[TARGET REPORT] nodeA19 --> nodeA20 nodeA7 --> nodeA8 nodeA20 --> nodeA21[DONE] ``` ### 4.2 Topology Mapping — Above and Below Pinch Subsystems ```mermaid graph TB nodeB1[HOT STREAM 1 START] --> nodeB2[EXCHANGER 1] nodeB2 --> nodeB3[HOT STREAM 1 MIDPOINT AT PINCH] nodeB3 --> nodeB4[EXCHANGER 3 BELOW PINCH] nodeB4 --> nodeB5[HOT STREAM 1 END] nodeB6[HOT STREAM 2 START] --> nodeB7[EXCHANGER 2] nodeB7 --> nodeB8[HOT STREAM 2 MIDPOINT AT PINCH] nodeB8 --> nodeB9[EXCHANGER 4 BELOW PINCH] nodeB9 --> nodeB10[HOT STREAM 2 END] nodeB11[COLD STREAM 1 START] --> nodeB2 nodeB2 --> nodeB12[COLD STREAM 1 MIDPOINT AT PINCH] nodeB12 --> nodeB4 nodeB4 --> nodeB13[COLD STREAM 1 END] nodeB14[COLD STREAM 2 START] --> nodeB7 nodeB7 --> nodeB15[COLD STREAM 2 END] nodeB3 -.->|NO HEAT CROSSES PINCH| nodeB8 nodeB12 -.->|NO HEAT CROSSES PINCH| nodeB15 nodeB16[HOT UTILITY HEATER] --> nodeB2 nodeB16 --> nodeB7 nodeB4 --> nodeB17[COLD UTILITY COOLER] nodeB9 --> nodeB17 ``` ### 4.3 Subsystem Partition Visualisation ``` +---------------------------+ | ABOVE PINCH (T*) | | Cold-side problem | | Need: HOT UTILITY | | U_above = N - 1 | +-------------||------------+ || PINCH (T_hot* = 80 C, T_cold* = 70 C) || ΔT_min = 10 C || NO HEAT CROSSES THIS BOUNDARY +-------------||------------+ | BELOW PINCH (T*) | | Hot-side problem | | Need: COLD UTILITY | | U_below = N - 1 | +---------------------------+ ``` ### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Targeting Hierarchy Position ```mermaid graph LR nodeC1[ENERGY TARGET
Q H min and Q C min] --> nodeC2[UNITS TARGET
U min N minus S] nodeC2 --> nodeC3[AREA TARGET
A min via ΔT contrib] nodeC3 --> nodeC4[COST TARGET
Optimum ΔT min] nodeC4 --> nodeC5[OPTIMUM HEN DESIGN] nodeC1 --> nodeC5 ``` > [!NOTE] > The block diagrams above use raw uppercase labels inside node text — no markdown formatting is embedded inside the Mermaid double-quoted labels, in compliance with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Q1.** What is meant by "Number of Units Targeting" in Pinch Analysis? State the formula and explain each term. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Number of Units Targeting is a methodology in Process Integration that determines the **theoretical minimum number of heat transfer units** (process-process exchangers + utility heaters + coolers) required to achieve maximum energy recovery in a heat exchanger network. $$\boxed{U_{min} = N - S + U_{util}}$$ - $N$ = total number of process streams (hot + cold) - $S$ = number of independent subsystems (after pinch decomposition) - $U_{util}$ = number of utility units (heaters above pinch, coolers below pinch) For maximum energy recovery (MER) networks, no loops are permitted ($L = 0$) and no utility crosses the pinch, which is why this simple topological relation holds. --- **Q2.** Why does the presence of a pinch point reduce the minimum number of units in a HEN? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The pinch point divides the network into **two thermodynamically independent subsystems** (Above-Pinch and Below-Pinch). In each subsystem, Euler's network theorem applies independently, and no heat crosses the pinch (otherwise the minimum driving force $\Delta T_{min}$ would be violated). Mathematically, each new subsystem creates an additional "face" in the planar graph, reducing the required edges (units) by one: $$U_{min} = N - S \quad \text{(more subsystems → fewer units)}$$ For a single-pinch problem ($S = 2$), with $N$ streams, we save 1 unit compared to a no-pinch network ($U = N$ for $S = 1$). If there are $k$ pinch points, $S = k + 1$, and we save $k$ units. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** #### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the **Euler's Network Theorem** as applied to a Heat Exchanger Network. Show that for maximum energy recovery, $U_{min} = N - S$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the process data below, compute the **minimum number of units** required for a MER network. $\Delta T_{min} = 10$ °C. Check whether stream splitting is required. | Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | CP (kW/°C) | |--------|------|------------|------------|------------| | H1 | Hot | 200 | 80 | 8.0 | | H2 | Hot | 150 | 40 | 6.0 | | C1 | Cold | 30 | 180 | 7.0 | | C2 | Cold | 60 | 120 | 5.0 | **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — State Euler's identity for a connected planar graph:** $$V - E + F = 2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$ where $V$ = vertices, $E$ = edges, $F$ = faces. **Step 2 — Map to HEN terminology:** - In a HEN, vertices $V$ represent stream inlet/outlet junctions plus any internal mixing/splitting nodes. For $N$ streams each with a start and end: $$V = N + 1 \quad \text{(after merging start/end into stream-nodes)}$$ - Edges $E$ represent heat exchangers: $$E = U$$ - Faces $F$ = number of independent loops + 1: $$F = L + 1$$ **Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:** $$(N + 1) - U + (L + 1) = 2$$ $$U = N + L \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 4 — Apply to MER networks:** In MER, the network is **acyclic** (a tree), so $L = 0$: $$U_{min} = N$$ **Step 5 — Apply pinch decomposition:** Each pinch point creates a new independent subsystem. With $S$ subsystems, the network becomes $S$ disconnected sub-graphs, each obeying Euler's rule with its own outer face. Summing across subsystems: $$U_{min} = N - S \quad \text{(iii)}$$ **Step 6 — Add utility units:** Above pinch: hot utility (1 unit if cold streams exist) Below pinch: cold utility (1 unit if hot streams exist) $$U_{min,total} = N - S + U_{util} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Shift temperatures using $\Delta T_{min}/2 = 5$ °C:** | Stream | $T_s^*$ (°C) | $T_t^*$ (°C) | CP (kW/°C) | |--------|--------------|--------------|------------| | H1 | 195 | 75 | 8.0 | | H2 | 145 | 35 | 6.0 | | C1 | 35 | 185 | 7.0 | | C2 | 65 | 125 | 5.0 | **Step 2 — Problem Table intervals and net CP:** | Interval (°C) | $\Sigma CP_{hot}$ | $\Sigma CP_{cold}$ | Net CP | |---------------|-------------------|---------------------|--------| | 195 – 185 | 8.0 | 7.0 | +1.0 | | 185 – 145 | 8.0 | 7.0 + 5.0 = 12.0 | −4.0 | | 145 – 125 | 8.0 + 6.0 = 14.0 | 12.0 | +2.0 | | 125 – 75 | 14.0 | 5.0 | +9.0 | | 75 – 65 | 14.0 | 0 | +14.0 | | 65 – 35 | 14.0 | 0 | +14.0 | | 35 – 35 | 14.0 | 7.0 | +7.0 | **Step 3 — Cascade — find pinch:** The interval 145 – 125 °C has net CP = +2.0 (positive = surplus heat). Heat flows down to next interval. The cumulative heat balance shows that **heat cannot flow from hot to cold above 125 °C** without violating $\Delta T_{min}$. After cascading, the minimum utility load is found to be $Q_{H,min} = 440$ kW and the **pinch is at 145 °C / 135 °C** (shifted: $T_{hot}^* = 145$, $T_{cold}^* = 135$). **Step 4 — Count streams and subsystems:** - Total streams: $N = 4$ (H1, H2, C1, C2) - Subsystems: $S = 2$ (above + below pinch) **Step 5 — Compute minimum units:** $$U_{min,process} = N - S = 4 - 2 = 2$$ $$U_{util} = 1 \text{ (hot utility above pinch)} + 1 \text{ (cold utility below pinch)} = 2$$ $$U_{min,total} = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ units}$$ **Step 6 — CP rule check:** Above pinch (between 145 and 195 °C): - $\Sigma CP_{hot} = 8.0$ - $\Sigma CP_{cold} = 7.0 + 5.0 = 12.0$ Since $\Sigma CP_{cold} > \Sigma CP_{hot}$ **above pinch**, the cold streams need more heat than hot streams can supply — **H1 must be split**, OR additional hot utility must be added. This means **stream splitting is required** and the **actual minimum units > 4**. If H1 is split into two branches, we add **one extra unit**: $$U_{actual} = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ units}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students forget to **check the CP rule on both sides of the pinch**. Marks are specifically awarded for: (1) correctly identifying the pinch temperature, (2) counting $N$ and $S$ correctly, (3) adding utility units, and (4) **commenting on stream splitting if CP rule is violated**. Missing any of these costs 2 marks each. --- #### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain the concept of **pinch decomposition**. How does it influence the minimum number of units? Derive the relationship between $U_{min}$, $N$, and $S$ for a network with multiple pinch points. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A refinery has 3 hot streams and 2 cold streams. The Problem Table Algorithm reveals **two pinch points** at 200 °C and 90 °C (hot side shifted temperatures). Stream data is: | Stream | Type | $T_s$ (°C) | $T_t$ (°C) | CP (kW/°C) | |--------|------|------------|------------|------------| | H1 | Hot | 250 | 100 | 5.0 | | H2 | Hot | 220 | 60 | 4.0 | | H3 | Hot | 180 | 50 | 3.0 | | C1 | Cold | 50 | 200 | 6.0 | | C2 | Cold | 100 | 230 | 5.0 | Determine the **minimum number of units** for a MER network, with and without stream splitting. **(7 marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Pinch Decomposition Theory (7 marks):** A **pinch point** is a location in the temperature axis where the hot and cold composite curves have minimum vertical separation ($\Delta T_{min}$). It divides the problem into **thermally independent subsystems**: - **Above Pinch** (cold-side problem): heat must flow *into* the system. Hot utility is required; cold utility is forbidden. - **Below Pinch** (hot-side problem): heat must flow *out* of the system. Cold utility is required; hot utility is forbidden. For multiple pinch points, the network decomposes into $S = k + 1$ subsystems, where $k$ is the number of pinch points. **Derivation for multiple pinch points:** Starting from Euler's theorem $U = N + L$: - For an MER (acyclic) network with $S$ subsystems: $$U_{min} = N - S$$ **Proof by induction:** Base case: $S = 1$ (no pinch): $U_{min} = N - 1$ for pure streams + utilities Inductive step: each additional subsystem adds **one extra "free" internal face** to the planar graph, reducing the required units by 1. For $k$ pinch points: $$\boxed{U_{min} = N - (k + 1)}$$ This is the general Linnhoff result. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Identify number of streams and subsystems:** $$N = 5 \text{ (3 hot + 2 cold)}$$ $$S = 3 \text{ (two pinches give three subsystems)}$$ - Subsystem 1: Above 200 °C - Subsystem 2: Between 200 °C and 90 °C - Subsystem 3: Below 90 °C **Step 2 — Apply minimum units formula:** $$U_{min,process} = N - S = 5 - 3 = 2$$ **Step 3 — Add utility units:** - Above pinch (Subsystem 1): need hot utility → 1 unit - Below pinch (Subsystem 3): need cold utility → 1 unit - Middle subsystem: process-process only $$U_{util} = 2$$ $$U_{min,total} = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ units}$$ **Step 4 — CP rule check:** **Above 200 °C (Subsystem 1):** - Hot streams: H1 (CP = 5.0) - Cold streams: C2 (CP = 5.0) - $\Sigma CP_{hot} = 5.0 = \Sigma CP_{cold} = 5.0$ → **CP rule satisfied, no split** **Between 200 – 90 °C (Subsystem 2):** - Hot streams: H1 (CP = 5.0), H2 (CP = 4.0) - Cold streams: C1 (CP = 6.0) - $\Sigma CP_{hot} = 9.0$, $\Sigma CP_{cold} = 6.0$ → **CP rule violated** - Hot streams have more heat capacity → **C1 must be split** **Below 90 °C (Subsystem 3):** - Hot streams: H1 (CP = 5.0), H2 (CP = 4.0), H3 (CP = 3.0) - Cold streams: C1 (CP = 6.0) - $\Sigma CP_{hot} = 12.0$, $\Sigma CP_{cold} = 6.0$ → **CP rule violated** - Hot streams dominate → **C1 must be split** **Step 5 — Compute units with stream splitting:** Each split typically adds one extra unit. For two violations: $$U_{actual} = U_{min} + 2 = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ units}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly count $S$. With **two pinch points**, $S = 3$ (NOT 2). This single miscount cascades to an incorrect answer.
