Always confirm: $S = (\text{number of pinch points}) + 1$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - 🔹 **Definition**: Number of Units Targeting establishes the **theoretical lower bound** on heat exchangers (units) needed for a heat exchanger network at maximum energy recovery. - 🔹 **Core formula**: $U_{min} = N - S$ (process-process only). - 🔹 **Total with utilities**: $U_{min,total} = N - S + U_{util}$. - 🔹 **Euler's theorem**: $U = N + L$; for MER, $L = 0$ so $U = N$. - 🔹 **Pinch splits** the network into $S$ subsystems; each pinch adds 1 to $S$. - 🔹 **Multiple pinches**: $S = k + 1$ where $k$ = number of pinch points. - 🔹 **Above pinch**: hot utility ONLY (1 heater if any cold streams). - 🔹 **Below pinch**: cold utility ONLY (1 cooler if any hot streams). - 🔹 **At pinch**: process-to-process exchange. - 🔹 **CP rule**: above pinch, if $\Sigma CP_{hot} < \Sigma CP_{cold}$ → split hot stream; below pinch, if $\Sigma CP_{hot} > \Sigma CP_{cold}$ → split cold stream. - 🔹 **Stream splitting increases units**: $U_{actual} = U_{min} + \text{(splits)}$. - 🔹 **Onion Diagram position**: 2nd layer — units → area → cost. - 🔹 **Common KTU traps**: - Forgetting to check CP rule on BOTH sides of pinch. - Miscounting $S$ when multiple pinches exist. - Adding utility units at the pinch instead of above/below. - Not specifying MER assumption. - Ignoring stream splitting in numericals. > [!NOTE] > **One-line memory trick:** *"U = N − S + Util"* — say it like a formula chant. Number of Units = total streams − subsystems + utility count.

topics.title: Number of Shells Targeting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Number of Shells Targeting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Number of Shells Targeting** is a post-pinch design procedure used in Heat Exchanger Network (HEN) synthesis to estimate the **minimum number of shell-and-tube heat exchangers (in series)** required to honour a specified network ΔT_min. Unlike the idealised counter-current heat exchanger assumed in Pinch Analysis, real **TEMA-type shell-and-tube exchangers** (1 shell pass – 2 tube passes, i.e., 1-2 configuration) cannot fully achieve counter-current flow. Consequently, the *effective* minimum temperature approach that can be realised inside a single physical shell is often **larger than the network ΔT_min**. Whenever a stream's temperature change across an exchanger exceeds this practical shell-side ΔT approach, the duty must be split across **two or more identical shells in series** (a 2-4, 3-6, … arrangement). The targeting method calculates this minimum shell count *before* the network is built, avoiding the costly discovery of infeasible units at the detailed design stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Number of Shells Targeting falls under Module 2 – *Setting Energy Targets*. It directly bridges the gap between the *idealised* counter-current ΔT_min assumed at the pinch and the *realistic* TEMA shell-and-tube hardware that will actually be purchased and installed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long, straight tunnel (the **shell**) and a set of U-shaped pipes (the **tubes**) running through it. The hot fluid fills the shell; the cold fluid wiggles back and forth inside the tubes. Because the tube fluid reverses direction, it cannot flow in *perfect* opposition to the shell fluid everywhere — at one end, both fluids flow in the **same direction (co-current)**. This co-current region prevents the cold outlet from ever becoming hotter than the hot outlet, i.e., it **forbids a temperature cross inside a single physical shell**.

> **Real-world analogy — the staircase rule:** Think of carrying water up a staircase. Each step can only lift the water by a fixed height (the ΔT approach). If the total climb (the stream's ΔT) is bigger than one step, you need *more than one step* (more than one shell in series). The number of "steps" you need is **ceiling(total climb ÷ step height)**.

If the cold stream in a 1-2 exchanger has to be heated from 80 °C to 200 °C while the hot stream cools from 250 °C to 100 °C, a single shell will *not* do — you must stack two or more shells to preserve a feasible driving force everywhere.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **ΔT_min** | Network minimum temperature approach (set at the pinch) | **10 °C** (default in KTU problems) |
| **ΔT_shell** | Effective minimum approach inside one TEMA shell | ≈ **ΔT_min** (assuming 1-2 TEMA shell) |
| **N_s** | Number of shells in series for an exchanger | integer ≥ 1 |
| **T_H,in / T_H,out** | Hot stream inlet / outlet temperature | °C |
| **T_C,in / T_C,out** | Cold stream inlet / outlet temperature | °C |
| **CP** | Heat capacity flow rate (m·C_p) | kW/°C |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Single Shell Cannot Always Honour ΔT_min

In a 1-2 TEMA exchanger the *shell-side* temperature profile is non-monotonic because the tube-side fluid changes direction. This creates a **temperature cross** (cold outlet hotter than hot outlet) which is *thermodynamically infeasible*. To avoid the cross, the **effective minimum approach inside one shell** is bounded by the larger of the two stream ΔT changes:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{shell,min}} \;=\; \max\!\left(\Delta T_{H,\text{stream}},\;\Delta T_{C,\text{stream}}\right)
$$

If either stream's temperature change in a single unit **exceeds** ΔT_min, the duty **must** be split across multiple shells in series, each handling a portion of the temperature change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The pinch ΔT_min is a *network* target. Inside one physical shell, the realised approach is often *larger*. Shells-in-series is the engineering device used to *recover* the lost driving force.

## 2.2 The Number-of-Shells Targeting Algorithm (KTU Board Method)

**Step 1 — Complete the Pinch Design.** Obtain a HEN satisfying ΔT_min for every match.

**Step 2 — Identify Each Exchanger Unit.** For every match, record the hot stream inlet/outlet and cold stream inlet/outlet temperatures.

**Step 3 — Compute Stream ΔT Inside Each Exchanger.**

$$
\Delta T_H \;=\; \lvert T_{H,\text{in}} - T_{H,\text{out}} \rvert
$$
$$
\Delta T_C \;=\; \lvert T_{C,\text{out}} - T_{C,\text{in}} \rvert
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Shell Rule.** The number of shells in series required for the *i-th* exchanger is

$$
N_{s,i} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\max(\Delta T_H,\;\Delta T_C)}{\Delta T_{\text{min}}} \right\rceil
$$

where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the **ceiling (smallest integer ≥ value)**.

**Step 5 — Sum the Shells.** The total minimum number of shells for the network is

$$
\boxed{\;N_{s,\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{units}}} N_{s,i}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative "Interval" Formulation:** When the network is not yet fixed, you can target shells from the **Problem Table Algorithm (PTA) intervals**. For each interval *k*, compute $N_{s,k} = \lceil \Delta T_{\text{stream},k} / \Delta T_{\text{min}} \rceil$ for each stream, and the network shell target is the **maximum** $N_{s,k}$ summed over the units the interval is partitioned into. The unit-by-unit method above is the one most often tested in KTU ESE.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Purpose / When Used | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = CP \cdot \Delta T$ | Energy balance on a stream | kW |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{H} = \lvert T_{H,\text{in}} - T_{H,\text{out}} \rvert$ | Hot stream temperature change in a unit | °C |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{C} = \lvert T_{C,\text{out}} - T_{C,\text{in}} \rvert$ | Cold stream temperature change in a unit | °C |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{\text{shell}} = \max(\Delta T_{H},\Delta T_{C})$ | Effective min approach inside one shell | °C |
| 5 | $N_{s,i} = \lceil \Delta T_{\text{shell}} / \Delta T_{\text{min}} \rceil$ | Shells in series for exchanger *i* | integer |
| 6 | $N_{s,\text{total}} = \sum N_{s,i}$ | Total shells in the HEN | integer |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{\text{LM}} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | LMTD for a single shell check (LMTD correction factor *F* used for multi-shell) | °C |
| 8 | $F = f(P,\;R)$ with $P = \dfrac{T_{C,\text{out}} - T_{C,\text{in}}}{T_{H,\text{in}} - T_{C,\text{in}}}$, $R = \dfrac{T_{H,\text{in}} - T_{H,\text{out}}}{T_{C,\text{out}} - T_{C,\text{in}}}$ | TEMA correction factor for 1-2 exchangers; if $F < 0.75$ ⇒ multiple shells | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Sign Convention:** Temperatures in **°C**, heat duties in **kW**, and the ceiling function must be applied **after** dividing, not before. Always show $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ explicitly in KTU answer scripts — examiners love it.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Capital Cost Targeting:** Each shell ≈ **$50 000–$500 000** depending on size, material and pressure rating. Shell targeting prevents *over-design* and gives realistic CAPEX estimates before detailed mechanical design.
- **Plot-Space & Layout:** Every extra shell is a separate pressure vessel, with foundations, nozzles and pipe racks. Knowing the count early drives plot layout decisions.
- **Operability & Maintenance:** Shells-in-series facilitate cleaning, isolation, and turn-down — important for refinery and petrochemical plants where on-stream time is revenue.
- **Driver for Network Resynthesis:** A unit demanding 4 shells is a strong signal that the HEN itself should be re-evolved (e.g., stream splitting, re-piping) to lower the count.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Single Exchanger (KTU Textbook Style)

**Problem:** A pinch-designed HEN has a single exchanger where a hot stream cools from **250 °C → 100 °C** and a cold stream heats from **80 °C → 200 °C**. Given **ΔT_min = 10 °C**, find the minimum number of shells required.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Extract stream ΔTs from the given temperatures.**

$$
\Delta T_H \;=\; \lvert 250 - 100 \rvert \;=\; 150 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$
$$
\Delta T_C \;=\; \lvert 200 - 80 \rvert \;=\; 120 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective shell minimum approach.**

$$
\Delta T_{\text{shell}} \;=\; \max(\Delta T_H,\;\Delta T_C) \;=\; \max(150,\;120) \;=\; 150 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the shell rule.**

$$
N_s \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\Delta T_{\text{shell}}}{\Delta T_{\text{min}}} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{150}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 15.0 \rceil \;=\; 15
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** A single TEMA 1-2 exchanger cannot absorb a 150 °C change. You need **15 shells in series** to keep the per-shell approach ≥ 10 °C. (In practice this triggers network resynthesis!)

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Full Network (The Classic KTU ESE Question)

**Problem:** A HEN has been pinch-designed with **ΔT_min = 10 °C** and contains three exchanger units with the following data:

| Unit | T_H,in (°C) | T_H,out (°C) | T_C,in (°C) | T_C,out (°C) |
|------|------------|-------------|------------|-------------|
| E1   | 200        | 120         | 80         | 140         |
| E2   | 180        | 100         | 90         | 150         |
| E3   | 150        | 100         | 60         | 100         |

Find the **total minimum number of shells** for the network.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute ΔT_H and ΔT_C for each unit.**

**Unit E1:**

$$
\Delta T_{H,E1} \;=\; \lvert 200 - 120 \rvert \;=\; 80 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$
$$
\Delta T_{C,E1} \;=\; \lvert 140 - 80 \rvert \;=\; 60 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Unit E2:**

$$
\Delta T_{H,E2} \;=\; \lvert 180 - 100 \rvert \;=\; 80 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$
$$
\Delta T_{C,E2} \;=\; \lvert 150 - 90 \rvert \;=\; 60 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Unit E3:**

$$
\Delta T_{H,E3} \;=\; \lvert 150 - 100 \rvert \;=\; 50 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$
$$
\Delta T_{C,E3} \;=\; \lvert 100 - 60 \rvert \;=\; 40 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ceiling rule to each unit.**

**Unit E1:**

$$
N_{s,E1} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\max(80,\;60)}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{80}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 8 \rceil \;=\; 8 \;\text{shells}
$$

**Unit E2:**

$$
N_{s,E2} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\max(80,\;60)}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{80}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 8 \rceil \;=\; 8 \;\text{shells}
$$

**Unit E3:**

$$
N_{s,E3} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\max(50,\;40)}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{50}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 5 \rceil \;=\; 5 \;\text{shells}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum across the network.**

$$
N_{s,\text{total}} \;=\; N_{s,E1} + N_{s,E2} + N_{s,E3} \;=\; 8 + 8 + 5 \;=\; \mathbf{21 \;shells}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic

| Condition on $\Delta T_{\text{shell}}$ vs. $\Delta T_{\text{min}}$ | Number of Shells | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta T_{\text{shell}} \le \Delta T_{\text{min}}$ | **1** | Single 1-2 TEMA shell suffices |
| $\Delta T_{\text{min}} < \Delta T_{\text{shell}} \le 2 \Delta T_{\text{min}}$ | **2** (2-4 arrangement) | Two shells in series; verify $F \ge 0.75$ |
| $2\Delta T_{\text{min}} < \Delta T_{\text{shell}} \le 3 \Delta T_{\text{min}}$ | **3** (3-6 arrangement) | Three shells in series |
| $k \cdot \Delta T_{\text{min}} < \Delta T_{\text{shell}} \le (k+1) \Delta T_{\text{min}}$ | **k + 1** | $(k+1)$ shells in series |
| $\Delta T_{\text{shell}} \gg \Delta T_{\text{min}}$ (e.g. > 5×) | high | **Re-evolve the network** (stream split, re-piping) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** If a unit demands more than 3 shells in a KTU problem, the network itself is the problem — not the shell rule. Always comment on resynthesis in your answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart — Number of Shells Targeting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pinch-Designed HEN] --> B[Identify all exchanger units in network]
    B --> C{For each unit i}
    C --> D[Extract T_H,in T_H,out T_C,in T_C,out]
    D --> E[Compute deltaT_H and deltaT_C]
    E --> F[Compute deltaT_shell = max deltaT_H deltaT_C]
    F --> G[Compute Ns_i = ceiling of deltaT_shell divided by deltaT_min]
    G --> H{Is Ns_i greater than 3}
    H -- No --> I[Add Ns_i to running total]
    H -- Yes --> J[Flag for network resynthesis]
    I --> K{All units processed}
    J --> K
    K -- No --> C
    K -- Yes --> L[Report Ns_total and resynthesis list]
    L --> M[End]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multi-Shell Exchanger

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1[Match Unit E1]
        direction TB
        H1[Hot Stream In 200 C] --> S1A[Shell 1A 1-2 TEMA]
        S1A --> S1B[Shell 1B 1-2 TEMA]
        S1B --> S1C[Shell 1C 1-2 TEMA]
        S1C --> H1O[Hot Stream Out]
        C1[Cold Stream In 80 C] --> S1A
        S1A --> S1B
        S1B --> S1C
        S1C --> C1O[Cold Stream Out 140 C]
    end
    subgraph M2[Match Unit E2]
        direction TB
        H2[Hot Stream In 180 C] --> S2A[Shell 2A 1-2 TEMA]
        S2A --> S2B[Shell 2B 1-2 TEMA]
        S2B --> H2O[Hot Stream Out]
        C2[Cold Stream In 90 C] --> S2A
        S2A --> S2B
        S2B --> C2O[Cold Stream Out 150 C]
    end
    M1 --> M2
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Shell-Stacking Diagram (Text Schematic)

The schematic below shows how a single "logical" pinch exchanger is implemented as a **train of physical shells in series**, each handling a ΔT_min-sized slice of the total temperature change:

```
LOGICAL EXCHANGER (1 unit, ΔT_min = 10 °C)
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Hot: 200 °C ─────────────────► 120 °C      │  ΔT_H = 80 °C
│   Cold:  80 °C ─────────────────► 140 °C      │  ΔT_C = 60 °C
│   Effective min approach inside one shell = 10 °C
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                    │
                    ▼  Splits into 8 physical shells in series
┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐
│  S1  │→│  S2  │→│  S3  │→│  S4  │→│  S5  │→│  S6  │→│  S7  │→│  S8  │
│ΔT=10 │ │ΔT=10 │ │ΔT=10 │ │ΔT=10 │ │ΔT=10 │ │ΔT=10 │ │ΔT=10 │ │ΔT=10 │
└──────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘ └──────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each physical shell "S_k" has its own bundle, nozzles and pressure rating. The hot and cold streams are piped in series through the train; inter-stage connections are the only extra piping cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Number of Shells" as used in the targeting of heat exchanger networks. Why is it necessary to target the number of shells before detailed design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Number of Shells** in a heat exchanger network is the *minimum number of shell-and-tube heat exchangers (in series)* required to realise a pinch-designed match while honouring the network minimum temperature approach ΔT_min. (1 mark)

Each physical TEMA 1-2 shell has an effective minimum approach that may exceed ΔT_min. If a stream's temperature change across a logical match is larger than ΔT_min, the duty must be split across **multiple identical shells in series**. (1 mark)

Targeting the shell count *before* detailed design is essential because it provides a realistic **capital-cost estimate** (each shell is a major pressure vessel), prevents infeasible unit specifications, and identifies matches that need network resynthesis to keep the shell count reasonable. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the "shell rule" formula. With ΔT_min = 15 °C, find the number of shells required for an exchanger where ΔT_H = 75 °C and ΔT_C = 45 °C.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Shell rule:**

$$
N_s \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\max(\Delta T_H,\;\Delta T_C)}{\Delta T_{\text{min}}} \right\rceil \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
\Delta T_{\text{shell}} \;=\; \max(75,\;45) \;=\; 75 \;^{\circ}\text{C} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$
$$
N_s \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{75}{15} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 5 \rceil \;=\; \mathbf{5 \;\text{shells}} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]****

A pinch-designed heat exchanger network with **ΔT_min = 10 °C** has four exchanger units with the following data:

| Unit | T_H,in (°C) | T_H,out (°C) | T_C,in (°C) | T_C,out (°C) |
|------|------------|-------------|------------|-------------|
| E1   | 220        | 140         | 100        | 160         |
| E2   | 200        | 120         | 90         | 150         |
| E3   | 180        | 130         | 80         | 120         |
| E4   | 150        | 100         | 60         | 90          |

**(a)** Determine the **number of shells required for each exchanger** and the **total minimum number of shells** for the network. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the value of ΔT_min is **revised to 15 °C**, recompute the shell count. **Comment on the trend** and recommend whether the HEN should be **resynthesised**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute ΔT_H and ΔT_C for each unit.**

**Unit E1:**

$$
\Delta T_{H,E1} \;=\; \lvert 220 - 140 \rvert \;=\; 80 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$
$$
\Delta T_{C,E1} \;=\; \lvert 160 - 100 \rvert \;=\; 60 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Unit E2:**

$$
\Delta T_{H,E2} \;=\; \lvert 200 - 120 \rvert \;=\; 80 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$
$$
\Delta T_{C,E2} \;=\; \lvert 150 - 90 \rvert \;=\; 60 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Unit E3:**

$$
\Delta T_{H,E3} \;=\; \lvert 180 - 130 \rvert \;=\; 50 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$
$$
\Delta T_{C,E3} \;=\; \lvert 120 - 80 \rvert \;=\; 40 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Unit E4:**

$$
\Delta T_{H,E4} \;=\; \lvert 150 - 100 \rvert \;=\; 50 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$
$$
\Delta T_{C,E4} \;=\; \lvert 90 - 60 \rvert \;=\; 30 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ceiling rule** with **ΔT_min = 10 °C**.

**Unit E1:**

$$
N_{s,E1} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\max(80,\;60)}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 8 \rceil \;=\; 8
$$

**Unit E2:**

$$
N_{s,E2} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{80}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; 8
$$

**Unit E3:**

$$
N_{s,E3} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{50}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; 5
$$

**Unit E4:**

$$
N_{s,E4} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{50}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; 5
$$

**Step 3 — Sum across the network.**

$$
N_{s,\text{total}} \;=\; 8 + 8 + 5 + 5 \;=\; \mathbf{26 \;\text{shells}}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Re-apply the ceiling rule with ΔT_min = 15 °C.**

**Unit E1:**

$$
N_{s,E1} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{80}{15} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 5.33 \rceil \;=\; 6
$$

**Unit E2:**

$$
N_{s,E2} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{80}{15} \right\rceil \;=\; 6
$$

**Unit E3:**

$$
N_{s,E3} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{50}{15} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 3.33 \rceil \;=\; 4
$$

**Unit E4:**

$$
N_{s,E4} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{50}{15} \right\rceil \;=\; 4
$$

**Step 2 — Sum across the network.**

$$
N_{s,\text{total}} \;=\; 6 + 6 + 4 + 4 \;=\; \mathbf{20 \;\text{shells}}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment on the trend.**

Increasing ΔT_min from 10 °C to 15 °C reduces the total shell count from 26 to 20 (a drop of ≈ 23 %). (1 mark)

**Trade-off interpretation:** A larger ΔT_min means a *smaller* driving force ⇒ *less* heat recovered ⇒ *higher* utility cost (hot/cold utility usage increases). (1 mark)

The shell count falls because each shell can now absorb a larger temperature slice (15 °C instead of 10 °C). (1 mark)

**Recommendation on resynthesis:** Even at ΔT_min = 15 °C, units E1 and E2 still demand **6 shells each** — this is a clear signal that the **HEN topology itself is inefficient** for these high-ΔT matches. (1 mark)

**Recommended actions:**
- Introduce **stream splitting** on the hot streams of E1 and E2 to parallelise the duty. (1 mark)
- Re-evolve the network above the pinch and below the pinch separately. (1 mark)
- Consider **utilising cheaper utilities** for the surplus duty instead of stuffing it into 6 shells. (1 mark)

**Conclusion:** The network should be **resynthesised** to bring the per-unit shell count below 3, even if it costs a modest increase in utility consumption. (Bonus mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]****

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain why a **single 1-2 TEMA shell-and-tube exchanger** cannot handle a stream temperature change greater than ΔT_min. Derive the relationship between the number of shells and the stream temperature change. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pinch-designed HEN has **three units** with the following data (ΔT_min = 10 °C):

| Unit | T_H,in (°C) | T_H,out (°C) | T_C,in (°C) | T_C,out (°C) |
|------|------------|-------------|------------|-------------|
| E1   | 240        | 160         | 100        | 180         |
| E2   | 200        | 120         | 90         | 150         |
| E3   | 170        | 100         | 60         | 110         |

Calculate the **total minimum number of shells**. If utilities cost ₹ 5000/ton of refrigeration and ₹ 3000/ton of steam, **briefly discuss** the cost implication of needing multiple shells. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Sketch (3 marks):** A 1-2 TEMA exchanger has *one* shell pass and *two* tube passes. Because the tube-side fluid reverses direction, the shell-side and tube-side fluids flow **co-currently at one end** and counter-currently at the other. This co-current region creates a **temperature cross** at the outlet if either stream's ΔT inside the unit is too large. (1 mark for sketch, 1 mark for explanation of co-current region, 1 mark for the temperature cross idea.)

```
   SHELL SIDE (hot) ─────────────────────────────►
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │ ░░░░░ HOT IN → ░░░░░░░░░░░░░ HOT OUT ░░░░░░ │
   │ ╔═══╗  ╔═══╗  ╔═══╗  ╔═══╗                  │
   │ ║   ║  ║   ║  ║   ║  ║   ║   TUBES (cold)  │
   │ ╚═══╝  ╚═══╝  ╚═══╝  ╚═══╝                  │
   │ ◄──COLD IN   COLD OUT──►  (U-turn shown)    │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Physical Argument (2 marks):** A true counter-current exchanger could in principle achieve arbitrarily small ΔT approaches. But the **co-current end** in a 1-2 TEMA geometry imposes a constraint: the *effective* minimum approach inside the shell is approximately equal to the *larger* of the two stream ΔT changes. If a single shell tried to handle a stream ΔT greater than ΔT_min, the cold outlet would exceed the hot outlet — an impossibility.

**Derivation of the Shell Rule (2 marks):**

Let $\Delta T_{\text{shell}}$ be the effective minimum approach inside a single shell. For an exchanger handling a stream temperature change $\Delta T_{\text{stream}}$ with a network minimum approach $\Delta T_{\text{min}}$, the number of shells in series is the smallest integer $N_s$ such that

$$
\dfrac{\Delta T_{\text{stream}}}{N_s} \;\le\; \Delta T_{\text{min}}
$$

Rearranging,

$$
N_s \;\ge\; \dfrac{\Delta T_{\text{stream}}}{\Delta T_{\text{min}}}
$$

Since $N_s$ must be an integer, we apply the ceiling function:

$$
\boxed{\;N_s \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\Delta T_{\text{stream}}}{\Delta T_{\text{min}}} \right\rceil \;\approx\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\max(\Delta T_H,\Delta T_C)}{\Delta T_{\text{min}}} \right\rceil\;}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute ΔT_H, ΔT_C for each unit (3 marks).**

**Unit E1:**

$$
\Delta T_{H,E1} \;=\; \lvert 240 - 160 \rvert \;=\; 80 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$
$$
\Delta T_{C,E1} \;=\; \lvert 180 - 100 \rvert \;=\; 80 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Unit E2:**

$$
\Delta T_{H,E2} \;=\; \lvert 200 - 120 \rvert \;=\; 80 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$
$$
\Delta T_{C,E2} \;=\; \lvert 150 - 90 \rvert \;=\; 60 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Unit E3:**

$$
\Delta T_{H,E3} \;=\; \lvert 170 - 100 \rvert \;=\; 70 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$
$$
\Delta T_{C,E3} \;=\; \lvert 110 - 60 \rvert \;=\; 50 \;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ceiling rule with ΔT_min = 10 °C (3 marks).**

**Unit E1:**

$$
N_{s,E1} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\max(80,\;80)}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 8 \rceil \;=\; 8
$$

**Unit E2:**

$$
N_{s,E2} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\max(80,\;60)}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 8 \rceil \;=\; 8
$$

**Unit E3:**

$$
N_{s,E3} \;=\; \left\lceil \dfrac{\max(70,\;50)}{10} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 7 \rceil \;=\; 7
$$

**Step 3 — Sum (1 mark).**

$$
N_{s,\text{total}} \;=\; 8 + 8 + 7 \;=\; \mathbf{23 \;\text{shells}}
$$

**Cost implication discussion (Bonus, 1 mark):**

- **Capital cost (CAPEX):** 23 shells is a very high mechanical count. Each shell costs roughly ₹ 20–80 lakhs depending on size and material. The HEN demands a CAPEX of several crores, plus extra plot space, foundations, and piping.
- **Operating cost (OPEX) — utilities:** High shell count usually implies the network is squeezing too much heat recovery with a low ΔT_min. Increasing ΔT_min slightly would cut the shell count sharply (e.g., to ≈ 16 at ΔT_min = 15 °C) at the cost of more refrigeration and steam.
- **Trade-off rule of thumb:** If the *annual utility saving* from the extra heat recovery is **less** than the *annualised CAPEX* of the extra shells, the multi-shell design is uneconomical and the network should be resynthesised (stream splitting, relax ΔT_min, or use utilities).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function** — students often write $N_s = 80/10 = 8$ (which happens to be exact here) but then forget the *ceiling* and write $N_s = 7$ for cases like $75/10 = 7.5$. Always use $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ explicitly.
> 2. **Using the wrong stream's ΔT** — the rule uses $\max(\Delta T_H, \Delta T_C)$, **not** the average or the smaller value.
> 3. **Mixing units** — KTU answer scripts frequently mix °C and K; they are *equivalent in differences* but always state the unit clearly.
> 4. **Omitting resynthesis comment** — examiners in Part B reward 1–2 marks for an *engineering judgement* line ("network should be resynthesised", "stream splitting recommended"). Don't leave it out.
> 5. **Ignoring TEMA F-factor** — in advanced questions, the TEMA correction factor $F$ must be $\ge 0.75$; if not, multiple shells are mandated even when the ceiling rule says 1.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Number of Shells = minimum count of 1-2 TEMA shells in series needed to honour ΔT_min in a pinch match.
- **Why needed:** Real 1-2 TEMA exchangers cannot achieve the idealised counter-current ΔT_min; the co-current end creates a temperature-cross infeasibility.
- **Shell Rule (Master Formula):** $N_{s,i} = \lceil \max(\Delta T_H, \Delta T_C) / \Delta T_{\text{min}} \rceil$
- **Network Total:** $N_{s,\text{total}} = \sum_i N_{s,i}$ over all units.
- **Decision threshold:** 1 shell ⇒ 1-2 TEMA; 2 shells ⇒ 2-4 TEMA; 3 shells ⇒ 3-6 TEMA; etc.
- **Resynthesis trigger:** If $N_{s,i} > 3$ for any unit, the network topology (not the shell rule) is the problem — use **stream splitting** or relax ΔT_min.
- **TEMA F-factor check:** $F \ge 0.75$ is the practical acceptance criterion; if violated, the ceiling rule is *underestimating* the shells.
- **Common error:** Using arithmetic mean or geometric mean of $\Delta T_H, \Delta T_C$ instead of the *max*.
- **Cost impact:** Each shell ≈ ₹ 20–80 lakhs; high shell count is a CAPEX red flag.
- **KTU quick-tip:** Always write the *ceiling* symbol explicitly — it is a 0.5–1 mark item in ESE.
- **Default ΔT_min in KTU problems:** usually **10 °C**, but always check the problem statement.
- **Connection to other Module-2 topics:** Number-of-shells targeting sits *after* Area Targeting and *before* the final HEN grid evolution; it bridges the *thermodynamic* minimum (ΔT_min) and the *mechanical* minimum (shells).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Exchanger Network Design: The Pinch Design Method
Stream Splitting Design for Single Pinch Networks
Mass Exchange Networks for Minimum External MSA
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mass Exchange Networks for Minimum External MSA

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Mass Exchange Network (MEN)** is an integrated network of mass transfer operations (such as absorption, stripping, extraction, adsorption, leaching, or ion exchange) that systematically recovers and transfers **key components (pollutants or valuable species)** from process **rich streams** to a set of **lean streams** known as **Mass Separating Agents (MSAs)**, with the objective of meeting specified outlet composition targets at **minimum operating cost** and **minimum external resource consumption**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> - **Rich Stream (Source):** A process stream that contains an unwanted (or valuable) component to be removed. Composition decreases from supply ($y^S$) to target ($y^T$).
> - **Lean Stream / MSA (Sink):** A stream capable of accepting the key component. Composition increases from supply ($x^S$) to target ($x^T$).
> - **External MSA:** A lean stream purchased/imported from outside the process boundary (e.g., fresh water, fresh solvent, fresh adsorbent).
> - **Internal MSA:** A lean stream already existing within the process (e.g., wastewater, condensate, off-gas, spent solvent).
> - **Key Component (KC):** The specific species (pollutant or solute) being transferred through the network.
> - **Pinch Point in MEN:** The composition where the driving force (gap between rich and lean composite curves) is minimum, analogous to the thermal pinch in HEN.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of MEN as the "Mass-Transfer Twin" of Heat Exchange Network (HEN):**
>
> In a **Heat Exchange Network**, hot streams (sources) transfer heat to cold streams (sinks), and we ask: *"What is the minimum external hot/cold utility?"* The **Pinch Method** answers this by analyzing the composite curves of hot and cold streams.
>
> In a **Mass Exchange Network**, the same logic applies, but instead of temperature, we use **composition** as the driving potential:
> - **Rich streams** (analogous to *hot streams*) carry the key component.
> - **Lean streams/MSAs** (analogous to *cold streams*) absorb that component.
> - The **Pinch** now occurs in the **composition axis**, not the temperature axis.
> - The **minimum external MSA** is the mass-transfer equivalent of *minimum hot/cold utility*.

### 1.2.1 Real-World Analogy: A Sponge Network

Imagine a factory emitting soiled water (rich stream). Instead of buying huge amounts of fresh clean water (external MSA) to scrub the pollutants, the plant first uses its **already-available clean-ish process streams** (internal MSAs — like cooling tower blowdown or condensate) to absorb the pollutants. Only the **shortfall** is met by fresh external MSA. **Mass Exchange Network synthesis** finds exactly how much of that fresh water is truly needed — no more, no less.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The composition driving force in MEN is the difference between the rich stream composition and the **equilibrium composition** that the lean stream can achieve under steady contact. The minimum external MSA is highly sensitive to the **slope of the equilibrium line ($m$)**, since steeper equilibrium reduces capacity.
> - **Equilibrium slope for linear systems:** $m = y^*/x$ (dimensionless).
> - **Mass-load transferred per rich stream:** $W_i = G_i (y_i^S - y_i^T)$ measured in **kg of key component / s** (SI).

## 1.4 Classification of MSAs (Per KTU Syllabus)

| Category | Examples | Cost Implication |
|:---------|:---------|:-----------------|
| **External MSAs** | Fresh water, fresh solvent, virgin activated carbon | Purchased — to be **minimized** |
| **Internal MSAs** | Wastewater, condensate, off-gas, spent solvent streams | Already in the process — use **freely** above the pinch |
| **Interception MSAs** | Used to prevent MSAs from being polluted further | Strategic placement |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Steps of MEN Synthesis for Minimum External MSA

The systematic procedure (based on **El-Halwagi & Manousiouthakis, 1989**, foundational to the KTU syllabus) is:

1. **Identify rich process streams** — list supply composition $y^S$, target composition $y^T$, and flow rate $G$ for each.
2. **Identify candidate external MSAs** — list supply composition $x^S$ (usually pure) and maximum allowable target $x^T$ (often limited by regeneration capacity or environmental regulation).
3. **Determine mass transfer equilibrium relations** $y^* = f(x)$ for each rich-MSA pair.
4. **Construct the Composition Interval Diagram (CID)** — divide the composition axis into intervals where the set of active streams is constant.
5. **For each interval**, compute the mass-load transferred by the rich streams (sources) and that absorbed by the lean streams (sinks) including any internal MSAs.
6. **Identify the pinch** — the interval where the cumulative deficit is maximum (driving force is minimum but non-zero, $\varepsilon_{\min} > 0$).
7. **Calculate the minimum external MSA** as the cumulative external MSA needed in the intervals **above the pinch**.

## 2.2 Why the Pinch Defines the Minimum External MSA

> [!NOTE]
> **Above the Pinch (high composition region):**
> Only **internal MSAs** are allowed to flow; no external MSA may be introduced here (else it would cross the pinch and pollute the high-purity region of the lean composite curve).
>
> **Below the Pinch (low composition region):**
> External MSA is introduced to make up the absorption deficit.
>
> The pinch thus fixes the **maximum amount of internal MSA that can be regenerated/recycled** — any attempt to reduce external MSA further would require the pinch to be violated, which is thermodynamically infeasible.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are exam-critical. Memorize the units and sign conventions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary Condition |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | Mass-load of a rich stream | $W_i = G_i \,(y_i^S - y_i^T)$ | $G_i$ = kg/s; $y$ = mass fraction; $W_i$ = kg KC/s | $y^S > y^T$ |
| 2 | Mass-load absorbed by an MSA | $W_k = L_k \,(x_k^T - x_k^S)$ | $L_k$ = kg/s of MSA; $x$ = mass fraction in MSA | $x^T > x^S$ |
| 3 | Linear equilibrium | $y^* = m \, x + b$ | $m$ = slope; $b$ = intercept | Linear systems |
| 4 | Driving force in interval $j$ | $\Delta y_j = y^{\text{rich}}_j - y^*_j$ | Dimensionless composition | $\Delta y_j \ge \varepsilon_{\min}$ |
| 5 | Minimum external MSA (above pinch) | $L_{\text{ext,min}} = \dfrac{\sum_{j \in \text{above}} W^{\text{rich}}_j - \sum_{k \in \text{int,above}} W^{\text{MSA}}_k}{x^T - x^S}$ | kg of fresh MSA/s | Strictly positive |
| 6 | Total mass balance | $\sum W^{\text{rich}}_i = \sum W^{\text{ext}}_k + \sum W^{\text{int}}_k$ | kg KC/s | Closed network |
| 7 | Composition Interval load | $\Delta W_j^{\text{net}} = \sum G_{i,j}\,\Delta y_{i,j} - \sum L_{k,j}\,\Delta x_{k,j}$ | kg KC/s per interval | Used to build CID |
| 8 | Pinch criterion | $\Delta W^{\text{net}}_{\text{above pinch}} = 0$ at $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{\min}$ | — | Defines bottleneck |
| 9 | Minimum number of units (empirical) | $N_{\min} = N_R + N_S - 1$ | $N_R$ = rich streams; $N_S$ = MSAs | Euler's network theorem |
| 10 | Operating cost of external MSA | $\text{OC} = C_{\text{MSA}} \cdot L_{\text{ext}}$ | Cost per kg/s of MSA | $C_{\text{MSA}}$ = unit cost |

> **Note on $\vert \cdot \vert$ in tables:** Per the formatting rule, the absolute-value brackets are written as `\vert` or `\mid` in raw LaTeX, e.g. $L_{\text{ext}} = \vert \sum \Delta W_j^{\text{net}} \vert \big/ (x^T - x^S)$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wastewater treatment networks** — minimize fresh water usage (water pinch).
- **VOC (Volatile Organic Compound) abatement** — minimize fresh solvent or adsorbent.
- **Hydrogen network integration** — analogous to mass exchange with hydrogen as a "MSA-like" resource.
- **Solvent recovery networks** in pharmaceutical and petrochemical plants.
- **Carbon capture networks** — minimize fresh amine solvent as the MSA.

The minimum-external-MSA target directly sets the **floor for fresh resource consumption** and the **ceiling for the waste-generation footprint** of a chemical plant.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mass-Load and the Composite Mass Transfer Curve

### 3.1.1 Starting from the Single-Stage Mass Balance

Consider a single counter-current mass transfer stage where a rich stream $G$ (carrying the key component) contacts a lean stream $L$ (the MSA). The overall mass balance on the key component (KC) is:

$$
\text{(KC in via rich)} \;-\; \text{(KC out via rich)} \;=\; \text{(KC in via lean)} \;-\; \text{(KC out via lean)}
$$

In symbols:

$$
G\,y^S - G\,y^T = L\,x^T - L\,x^S
$$

Define the **mass-load of the rich stream** and the **mass-load of the MSA** as:

$$
W_R \;=\; G\,(y^S - y^T) \qquad \text{(KC transferred away from rich)}
$$

$$
W_L \;=\; L\,(x^T - x^S) \qquad \text{(KC absorbed by MSA)}
$$

The stage balance reduces to:

$$
W_R \;=\; W_L
$$

### 3.1.2 Driving Force Constraint

Thermodynamic feasibility demands that at every composition point along the contact, the rich stream composition must exceed the lean stream's **equilibrium composition**:

$$
y \;>\; y^*(x)
$$

At the rich inlet (supply point) of the interval, the available driving force is:

$$
\Delta y^{\text{supply}} = y^S - y^*(x^S)
$$

At the rich outlet (target point) of the interval, the remaining driving force is:

$$
\Delta y^{\text{target}} = y^T - y^*(x^T)
$$

The minimum driving force in the interval, $\varepsilon_{\min}$, must be strictly positive. This is the mass-transfer equivalent of $\Delta T_{\min}$ in HEN.

### 3.1.3 Composite Curves Construction

For a set of $N_R$ rich streams and $N_S$ lean streams (MSAs), the **rich composite** is the sum of mass-loads of all rich streams active in each composition interval, plotted as $W_R$ versus composition $y$ (rich). Similarly, the **lean composite** plots $\sum W_L$ versus the corresponding $x$ (lean) axis. Shifting the lean composite to the right by $\varepsilon_{\min}/m$ (for linear equilibrium $y^* = m x$) gives the **feasible lean composite** that touches the rich composite at the pinch.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Minimum External MSA Targeting

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem statement (KTU-style):**
> A process has **two rich streams** carrying a key pollutant (component A) and **one external MSA** (fresh water) plus **one internal MSA** (process condensate). Determine the **minimum external MSA** flow rate.
>
> **Data:**
> | Stream | Type | $G$ or $L$ (kg/s) | Supply $y^S$ or $x^S$ | Target $y^T$ or $x^T$ |
> |:------|:-----|:-----------------:|:----------------------:|:----------------------:|
> | $R_1$ | Rich | $G_1 = 100$ | $y_1^S = 0.10$ | $y_1^T = 0.02$ |
> | $R_2$ | Rich | $G_2 = 200$ | $y_2^S = 0.05$ | $y_2^T = 0.005$ |
> | $S_{\text{ext}}$ | External MSA | $L_{\text{ext}}$ | $x^S = 0.000$ | $x^T = 0.015$ |
> | $S_{\text{int}}$ | Internal MSA | $L_{\text{int}} = 50$ | $x^S = 0.005$ | $x^T = 0.020$ |
>
> **Equilibrium** (linear): $y^* = 1.5\,x$ (for both rich streams, simplified).
> **Minimum composition driving force:** $\varepsilon_{\min} = 0.005$.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Compute Mass-Load of Each Rich Stream

$$
W_{R_1} = G_1\,(y_1^S - y_1^T) = 100 \times (0.10 - 0.02) = 8.0 \;\;\text{kg A/s}
$$

$$
W_{R_2} = G_2\,(y_2^S - y_2^T) = 200 \times (0.05 - 0.005) = 9.0 \;\;\text{kg A/s}
$$

$$
W_{R,\text{total}} = 8.0 + 9.0 = 17.0 \;\;\text{kg A/s}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Compute Maximum Mass-Load Absorbable by Internal MSA

$$
W_{S_{\text{int}}} = L_{\text{int}}\,(x_{\text{int}}^T - x_{\text{int}}^S) = 50 \times (0.020 - 0.005) = 0.75 \;\;\text{kg A/s}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Compute Mass-Load Required from External MSA (Total)

By the overall key-component mass balance:

$$
W_{R,\text{total}} = W_{S_{\text{ext}}} + W_{S_{\text{int}}}
$$

$$
W_{S_{\text{ext}}} = 17.0 - 0.75 = 16.25 \;\;\text{kg A/s}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Convert Mass-Load to Minimum External MSA Flow Rate

$$
L_{\text{ext,min}} \;=\; \frac{W_{S_{\text{ext}}}}{x_{\text{ext}}^T - x_{\text{ext}}^S} \;=\; \frac{16.25}{0.015 - 0.000} \;=\; 1083.33 \;\;\text{kg/s of fresh water}
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Pinch Identification (Feasibility Check)

Check the driving force at each endpoint to ensure the interval is feasible:

**At rich stream $R_1$ supply ($y_1^S = 0.10$):**

$$
y^*(x = 0.000) = 1.5 \times 0.000 = 0.000
$$

$$
\Delta y = 0.10 - 0.000 = 0.100 \;\;\geq\;\; \varepsilon_{\min} = 0.005 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**At rich stream $R_2$ target ($y_2^T = 0.005$):**

The MSA at the corresponding equilibrium:

$$
x \;=\; \frac{y}{m} \;=\; \frac{0.005}{1.5} \;=\; 0.00333
$$

$$
\Delta y = 0.005 - y^*(x = 0.00333) = 0.005 - 0.005 = 0.000
$$

The driving force vanishes — this is the **pinch**. A minimum driving force $\varepsilon_{\min} = 0.005$ must be enforced by adjusting either the equilibrium slope, the MSA target composition, or accepting a slightly higher external MSA flow rate.

### 3.2.6 Step 6 — Recompute with Enforced $\varepsilon_{\min}$

Shift the lean composite curve to the right by $\varepsilon_{\min}/m$:

$$
\text{Shift} \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{\min}}{m} \;=\; \frac{0.005}{1.5} \;=\; 0.00333 \;\;\text{(in $x$-units)}
$$

The effective target composition of the external MSA becomes:

$$
x_{\text{ext,eff}}^T \;=\; 0.015 - 0.00333 \;=\; 0.01167
$$

Re-solving for the external MSA:

$$
L_{\text{ext,min}}^{\text{pinch-corrected}} \;=\; \frac{16.25}{0.01167 - 0.000} \;\approx\; 1392.86 \;\;\text{kg/s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** With a realistic minimum driving force of $\varepsilon_{\min} = 0.005$, the minimum external MSA requirement rises from **1083.33 kg/s** (idealized) to **~1392.86 kg/s** — a 28.6 % increase. This is precisely the trade-off the designer must report to management: tighter purity = more fresh MSA.

### 3.2.7 Step 7 — Final Tabulated Answer

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|:---------|:-------|:------|:-----|
| Mass-load, rich 1 | $W_{R_1}$ | 8.00 | kg A/s |
| Mass-load, rich 2 | $W_{R_2}$ | 9.00 | kg A/s |
| Total rich load | $W_{R,\text{total}}$ | 17.00 | kg A/s |
| Internal MSA load | $W_{S_{\text{int}}}$ | 0.75 | kg A/s |
| External MSA load | $W_{S_{\text{ext}}}$ | 16.25 | kg A/s |
| **Min. external MSA (pinch-corrected)** | $L_{\text{ext,min}}$ | **1392.86** | **kg/s** |
| Pinch location (in $y$) | $y_{\text{pinch}}$ | 0.0050 | mass fraction |
| Pinch location (in $x$) | $x_{\text{pinch}}$ | 0.0033 | mass fraction |
| Min. driving force | $\varepsilon_{\min}$ | 0.0050 | mass fraction |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Above vs Below the Pinch

| Region | Streams Active | External MSA Permitted? | Mass-Load Balance |
|:-------|:---------------|:------------------------|:------------------|
| **Above the pinch** (rich composition high) | All rich streams + internal MSA (no external) | **NO** — would pollute lean composite | $\sum W_R^{\text{above}} = W_{S_{\text{int}}}^{\text{above}}$ |
| **At the pinch** | Bottleneck interval | Only as required by $\varepsilon_{\min}$ | $\Delta y = \varepsilon_{\min}$ exactly |
| **Below the pinch** (rich composition low) | Remaining rich streams + external MSA | **YES** — primary supply | $\sum W_R^{\text{below}} = W_{S_{\text{ext}}}^{\text{below}}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — MEN Synthesis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define process streams and MSAs] --> B[List rich streams: G, yS, yT]
    A --> C[List external MSAs: L, xS, xT]
    A --> D[List internal MSAs: L, xS, xT]
    B --> E[Determine equilibrium: ystar equals f of x]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Construct Composition Interval Diagram CID]
    F --> G[Compute mass-loads in each interval]
    G --> H[Plot rich composite curve in y axis]
    G --> I[Plot lean composite curve in x axis]
    I --> J[Shift lean composite by epsilon min over m]
    J --> K{Lean touches rich?}
    K -->|No| L[Reduce epsilon and retry]
    L --> J
    K -->|Yes| M[Identify pinch point and pinch interval]
    M --> N[Above pinch: internal MSA only]
    M --> O[Below pinch: external MSA allowed]
    N --> P[Compute minimum external MSA above pinch]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Final answer: L ext min in kg per s]
    Q --> R[End: Report target to design team]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Conceptual MEN Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Rich Stream R1: G1, y1S to y1T] -->|stage 1| M1[Mass Exchanger 1]
    R2[Rich Stream R2: G2, y2S to y2T] -->|stage 2| M2[Mass Exchanger 2]
    S1[Internal MSA: Lint, xS to xT] -->|recycle| M1
    S2[External MSA: Lext, xS to xT] -->|fresh feed| M2
    M1 --> P1[Loaded internal MSA: Wint kg KC per s]
    M2 --> P2[Loaded external MSA: Wext kg KC per s]
    R1 --> O1[Purified R1 to process]
    R2 --> O2[Purified R2 to process]
    P1 --> REG[Regeneration or Discharge]
    P2 --> REG
    REG -.->|regen or treat| S1
```

## 4.3 Composition Interval Diagram (CID) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Composition axis y in rich, x in lean] --> I1[Interval 1: y from 0.10 to 0.05]
    I1 --> I2[Interval 2: y from 0.05 to 0.02]
    I2 --> I3[Interval 3: y from 0.02 to 0.005]
    I3 --> I4[Interval 4: y below 0.005 pinch region]
    I4 --> P[Pinch at y equals 0.005]
    P --> AP[Above pinch: internal MSA only]
    P --> BP[Below pinch: external MSA supply]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the CID:** Each interval lists the active rich streams and active MSAs. The cumulative mass-load difference between rich and lean streams, integrated across intervals, gives the surplus (above) and deficit (below) — the pinch interval is where this cumulative difference peaks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q1.** Define the term **Mass Separating Agent (MSA)**. Distinguish between **external** and **internal** MSAs with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

A **Mass Separating Agent (MSA)** is a lean stream, internal or external to the process, that selectively accepts one or more key components (such as pollutants or valuable species) from rich process streams through a mass transfer operation (e.g., absorption, extraction, adsorption).

- **External MSA:** A lean stream imported from outside the process boundary, e.g., **fresh water** used to absorb $SO_2$ from a flue gas.
- **Internal MSA:** A lean stream already available within the process, e.g., **process condensate** that can absorb residual VOCs from an off-gas.

The process-integration objective is to **maximize the use of internal MSAs** and thereby **minimize the external MSA requirement**. **[3 Marks]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**

**Q2.** What is the **pinch point** in a Mass Exchange Network? Why is it the bottleneck for the minimum external MSA target?

**Model Answer:**

The **pinch point** in a MEN is the composition at which the driving force (gap between the rich composite curve and the feasible lean composite curve) is minimum, i.e., equal to the prescribed $\varepsilon_{\min}$. It is the mass-transfer analogue of the HEN thermal pinch.

It is the **bottleneck** because:

- **Above the pinch**, the rich stream composition is high; only the most-pure internal MSAs can absorb further. No external MSA may be introduced above the pinch without polluting the lean composite and driving the design infeasibly to the right.
- **Below the pinch**, the rich composition is low and the external MSA absorbs the remaining load.

Thus the **maximum internal MSA absorption** achievable above the pinch fixes the **minimum external MSA** that must be introduced below the pinch. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **pinch-analysis procedure** for synthesizing a Mass Exchange Network that meets outlet composition targets with the **minimum external MSA**. List the role of (i) rich streams, (ii) lean streams, (iii) the equilibrium relation, and (iv) the minimum composition driving force $\varepsilon_{\min}$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Pinch-analysis procedure for minimum external MSA (El-Halwagi & Manousiouthakis, 1989):**

1. **Identify rich streams (sources):** For each, record flow rate $G$, supply composition $y^S$, and target composition $y^T$.
2. **Identify lean streams / MSAs (sinks):** For each, record $L$, supply $x^S$, target $x^T$, and whether it is **internal** (already in process) or **external** (to be minimized).
3. **Determine the equilibrium relation** $y^* = f(x)$ between the rich and lean streams in the contactor (often linear: $y^* = m x + b$).
4. **Construct the Composition Interval Diagram (CID):** Sort all $y^S$, $y^T$, and equivalent $x$ values; divide the composition axis into intervals where the set of active streams is constant.
5. **For each interval**, calculate the net mass-load $\Delta W_j = \sum G_{i,j}\,\Delta y_{i,j} - \sum L_{k,j}\,\Delta x_{k,j}$.
6. **Plot rich composite curve** ($W$ vs $y$) and **lean composite curve** ($W$ vs $x$).
7. **Shift the lean composite** to the right by $\varepsilon_{\min}/m$ (linear case) to enforce the minimum driving force.
8. **Identify the pinch** — the interval where the feasible lean curve just touches the rich curve.
9. **Compute minimum external MSA** as the net load below the pinch divided by the lean-stream composition change:
$$
L_{\text{ext,min}} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{j \le \text{pinch}} \Delta W_j^{\text{below}}}{x_{\text{ext}}^T - x_{\text{ext}}^S}
$$

Roles: (i) rich streams supply the key component; (ii) MSAs absorb it; (iii) equilibrium dictates the feasible region (driving force); (iv) $\varepsilon_{\min}$ ensures thermodynamic feasibility. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b)** A process has **two rich gas streams** and **one external liquid MSA** (water). The data are:

| Stream | $G$ or $L$ (kg/s) | Supply | Target | Equilibrium with water |
|:------|:-----------------:|:------:|:------:|:------------------------|
| $R_1$ (gas) | $G_1 = 120$ | $y_1^S = 0.080$ | $y_1^T = 0.010$ | $y^* = 1.20\,x$ |
| $R_2$ (gas) | $G_2 = 80$ | $y_2^S = 0.050$ | $y_2^T = 0.005$ | $y^* = 1.20\,x$ |
| External MSA (water) | $L_{\text{ext}}$ | $x^S = 0.000$ | $x^T = 0.020$ | — |

Take $\varepsilon_{\min} = 0.002$ (in $y$-units). Calculate the **minimum external water (MSA) flow rate** and the **pinch composition**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass-load of each rich stream:**

$$
W_{R_1} = 120 \times (0.080 - 0.010) = 120 \times 0.070 = 8.40 \;\;\text{kg KC/s}
$$

$$
W_{R_2} = 80 \times (0.050 - 0.005) = 80 \times 0.045 = 3.60 \;\;\text{kg KC/s}
$$

$$
W_{R,\text{total}} = 8.40 + 3.60 = 12.00 \;\;\text{kg KC/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Check pinch location (lowest end of $R_2$):**

At $R_2$ target $y_2^T = 0.005$, the equilibrium composition of water is:

$$
x_{\text{eq}} = \frac{y_2^T}{m} = \frac{0.005}{1.20} = 0.00417
$$

The driving force without $\varepsilon_{\min}$ enforcement is:

$$
\Delta y = y_2^T - y^*(x = 0.00417) = 0.005 - 0.005 = 0.000
$$

Thus the **pinch** is at the **$R_2$ target end**, with $y_{\text{pinch}} = 0.005$.

**Step 3 — Apply $\varepsilon_{\min}$ shift on the lean (water) composite:**

$$
\text{Shift} = \frac{\varepsilon_{\min}}{m} = \frac{0.002}{1.20} = 0.001667
$$

The effective target composition for the water becomes:

$$
x_{\text{eff}}^T = 0.020 - 0.001667 = 0.01833
$$

**Step 4 — Compute minimum external water flow rate:**

$$
L_{\text{ext,min}} = \frac{W_{R,\text{total}}}{x_{\text{eff}}^T - x^S} = \frac{12.00}{0.01833 - 0.000} = 654.55 \;\;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Final results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|:---------|:------|
| Total rich mass-load | **12.00 kg KC/s** |
| Pinch composition (rich) | **$y_{\text{pinch}} = 0.005$** |
| Pinch composition (lean) | **$x_{\text{pinch}} = 0.00417$** |
| Effective water target | **$x_{\text{eff}}^T = 0.01833$** |
| **Minimum external water (MSA)** | **$\boxed{L_{\text{ext,min}} = 654.55 \;\text{kg/s}}$** |

**[7 Marks]**

---

### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **Composition Interval Diagram (CID)** method for targeting the **minimum external MSA**. How does the CID differ from the heat-cascade used in HEN pinch analysis? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Composition Interval Diagram (CID)** is a tabular/numerical representation of the composite mass transfer problem in MEN synthesis. The procedure is:

1. Sort all supply and target compositions (both $y$ and equivalent $x$) in descending order.
2. Define intervals between successive composition values.
3. For each interval $j$, sum the mass-loads of all **rich streams** active in that interval:
$$
\Delta W_R^{\text{net}}(j) = \sum_{i \in \text{rich, active in } j} G_i\,\Delta y_{i,j}
$$
4. Sum the mass-loads of all **MSAs** active in the interval:
$$
\Delta W_L^{\text{net}}(j) = \sum_{k \in \text{MSA, active in } j} L_k\,\Delta x_{k,j}
$$
5. The **net mass-load** in each interval is:
$$
\Delta W_{\text{net}}(j) = \Delta W_R^{\text{net}}(j) - \Delta W_L^{\text{net}}(j)
$$
6. Integrate from the highest composition downwards; the **interval where the cumulative load peaks** is the **pinch**.
7. The **minimum external MSA** is the cumulative net load **below the pinch** divided by the lean-stream composition change.

**Difference from HEN heat-cascade:**

| Aspect | HEN Heat-Cascade | MEN Composition Interval Diagram |
|:-------|:-----------------|:--------------------------------|
| Axis | Temperature $T$ (K) | Composition $y$, $x$ (mass fraction) |
| Source potential | $T^S$ (supply) | $y^S$ (rich supply) |
| Sink potential | $T^T$ (target) | $x^T$ (MSA target) |
| Driving force | $\Delta T_{\min}$ | $\varepsilon_{\min}$ (composition gap) |
| Cascade quantity | Heat $Q$ (kW) | Mass-load $W$ (kg KC/s) |
| Feasibility constraint | $\Delta T \ge \Delta T_{\min}$ | $y - y^*(x) \ge \varepsilon_{\min}$ |

The CID is the **mass-transfer analogue of the HEN problem table algorithm** and is treated using the same cascade-from-top (or bottom) approach, with the pinch pinpointed at the interval of zero (or maximum) cumulative net load. **[7 Marks]**

---

**(b)** A refinery has **three rich process streams** and an **external solvent MSA**. Compute the **minimum external solvent flow rate** and the **pinch composition**. **[7 Marks]**

**Data:**

| Stream | $G$ or $L$ (kg/s) | Supply | Target | Equilibrium with solvent |
|:------|:-----------------:|:------:|:------:|:------------------------|
| $R_1$ | $G_1 = 150$ | $y_1^S = 0.12$ | $y_1^T = 0.02$ | $y^* = 0.80\,x$ |
| $R_2$ | $G_2 = 100$ | $y_2^S = 0.06$ | $y_2^T = 0.01$ | $y^* = 0.80\,x$ |
| $R_3$ | $G_3 = 200$ | $y_3^S = 0.04$ | $y_3^T = 0.005$ | $y^* = 0.80\,x$ |
| External MSA | $L_{\text{ext}}$ | $x^S = 0$ | $x^T = 0.025$ | — |

Take $\varepsilon_{\min} = 0.003$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass-loads:**

$$
W_{R_1} = 150 \times (0.12 - 0.02) = 15.00 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
W_{R_2} = 100 \times (0.06 - 0.01) = 5.00 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
W_{R_3} = 200 \times (0.04 - 0.005) = 7.00 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
W_{R,\text{total}} = 15.00 + 5.00 + 7.00 = 27.00 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify pinch candidate (lowest target = tightest constraint):**

The smallest target is $R_3$ with $y_3^T = 0.005$. Equilibrium composition of solvent:

$$
x_{\text{eq}} = \frac{y_3^T}{m} = \frac{0.005}{0.80} = 0.00625
$$

Driving force:

$$
\Delta y = y_3^T - y^*(x=0.00625) = 0.005 - 0.005 = 0.000
$$

So the **pinch** is at $y_{\text{pinch}} = 0.005$ (on the rich axis), $x_{\text{pinch}} = 0.00625$ (on the lean axis).

**Step 3 — Apply $\varepsilon_{\min}$ shift:**

$$
\text{Shift} = \frac{\varepsilon_{\min}}{m} = \frac{0.003}{0.80} = 0.00375
$$

$$
x_{\text{eff}}^T = 0.025 - 0.00375 = 0.02125
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum external solvent:**

$$
L_{\text{ext,min}} = \frac{27.00}{0.02125 - 0} = 1270.59 \;\text{kg/s of solvent}
$$

**Final answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|:---------|:------|
| Total rich load | 27.00 kg KC/s |
| Pinch ($y$-axis) | 0.0050 |
| Pinch ($x$-axis) | 0.00625 |
| Effective MSA target | 0.02125 |
| **Minimum external solvent** | **$\boxed{L_{\text{ext,min}} \approx 1270.59 \;\text{kg/s}}$** |

**[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in MEN Minimum External MSA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\varepsilon_{\min}$ shift.** A surprising number of students compute $L_{\text{ext,min}}$ using the raw $x^T$ instead of $x_{\text{eff}}^T = x^T - \varepsilon_{\min}/m$. The correct, **thermodynamically feasible** value is **larger** than the naive value. The examiner deducts up to **2 marks** for this omission.
>
> 2. **Sign error in mass balance.** Remember: rich streams **lose** the key component ($y^S > y^T$), MSAs **gain** it ($x^T > x^S$). Mixing the sign gives a negative or impossibly small $L_{\text{ext,min}}$.
>
> 3. **Confusing the equilibrium slope direction.** For gas absorption into a liquid, $y^* = m x$ where $m$ can be $>1$ or $<1$. Forgetting to invert $m$ when converting between the rich ($y$) and lean ($x$) axes loses up to **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the pinch completely.** Some students use the *total* mass balance without identifying the pinch. The minimum external MSA in a true MEN is **not** a single mass balance — it is determined by the **pinch interval**. The examiner awards at most 3 out of 7 if the pinch is missing.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always report $L_{\text{ext,min}}$ in **kg/s** or **kg/h** consistently with the flow rates given.
>
> 6. **Mixing up "external" vs "internal" MSAs.** Internal MSAs can be used freely above the pinch; external MSAs cannot. Confusing them leads to over-designed networks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Mass Exchange Networks for Minimum External MSA**
>
> - **MEN** = integrated network of mass transfer operations (absorption, stripping, extraction, adsorption, leaching, ion exchange) that transfers a key component from rich streams to lean streams (MSAs).
> - **Rich stream (source):** $y^S \to y^T$, with $y^S > y^T$.
> - **Lean stream / MSA (sink):** $x^S \to x^T$, with $x^T > x^S$.
> - **External MSA:** imported, must be **minimized**.
> - **Internal MSA:** already in process, used **freely above the pinch**.
> - **Equilibrium relation:** $y^* = m x + b$ (linear) or $y^* = f(x)$ (non-linear).
> - **Driving force:** $\Delta y = y - y^*(x) \ge \varepsilon_{\min}$.
> - **Pinch** = the composition where $\Delta y$ is minimum (typically at one of the rich stream target compositions).
> - **CID (Composition Interval Diagram):** divides the composition axis into intervals with constant active streams; cumulative net load is computed from top.
> - **Pinch-shift correction:** $x_{\text{eff}}^T = x^T - \varepsilon_{\min}/m$ for linear equilibrium.
> - **Minimum external MSA formula:**
$$
L_{\text{ext,min}} = \frac{\sum_{j \in \text{below pinch}} \Delta W_j^{\text{net}}}{x_{\text{ext}}^T - x^S}
$$
> - **Above-pinch rule:** Only internal MSAs are used; **no** external MSA is permitted above the pinch (would cross the pinch and violate feasibility).
> - **Below-pinch rule:** External MSA supplies the residual absorption load.
> - **Network minimum units (empirical):** $N_{\min} = N_R + N_S - 1$ (Euler's theorem).
> - **Analogy with HEN:** Temperature $\leftrightarrow$ Composition; $\Delta T_{\min} \leftrightarrow \varepsilon_{\min}$; hot utility $\leftrightarrow$ external MSA; composite curves $\leftrightarrow$ rich/lean composite curves; problem table $\leftrightarrow$ CID.
> - **Key takeaway:** The pinch is the **bottleneck**; relaxing $\varepsilon_{\min}$ reduces $L_{\text{ext,min}}$ at the cost of larger mass-transfer equipment; tightening $\varepsilon_{\min}$ increases $L_{\text{ext,min}}$ but reduces exchanger size — a classic **operating-vs-capital trade-off**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water System Design: Water Use
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Water System Design: Water Use # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the framework of **Process Integration** (specifically **Water Pinch Analysis** pioneered by Wang & Smith, 1994), **Water System Design: Water Use** refers to the systematic methodology of characterizing industrial water-consuming operations to establish the *minimum theoretical freshwater demand* and *minimum wastewater generation* of a process plant. It is founded on the principle that water is a **mass-transfer agent**, not a consumer — its role is to *pick up contaminants* from process streams up to a maximum allowable limit. A **water-using operation** (WUO) is any unit operation where water enters, contacts a process stream, and leaves carrying an increased contaminant load. For each WUO, three parameters are mandatory: 1. **Maximum inlet concentration** $C_{in}^{max}$ — the highest contaminant concentration the operation can tolerate at the water inlet (driven by equilibrium, solubility, or product purity constraints). 2. **Maximum outlet concentration** $C_{out}^{max}$ — the highest contaminant concentration permitted in the discharged water (driven by downstream reuse, corrosion, fouling, or environmental discharge limits). 3. **Mass load transferred** $\Delta m$ (kg/h) — the fixed quantity of contaminant the operation must remove from the process stream per unit time. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT745 Module 3):** > The student must be able to identify water sources, sinks, and determine **limiting water flowrates** ($L_{min}$) for each water-using operation. This is the *first* step in any water network pinch problem. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of Water as a "Dirty Laundry Truck" 🚛** > - A laundry truck (water stream) arrives **clean** (low concentration $C_{in}$) and leaves **dirty** (high concentration $C_{out}$). > - The truck has a **size limit** — you cannot make it infinitely big; so to carry more dirt (mass load $\Delta m$), the dirt must be **concentrated** more efficiently. > - The factory rule ($C_{out}^{max}$) says: *the dirt in the truck cannot exceed a certain concentration*, otherwise it spills out and contaminates the environment. > - The product rule ($C_{in}^{max}$) says: *if the truck arrives too dirty, it cannot accept more dirt without violating product purity*. > - Therefore, to carry a **fixed amount of dirt** ($\Delta m$) at the **highest allowable dirtiness level** ($C_{out}^{max}$) starting from the **cleanest allowable starting state** ($C_{in}^{max}$), you need a **minimum truck size** (limiting water flowrate $L_{min}$). In short: **water flowrate is dictated by the "carrying capacity" between two concentration limits for a given mass load.** ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - Standard concentration unit for water pinch: **ppm (parts per million)** or **mg/kg** — equivalent for dilute aqueous systems. - Standard flowrate unit: **kg/s** or **t/h** (mass-based preferred over volumetric for temperature independence). - Standard mass-load unit: **kg/h** or **kg/s** of contaminant. - Default reference for pure water inlet: **0 ppm** (uncontaminated freshwater). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Water-Using Operations (WUOs) Water-using operations in chemical plants fall into two broad categories: ### A. Mass-Transfer-Based Operations Water physically contacts a process stream and absorbs/desorbs contaminants. | Operation Type | Mechanism | Typical Contaminants | |---|---|---| | **Gas Absorption / Scrubbing** | Gas → Liquid transfer | H₂S, CO₂, SO₂, VOCs | | **Liquid–Liquid Extraction** | Solute partition between solvents | Organic acids, phenols | | **Stripping / Desorption** | Liquid → Gas transfer | NH₃, H₂S | | **Leaching / Washing** | Solid → Liquid dissolution | Salts, sugars, ores | | **Reaction with water (quench, hydrolysis)** | Water participates chemically | Acids, alkalis | ### B. Non-Mass-Transfer Operations Water does **not** pick up contaminants from a process stream but may still have quality constraints. | Operation Type | Constraint | |---|---| | Cooling tower make-up | TDS, hardness, silica limits | | Boiler feedwater | Hardness, dissolved O₂, silica | | General utility / cleaning | Mostly $C_{in}^{max}$ constraint only | ## 2.2 The Three Building Blocks of Any WUO For *every* water-using operation, the following data triad is fixed by the process: 1. **Mass Load** $\Delta m$ (kg/h): Determined by process chemistry — *the operation MUST remove this much contaminant per unit time*. 2. **Maximum Inlet Concentration** $C_{in}^{max}$ (ppm): Determined by *equilibrium*, *solubility*, *product purity*, or *equipment integrity*. 3. **Maximum Outlet Concentration** $C_{out}^{max}$ (ppm): Determined by *downstream reuse compatibility*, *corrosion limit*, or *environmental discharge*. > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Concept:** $C_{in}^{max}$ and $C_{out}^{max}$ are *opposing limits*. $C_{out}^{max}$ sets how much dirt the water can carry out; $C_{in}^{max}$ sets how much dirt the water can already have coming in. The *driving force* for mass transfer is $(C_{out} - C_{in})$ — but for **limiting flowrate** we always use the *maximum allowable driving force*: $(C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max})$. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta m = L \cdot (C_{out} - C_{in})$ | Mass balance on contaminant across WUO | kg/h | | 2 | $L_{min} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max}}$ | Limiting (minimum) water flowrate for a WUO | kg/h | | 3 | $C_{out}^{max} = C_{in}^{max} + \dfrac{\Delta m}{L_{min}}$ | Rearranged form to back-calculate outlet | ppm | | 4 | $\Delta m_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta m_i$ | Total contaminant load of the plant | kg/h | | 5 | $L_{freshwater,min} = \dfrac{\Delta m_{total}}{C_{out,global}^{max} - 0}$ | Minimum freshwater if no reuse | kg/h | | 6 | $F_{waste,max} = L_{waste} \cdot C_{out,waste}^{max}$ | Maximum waste contaminant discharge | kg/h | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vertical pipe rule for tables:** When writing absolute values or magnitudes in exam answers, use $\lvert x \rvert$ or "mod x" notation, **never** the bare pipe `|` symbol, to avoid markdown rendering errors. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters - **Water Scarcity**: In process hubs like Kerala's industrial belts (Cochin, Palakkad), freshwater cost is rising. KTU projects routinely involve targeting 30–60% reduction in freshwater intake. - **Effluent Treatment Cost (ETC)**: Wastewater treatment scales with **both** flowrate and concentration. Minimizing $L_{min}$ directly reduces capex. - **Regulatory Compliance**: Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB) norms mandate discharge concentrations below threshold — $C_{out}^{max}$ is often set by regulation. - **Energy Pinch Linkage**: Reducing hot/cold utility water also reduces the heating/cooling duty in the heat exchanger network (HEN). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Limiting Water Flowrate $L_{min}$ from Mass Balance ### Starting Premise For a single water-using operation (WUO) operating at steady state, the contaminant mass balance is: $$ \text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} $$ At steady state, Accumulation = 0, and no chemical reaction occurs in the water phase (the contaminant is merely *picked up*, not generated). So: $$ 0 = L \cdot C_{in} + 0 - L \cdot C_{out} + \Delta m_{pickup} $$ > **Step 1 — Write the mass balance over the WUO:** > > $$L \cdot C_{out} = L \cdot C_{in} + \Delta m$$ > > where $L$ = water mass flowrate (kg/h), $C_{in}$ / $C_{out}$ = inlet/outlet contaminant concentrations (ppm = mg/kg), and $\Delta m$ = contaminant mass load picked up by water per unit time (kg/h). > **Step 2 — Factor out $L$:** > > $$L \cdot (C_{out} - C_{in}) = \Delta m$$ > **Step 3 — Solve for $L$:** > > $$L = \dfrac{\Delta m}{C_{out} - C_{in}}$$ > > This is the *general* water flowrate for *any* chosen inlet/outlet concentrations. > **Step 4 — Apply the maximum allowable driving force to find $L_{min}$:** > The operation will work for *any* $C_{out} \leq C_{out}^{max}$ and $C_{in} \geq C_{in}^{max}$ (note reverse inequalities — operation cannot start too dirty and cannot exit too concentrated). To **minimize** $L$, the *driving force* $(C_{out} - C_{in})$ must be **maximized**. Therefore, plug in the *maximum allowable* concentrations: > > $$\boxed{\,L_{min} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max}}\,}$$ > **Step 5 — Sanity check on limiting case:** If $C_{in}^{max} = 0$ (freshwater, no reuse allowed) and $C_{out}^{max}$ is at discharge limit, $L_{min}$ is largest (worst case). If $C_{in}^{max}$ rises (reuse permitted), $L_{min}$ shrinks — confirming that **reuse reduces freshwater demand**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Limiting Water Profile (Composite Curve Concept) When multiple WUOs exist, each has its own $(C_{in}^{max}, C_{out}^{max}, \Delta m, L_{min})$. Plotting all of them on a **Concentration vs Mass Load (C-M)** diagram produces the **Limiting Composite Curve**. ### Procedure (Board Exam Step-by-Step) **Step 1.** List all WUOs with their $\Delta m$, $C_{in}^{max}$, $C_{out}^{max}$. **Step 2.** Order WUOs by *increasing* $C_{out}^{max}$ (i.e., the *strictest exit* first). **Step 3.** Construct a table with cumulative mass load: $$ M_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \Delta m_i $$ **Step 4.** For each WUO, calculate $L_{min,i}$. **Step 5.** Plot $C$ (y-axis) vs $M$ (x-axis). Each WUO becomes a *horizontal segment* at $C_{out}^{max}$ spanning a horizontal width $\Delta m_i$. **Step 6.** Connect the right-end of each segment vertically down (mass-balance continuity) to the $C_{in}^{max}$ of the *next* WUO. These vertical connectors represent **mixing points** in a reuse network. **Step 7.** The resulting composite curve is the **Limiting Composite Curve**. Its slope at any vertical line = the cumulative $L$ in that region. ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Style) ### Problem A process has **three** water-using operations with the following limiting data: | WUO | $\Delta m$ (kg/h) | $C_{in}^{max}$ (ppm) | $C_{out}^{max}$ (ppm) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 4.0 | 0 | 200 | | 2 | 3.0 | 50 | 100 | | 3 | 5.0 | 100 | 400 | **Task:** Calculate $L_{min,i}$ for each WUO, the cumulative mass load, and the limiting composite curve vertices. ### Step-by-Step Solution > **Step 1 — Calculate $L_{min}$ for each WUO** (applying $L_{min} = \Delta m / (C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max})$): > > $$\begin{aligned} > L_{min,1} &= \dfrac{4.0}{200 - 0} = \dfrac{4.0}{200} = 0.0200 \ \text{kg water/kg load} \quad \Rightarrow \text{for } 4 \text{ kg/h: } 80{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \\ > L_{min,2} &= \dfrac{3.0}{100 - 50} = \dfrac{3.0}{50} = 0.0600 \quad \Rightarrow 50{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \\ > L_{min,3} &= \dfrac{5.0}{400 - 100} = \dfrac{5.0}{300} = 0.01667 \quad \Rightarrow 125{,}000 \text{ kg/h} > \end{aligned}$$ > **Step 2 — Order WUOs by increasing $C_{out}^{max}$:** > > WUO 2 ($C_{out}=100$) → WUO 1 ($C_{out}=200$) → WUO 3 ($C_{out}=400$) > **Step 3 — Build the cumulative mass-load table:** > > $$\begin{aligned} > M_0 &= 0 \ \text{kg/h} \quad (\text{freshwater inlet}) \\ > M_1 &= 0 + 3.0 = 3.0 \ \text{kg/h} \quad (\text{after WUO 2}) \\ > M_2 &= 3.0 + 4.0 = 7.0 \ \text{kg/h} \quad (\text{after WUO 1}) \\ > M_3 &= 7.0 + 5.0 = 12.0 \ \text{kg/h} \quad (\text{after WUO 3}) > \end{aligned}$$ > **Step 4 — Build the C-M diagram vertices (concentration at each mass-load point):** > > $$\begin{aligned} > \text{Point A: } &(M=0, \ C=0) && \text{Freshwater enters} \\ > \text{Point B: } &(M=0, \ C=C_{in,2}^{max}=50) && \text{Rise to WUO 2 inlet} \\ > \text{Point C: } &(M=3.0, \ C=C_{out,2}^{max}=100) && \text{WUO 2 exit} \\ > \text{Point D: } &(M=3.0, \ C=C_{in,1}^{max}=0) && \text{Drop to WUO 1 inlet} \\ > \text{Point E: } &(M=7.0, \ C=C_{out,1}^{max}=200) && \text{WUO 1 exit} \\ > \text{Point F: } &(M=7.0, \ C=C_{in,3}^{max}=100) && \text{Drop to WUO 3 inlet} \\ > \text{Point G: } &(M=12.0, \ C=C_{out,3}^{max}=400) && \text{Final wastewater} > \end{aligned}$$ > **Step 5 — Total plant freshwater without reuse:** > > $$L_{fresh} = \sum L_{min,i} = 80{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 125{,}000 = 255{,}000 \ \text{kg/h}$$ > **Step 6 — Verification of total load:** > > $$\Delta m_{total} = 4.0 + 3.0 + 5.0 = 12.0 \ \text{kg/h} \quad \checkmark$$ The **limiting composite curve** is plotted from the vertices A→B→C→D→E→F→G. The *minimum freshwater target* is read off as the slope of the *lowest translated* supply line touching the composite curve at the **water pinch**. ## 3.4 Inlet vs Outlet Concentration — The "Reverse Inequality" Pitfall > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Exam Trap:** Students confuse $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ as both increasing. They are **inlet** and **outlet** of the *water* stream. For an operation that **picks up** contaminant: > > - $C_{in} \leq C_{in}^{max}$ (water entering — *cannot* be dirtier than the limit, hence the upper bound). > - $C_{out} \leq C_{out}^{max}$ (water leaving — *cannot* be dirtier than the limit, hence the upper bound). > > Numerically, $C_{out} > C_{in}$ *always* (water picks up dirt). But both are *bounded above*. Writing "$C_{in}^{max}$ = 200" when you mean $C_{out}^{max}$ is the single most common error in KTU valuation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Single Water-Using Operation ```mermaid flowchart LR A["FRESHWATER OR REUSED STREAM
L kg/h at Cin ppm"] --> B["WATER USING OPERATION WUO
Mass load picked up = dM kg/h"] B --> C["EXIT WATER STREAM
L kg/h at Cout ppm
Cout greater than Cin"] P["PROCESS STREAM
enters with high contaminant"] --> B B --> P2["PROCESS STREAM LEAVES
contaminant reduced"] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600 style C fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style P fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 style P2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Reuse Network Concept ```mermaid flowchart TB FW["FRESHWATER SOURCE
Cin = 0 ppm"]:::source MXR1["MIXING POINT 1"]:::mix WUO1["WUO 1
dM1 kg/h
Cin max to Cout max"]:::op WUO2["WUO 2
dM2 kg/h
Cin max to Cout max"]:::op WUO3["WUO 3
dM3 kg/h
Cin max to Cout max"]:::op MXR2["MIXING POINT 2"]:::mix MXR3["MIXING POINT 3"]:::mix WW["WASTEWATER DISCHARGE
Cout max highest"]:::sink FW --> MXR1 MXR1 -- L1 kg/h --> WUO1 WUO1 -- partially used --> MXR2 MXR2 -- reused --> WUO2 WUO2 -- partially used --> MXR3 MXR3 -- reused --> WUO3 WUO3 --> WW WUO1 -.excess.-> WW WUO2 -.excess.-> WW classDef source fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef sink fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 classDef op fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000 classDef mix fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Limiting Water Profile Construction (Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["Step 1: List all WUOs
Cin max, Cout max, dM"]:::step S2["Step 2: Compute Lmin for each WUO
Lmin = dM / delta C"]:::step S3["Step 3: Order WUOs by
increasing Cout max"]:::step S4["Step 4: Build cumulative mass load
M_k = sum of dM up to k"]:::step S5["Step 5: Plot C vs M
horizontal segments and vertical connectors"]:::step S6["Step 6: Identify water pinch
and minimum freshwater target"]:::step S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 classDef step fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Limiting Composite Curve on C-M Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CM["C-M DIAGRAM FOR 3 WUOs"] direction LR Y["Y AXIS: CONCENTRATION C ppm"]:::axis X["X AXIS: CUMULATIVE MASS LOAD M kg/h"]:::axis SEG2["Segment B to C
WUO 2 horizontal
