KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Define the term "limiting water flowrate" for a water-using operation. List the three parameters required to compute it.** **Model Answer:** The *limiting water flowrate* ($L_{min}$) is the **minimum theoretical water flowrate** required by a water-using operation when operated at the **maximum allowable inlet and outlet contaminant concentrations**. It represents the absolute minimum water demand if the operation is pushed to its thermodynamic/process limits. The three required parameters are: 1. **Maximum inlet concentration** $C_{in}^{max}$ (ppm) 2. **Maximum outlet concentration** $C_{out}^{max}$ (ppm) 3. **Mass load transferred** $\Delta m$ (kg/h) The formula is: $$ L_{min} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max}} $$ (3 marks — definition 1 mark, list 1 mark, formula 1 mark) ### Question A2 — Application `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: A scrubber removes 2 kg/h of H₂S from a natural gas stream. The water inlet must contain at most 20 ppm H₂S and the outlet at most 250 ppm. Calculate the limiting water flowrate.** **Model Answer:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{min} &= \dfrac{\Delta m}{C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max}} \\ &= \dfrac{2 \ \text{kg/h}}{(250 - 20) \ \text{ppm}} \\ &= \dfrac{2}{230} \\ &= 0.008696 \ \text{kg water / kg contaminant} \end{aligned} $$ For a contaminant load of 2 kg/h: $$ L_{min} = \dfrac{2}{250 - 20} \times 10^{6} \ \text{ppm}^{-1} \cdot (\text{kg/h}) $$ Wait — the units require care: $C$ is in **mg/kg = ppm**, $\Delta m$ is in **kg/h**, so $L$ is in **kg water/h**: $$ L_{min} = \dfrac{2 \ \text{kg/h}}{(250 - 20) \times 10^{-6} \ \text{kg contaminant / kg water}} = \dfrac{2}{230 \times 10^{-6}} = 8{,}695.65 \ \text{kg/h} \approx 8.7 \ \text{t/h} $$ (3 marks — 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution with unit conversion, 1 mark final answer with units) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Regulation 2024:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Full internal choice means you may attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. ### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **A process plant has four water-using operations with the following limiting data:** | WUO | $\Delta m$ (kg/h) | $C_{in}^{max}$ (ppm) | $C_{out}^{max}$ (ppm) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 6.0 | 0 | 250 | | 2 | 4.5 | 50 | 150 | | 3 | 8.0 | 100 | 350 | | 4 | 3.5 | 200 | 450 | #### Part (a) — [7 Marks] **(i)** Calculate the limiting water flowrate $L_{min}$ for **each** operation. **(ii)** Arrange the operations in increasing order of $C_{out}^{max}$ and compute the cumulative mass load $M_k$ at each stage. #### Part (b) — [7 Marks] **(i)** Determine the **minimum freshwater requirement** for the plant assuming no reuse (i.e., each operation receives freshwater directly). **(ii)** If perfect reuse is permitted (subject only to $C_{in}^{max}$ constraints), what is the **theoretical minimum freshwater flowrate** using the limiting composite curve? **(iii)** Comment on the percentage reduction in freshwater demand achievable. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **A solvent extraction plant uses water to wash an organic stream. Two water-using operations exist:** | WUO | $\Delta m$ (kg/h) | $C_{in}^{max}$ (ppm) | $C_{out}^{max}$ (ppm) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 5.0 | 0 | 100 | | 2 | 7.5 | 50 | 300 | #### Part (a) — [7 Marks] **(i)** Write the general contaminant mass balance over a single WUO and derive the limiting water flowrate formula. **(ii)** Calculate $L_{min}$ for both WUOs. #### Part (b) — [7 Marks] **(i)** Construct the **limiting composite curve** by computing all C-M diagram vertices. **(ii)** Identify the **water pinch concentration** and justify whether direct freshwater reuse is feasible. --- ### 📘 Complete Model Solution — Question A #### Solution to A(a): > **Step A1 — Compute $L_{min,i}$ for each WUO using** $L_{min} = \Delta m / (C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max})$: > > $$\begin{aligned} > L_{min,1} &= \dfrac{6.0}{250 - 0} \times 10^{6} = \dfrac{6.0}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 24{,}000 \ \text{kg/h} \\ > L_{min,2} &= \dfrac{4.5}{150 - 50} \times 10^{6} = \dfrac{4.5}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 45{,}000 \ \text{kg/h} \\ > L_{min,3} &= \dfrac{8.0}{350 - 100} \times 10^{6} = \dfrac{8.0}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 32{,}000 \ \text{kg/h} \\ > L_{min,4} &= \dfrac{3.5}{450 - 200} \times 10^{6} = \dfrac{3.5}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 14{,}000 \ \text{kg/h} > \end{aligned}$$ > **Step A2 — Order operations by increasing $C_{out}^{max}$:** > > WUO 1 (250) ← WUO 2 (150) ← WUO 4 (450) ← WUO 3 (350) > > Correct ascending order: > > WUO 2 (150) → WUO 1 (250) → WUO 3 (350) → WUO 4 (450) > **Step A3 — Cumulative mass load table:** > > $$\begin{aligned} > M_0 &= 0 \\ > M_1 &= 0 + 4.5 = 4.5 \ \text{kg/h (after WUO 2)} \\ > M_2 &= 4.5 + 6.0 = 10.5 \ \text{kg/h (after WUO 1)} \\ > M_3 &= 10.5 + 8.0 = 18.5 \ \text{kg/h (after WUO 3)} \\ > M_4 &= 18.5 + 3.5 = 22.0 \ \text{kg/h (after WUO 4)} > \end{aligned}$$ (7 marks complete) #### Solution to A(b): > **Step B1 — Total freshwater without reuse:** > > $$L_{fresh,no\,reuse} = \sum L_{min,i} = 24{,}000 + 45{,}000 + 32{,}000 + 14{,}000 = 115{,}000 \ \text{kg/h}$$ > **Step B2 — Total contaminant load:** > > $$\Delta m_{total} = 6.0 + 4.5 + 8.0 + 3.5 = 22.0 \ \text{kg/h}$$ > **Step B3 — Minimum freshwater with reuse (limiting composite curve approach):** > The limiting composite curve has its **highest concentration at the final waste stream** ($C_{out,4}^{max} = 450$ ppm) and **freshwater inlet at 0 ppm**. The pinch corresponds to the minimum slope supply line that just touches the composite curve without crossing it. From the C-M vertices calculated in the example above, the minimum freshwater target is the slope of the supply line from $(M=0, C=0)$ to the lowest point on the composite curve where it can sit tangent. > > At the pinch, the freshwater slope is approximately: > > $$L_{fresh,min} \approx \dfrac{\Delta m_{total}}{C_{out,final}^{max}} = \dfrac{22.0}{450 \times 10^{-6}} = 48{,}889 \ \text{kg/h}$$ > **Step B4 — Percentage reduction:** > > $$\% \text{reduction} = \dfrac{115{,}000 - 48{,}889}{115{,}000} \times 100 \approx 57.5\%$$ (7 marks complete) ### 📘 Complete Model Solution — Question B (Alternative) #### Solution to B(a): > **Step 1 — Mass balance derivation** (as shown in Section 3.1 above) — **3 marks** > > **Step 2 — Calculate $L_{min}$ for both WUOs:** > > $$\begin{aligned} > L_{min,1} &= \dfrac{5.0}{100 - 0} \times 10^{6} = \dfrac{5.0}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 50{,}000 \ \text{kg/h} \\ > L_{min,2} &= \dfrac{7.5}{300 - 50} \times 10^{6} = \dfrac{7.5}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 30{,}000 \ \text{kg/h} > \end{aligned}$$ (7 marks — derivation 3, calculation 4) #### Solution to B(b): > **Step 1 — Order by increasing $C_{out}^{max}$:** > WUO 1 (100 ppm) → WUO 2 (300 ppm) > **Step 2 — Build C-M vertices:** > > $$\begin{aligned} > \text{Point A: } &(0, 0) \\ > \text{Point B: } &(0, C_{in,1}^{max} = 0) \\ > \text{Point C: } &(5.0, C_{out,1}^{max} = 100) \\ > \text{Point D: } &(5.0, C_{in,2}^{max} = 50) \\ > \text{Point E: } &(12.5, C_{out,2}^{max} = 300) > \end{aligned}$$ > **Step 3 — Pinch identification:** The pinch lies between points D and C (concentrations overlap: 50 ≤ 100), so the exit of WUO 1 (100 ppm) can be reused as inlet to WUO 2 (max 50 ppm) only with **freshwater dilution**. The minimum freshwater target is: > > $$L_{fresh,min} = \dfrac{12.5}{300 \times 10^{-6}} = 41{,}667 \ \text{kg/h}$$ > **Step 4 — Direct reuse feasibility:** Not fully feasible because $C_{out,1}^{max}$ (100 ppm) > $C_{in,2}^{max}$ (50 ppm); partial reuse with bypass is required. (7 marks — vertices 3, pinch 2, comment 2) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Conversion on ppm:** 1 ppm = 1 mg/kg = $10^{-6}$ kg/kg. Forgetting this gives answers off by a factor of $10^6$. Always show the unit conversion step. > 2. **Mixing vs Reuse confusion:** Mixing two streams reduces concentration — this is the **whole point** of reuse. State the mixing equation $L_1 C_1 + L_2 C_2 = (L_1 + L_2) C_{mix}$ explicitly. > 3. **Ordering by $C_{out}^{max}$ vs $C_{in}^{max}$:** Must sort by **outlet** concentration for the composite curve. > 4. **Negative driving force:** If $C_{out}^{max} \leq C_{in}^{max}$, the WUO is infeasible — flag this in your answer (rare but a board favorite). > 5. **Missing the vertical drop:** In the C-M diagram, the connection from one WUO outlet to the next inlet is a **vertical line at constant M** — students often draw a diagonal and lose 1–2 marks. ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember ⚡ > **Quick-Fire Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam)** - **Water pinch goal:** Minimize freshwater $L_f$ AND wastewater $L_w$ simultaneously. - **Limiting flowrate formula:** $L_{min} = \Delta m / (C_{out}^{max} - C_{in}^{max})$. Memorize. - **Three mandatory WUO data points:** $\Delta m$, $C_{in}^{max}$, $C_{out}^{max}$. Without all three, no $L_{min}$. - **1 ppm = $10^{-6}$ kg/kg = 1 mg/kg.** Use mass fractions or consistent mass-basis ppm only. - **Composite curve construction:** Sort WUOs by **increasing** $C_{out}^{max}$, then by **increasing** $C_{in}^{max}$ for ties. - **C-M diagram shape:** Horizontal segments = WUOs (mass pickup), vertical segments = mixing/reuse transitions. - **Pinch = the "kink"** where supply line touches the composite curve at the minimum slope. - **Total freshwater no-reuse baseline** = $\sum L_{min,i}$. Always compute this as the upper bound. - **Theoretical minimum freshwater** (perfect reuse) = $\Delta m_{total} / C_{out,final}^{max}$ (often lower bound). - **Wang–Smith (1994) assumption:** Single contaminant; multi-contaminant problems require hierarchical or iterative targeting. - **Water ≠ only freshwater:** Reuse, regeneration, recycle are three distinct strategies — *reuse* means sequential use across operations; *recycle* means return to the same operation; *regeneration* means treatment between uses. - **Real-world constraint:** Corrosion, fouling, biological growth impose *practical* $C_{out}^{max}$ tighter than theoretical. - **KTU Module 3 link to Module 4:** The water network design step (next module) uses these $L_{min}$ values as the basis for Minimum Flowrate Targeting via Water Cascade Analysis.

topics.title: Targeting Maximum Water Reuse for Single Contaminants
Choice of Idealized Reactor Model and Reactor Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Choice of Idealized Reactor Model and Reactor Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Process Integration** and **Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE)**, the **Choice of Idealized Reactor Model** refers to the systematic, quantitative procedure used by chemical engineers to select the most economically and technically suitable reactor type from the three canonical idealized models — the **Batch Reactor (BR)**, **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR / Mixed Flow Reactor / MFR)**, and **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** — to carry out a given homogeneous reaction with maximum **Reactor Performance**.

**Reactor Performance** is formally defined as a multi-parameter construct quantified by three principal metrics:

1. **Conversion ($X_A$)** — the fraction of limiting reactant $A$ consumed inside the reactor.
2. **Selectivity ($\hat{S}$)** — the moles of desired product formed per mole of reactant consumed (especially for multiple reactions).
3. **Space Time ($\tau$)** — the time required to process one reactor volume of feed, given by $\tau = V / \nu_0$, where $V$ is reactor volume and $\nu_0$ is the volumetric feed flow rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For PECHT745, Module 4 emphasizes that the **choice is NOT made by preference but by reaction kinetics (order, endothermic/exothermic), production scale, and downstream separation cost**. The model is *idealized* because it assumes perfect mixing (CSTR) or zero axial mixing (PFR), and these bounds act as the **performance envelope** for any real industrial reactor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a **coffee-roasting café** and want every bean roasted uniformly.

- A **Batch Reactor** is like a **frying pan** — you put all the beans in, roast for a fixed time, and dump them out. Excellent for small quantities and slow reactions, but slow and labor-intensive.
- A **CSTR (Mixed Flow)** is like a **large pot of soup kept boiling and stirred vigorously** — fresh beans enter and mix instantly with beans already inside. Every bean spends a *different* time in the pot, so quality varies.
- A **PFR (Plug Flow)** is like a **conveyor belt passing through an oven** — beans enter in a "plug" and each plug sees a *different* temperature profile along the belt, but within a plug all beans are identical.

> [!NOTE]
> The **PFR gives the highest conversion per unit volume for any positive-order reaction**, while the **CSTR gives the lowest**. The Batch reactor is the *time-domain* equivalent of the PFR and equals its performance when end-time conversion is matched.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | Importance |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Volumetric flow rate | $\nu_0$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Determines throughput |
| Reactor volume | $V$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Capital cost driver |
| Space time | $\tau$ | $\text{s}$ | Performance measure |
| Space velocity | $SV$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Inverse of $\tau$ |
| Recycle ratio | $R$ | dimensionless | Hybrid reactor design |
| Heat of reaction | $\Delta H_R$ | $\text{kJ/kmol}$ | Selects adiabatic vs. non-adiabatic |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decision Logic for Choosing an Idealized Reactor

The selection of an idealized reactor follows a strict **decision tree** based on reaction kinetics, production scale, and operating philosophy.

### Step 1 — Classify the Production Scale

- **Small scale (high-value, low-volume)** → **Batch Reactor**
  - Pharmaceuticals, fine chemicals, polymers, fermentation
- **Large scale (high-volume, continuous)** → **Flow Reactor (CSTR or PFR)**

### Step 2 — Classify the Reaction Kinetics

- **Positive-order reactions (n > 0)** → **PFR is best** (volume saving).
- **Zero-order reactions** → **PFR and CSTR give identical volume**.
- **Negative-order or autocatalytic reactions** → **CSTR is best** (well-mixed environment essential for catalyst contact).
- **Autocatalytic reactions ($\text{A} \to \text{P}$ where P catalyzes)** → **CSTR (or recycle PFR)** is required because the catalyst must be present from the start.

### Step 3 — Classify the Operating Mode

- **Adiabatic exothermic with sensitive temperature control** → **CSTR** (stability of operating point at steady state).
- **Highly endothermic** → **PFR** (better heat integration along length).

### Step 4 — Consider Selectivity (Multiple Reactions)

For $\text{A} \xrightarrow{k_1} \text{P (desired)} \xrightarrow{k_2} \text{Q (unwanted)}$:
- **If $k_1 < k_2$** (desired reaction is slower) → use **CSTR** to keep $[A]$ high and $[P]$ low, suppressing side reaction.
- **If $k_1 > k_2$** → use **PFR** to maximize exposure of $A$ in early sections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these choices?** Higher reactant concentration in a PFR early in the reactor length drives fast reactions, while the well-mixed low concentration in a CSTR suppresses consecutive side reactions — this is the heart of the Levenspiel *Product Distribution* charts.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table contains **all** the high-yield equations tested in KTU exams for Module 4. **Note:** All $\vert$ symbols use `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Reactor Type | Design Equation | Form of $X_A$ |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Batch Reactor (BR)** | $t = N_{A0} \displaystyle\int_0^{X_{Af}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | Time-domain integral |
| 2 | **PFR** | $\tau = C_{A0} \displaystyle\int_0^{X_{Af}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | Spatial integral |
| 3 | **CSTR (Single)** | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0}\,(X_{Af} - 0)}{-r_{Af}}$ | Algebraic |
| 4 | **CSTRs in series ($N$ tanks)** | $\tau_i = \dfrac{C_{A0}\,(X_{A,i} - X_{A,i-1})}{-r_{A,i}}$ | Sequential algebraic |
| 5 | **PFR with Recycle** | $\tau = (1+R)\,C_{A0} \displaystyle\int_{X_{Af}}^{X_{A0}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | Integral with $(1+R)$ factor |
| 6 | **CSTR with Recycle** | $\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0}\,X_{Af}\,(1+R)}{-r_{A,\text{eff}}}$ | Algebraic with $(1+R)$ |
| 7 | **Space Velocity** | $SV = \dfrac{1}{\tau} = \dfrac{\nu_0}{V}$ | Reciprocal of $\tau$ |
| 8 | **1st Order CSTR** | $\tau = \dfrac{X_A}{k\,(1-X_A)}$ | Closed form |
| 9 | **1st Order PFR** | $\tau = -\dfrac{\ln(1-X_A)}{k}$ | Closed form |
| 10 | **2nd Order CSTR** | $\tau = \dfrac{X_A^2}{k\,C_{A0}\,(1-X_A)}$ | Closed form |
| 11 | **2nd Order PFR** | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k\,C_{A0}}\!\left(\dfrac{X_A}{1-X_A}\right)$ | Closed form |
| 12 | **Levenspiel PFR volume** | $V_{PFR} = \nu_0\,C_{A0}\!\displaystyle\int_0^{X_A}\!\dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$ | $\nu_0 \cdot \tau$ |
| 13 | **Autocatalytic CSTR** | $\tau_{\min} = \dfrac{1}{k\,C_{A0}}\!\left(\dfrac{C_{P0}}{C_{A0}}\right)$ | Occurs at optimum $X_A$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (Levenspiel):** For any **positive-order single reaction**, the **volume ratio** is bounded as
>
> $$\dfrac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} \ge 1$$
>
> with **equality only at $X_A = 0$ or for zero-order reactions**. The ratio diverges as $X_A \to 1$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Petrochemical cracking furnaces** — modeled as PFRs with large length-to-diameter ratio.
2. **Ammonia synthesis loops** — operate as recycle reactors (mix of PFR + recycle) to control conversion per pass and prevent equilibrium limitations.
3. **SABIC polymer plants** — use CSTRs in series to narrow the molecular weight distribution (closer to batch quality) while maintaining continuous operation.
4. **Bioreactors (fermentation)** — operated as CSTRs for aerobic fermentations due to oxygen transfer uniformity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why PFR Volume is Less Than or Equal to CSTR Volume (Positive-Order Reactions)

**Goal:** Show mathematically that $V_{PFR} \le V_{CSTR}$ for any $n > 0$ reaction at equal feed and equal exit conversion.

**Starting Point — CSTR Design Equation:**

For a single CSTR at steady state, a mole balance on reactant $A$ over the control volume $V$ gives:

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A\,V = 0$$

Substituting $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ and $r_A = -(-r_A)$:

$$F_{A0} - F_{A0}(1 - X_A) - (-r_A)\,V = 0$$

This simplifies to the **canonical CSTR design equation**:

$$\tau_{CSTR} = \dfrac{C_{A0}\,X_A}{-r_A} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_{CSTR} = \nu_0\,C_{A0}\,\dfrac{X_A}{-r_A}$$

**Starting Point — PFR Design Equation:**

For a PFR, the differential mole balance on a slice of volume $dV$ gives:

$$F_{A0}\,dX_A = -r_A\,dV$$

Separating variables and integrating from inlet ($X_A=0$) to outlet ($X_A = X_{Af}$):

$$V_{PFR} = \nu_0\,C_{A0} \int_0^{X_{Af}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$

**Comparison via the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals:**

By the **Mean Value Theorem for Integrals**, there exists some $X_A^* \in [0, X_{Af}]$ such that:

$$\int_0^{X_{Af}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A} = \dfrac{X_{Af}}{-r_A(X_A^*)}$$

If $-r_A$ is a **monotonically decreasing** function of $X_A$ (i.e., the rate slows as reactant is consumed — true for all $n \ge 0$ reactions), then:

$$-r_A(X_A^*) \;\ge\; -r_A(X_{Af})$$

Therefore:

$$V_{PFR} = \nu_0\,C_{A0}\,\dfrac{X_{Af}}{-r_A(X_A^*)} \;\le\; \nu_0\,C_{A0}\,\dfrac{X_{Af}}{-r_A(X_{Af})} = V_{CSTR}$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\;V_{PFR} \;\le\; V_{CSTR}\;}$$

with equality only when $-r_A$ is **independent of $X_A$** (i.e., **zero-order kinetics**).

## 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Number of CSTRs in Series to Mimic a PFR

**Physical Premise:** As $N \to \infty$, $N$-CSTRs-in-series behaves like a PFR.

For a **first-order irreversible reaction** $\text{A} \to \text{B}$ with $k$ as the rate constant:

The exit concentration from $N$ equal-volume CSTRs in series is given by:

$$\dfrac{C_{A,N}}{C_{A0}} = \left(\dfrac{1}{1 + k\tau_i}\right)^{N}$$

where $\tau_i$ is the space time of each individual tank. The total space time is $\tau_N = N\tau_i$.

For a **PFR** at the same total space time $\tau_N$:

$$\dfrac{C_{A,PFR}}{C_{A0}} = e^{-k\tau_N}$$

For the $N$-CSTR system to **mimic** the PFR, we need:

$$\left(1 + k\tau_i\right)^{-N} = e^{-k\tau_N}$$

Taking natural logarithms of both sides:

$$-N\,\ln(1 + k\tau_i) = -k\tau_N$$

Using the relation $\tau_N = N\tau_i$:

$$N\,\ln(1 + k\tau_i) = k\,N\tau_i \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \ln(1 + k\tau_i) = k\tau_i$$

The **only solution** to this transcendental equation is $k\tau_i = 0$, which would mean $\tau_N = 0$ (no reaction).

Therefore, **N-CSTRs in series can NEVER exactly equal a PFR**, but as $N \to \infty$, $k\tau_i \to 0$ and we recover the PFR limit. Practically, **$N \ge 4$ CSTRs in series gives a volume within 5% of a PFR** for first-order reactions.

## 3.3 Derivation: PFR with Recycle — Effective Reactor Performance

**Physical Premise:** The exit stream of a PFR is split; a fraction $R$ is recycled to the inlet and mixed with the fresh feed.

Let:
- $F_{A0}$ = molar feed rate of fresh reactant,
- $F_{A0}' = (1+R)\,F_{A0}$ = molar flow rate entering the reactor (fresh + recycle),
- $X_{A0}'$ = conversion of the mixed stream at reactor inlet.

From the recycle stream definition:

$$X_{A0}' = \dfrac{X_{Af}}{1 + R} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad X_{A0}' = \dfrac{F_{A0}\,X_{Af}}{F_{A0}(1+R)} = \dfrac{X_{Af}}{1+R}$$

The **differential mole balance** on the PFR with recycle becomes:

$$F_{A0}'\,dX_A = -r_A\,dV$$

$$F_{A0}(1+R)\,dX_A = -r_A\,dV$$

Integrating from reactor inlet ($X_A = X_{A0}'$) to outlet ($X_A = X_{Af}$):

$$V = \nu_0\,C_{A0}(1+R) \int_{X_{A0}'}^{X_{Af}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$

Substituting $X_{A0}' = X_{Af}/(1+R)$:

$$V = \nu_0\,C_{A0}(1+R) \int_{X_{Af}/(1+R)}^{X_{Af}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$

This is the **canonical recycle PFR equation**. The **space time** is:

$$\boxed{\;\tau = (1+R)\,C_{A0} \int_{X_{Af}/(1+R)}^{X_{Af}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}\;}$$

**Special Case: $R \to \infty$ (Infinite Recycle):**
The inlet conversion approaches $X_{A0}' = X_{Af}/(1+R) \to 0$ for finite $X_{Af}$. The lower integration limit vanishes, but the $(1+R)$ factor remains. However, in the limit $R \to \infty$, the entire reactor behaves as a **CSTR** because every point inside the reactor sees the same composition as the outlet.

**Special Case: $R = 0$ (No Recycle):**
The equation reduces to the standard PFR design equation, as expected.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** The liquid-phase elementary reaction $\text{A} \to \text{B}$ follows **second-order kinetics** with $k = 0.5\ \text{L/(mol·min)}$ at 350 K. The feed has $C_{A0} = 2\ \text{mol/L}$ and $\nu_0 = 10\ \text{L/min}$. Compute the **reactor volume** needed to achieve $X_A = 0.80$ in (a) a PFR and (b) a single CSTR.

**Part (a) — PFR Volume:**

For second-order kinetics, $-r_A = k\,C_A^2 = k\,C_{A0}^2(1 - X_A)^2$.

$$V_{PFR} = \nu_0\,C_{A0} \int_0^{0.80} \dfrac{dX_A}{k\,C_{A0}^2(1-X_A)^2}$$

$$V_{PFR} = \dfrac{\nu_0}{k\,C_{A0}} \int_0^{0.80} \dfrac{dX_A}{(1-X_A)^2}$$

The integral evaluates to:

$$\int_0^{0.80} \dfrac{dX_A}{(1-X_A)^2} = \left[\dfrac{1}{1-X_A}\right]_0^{0.80} = \dfrac{1}{0.20} - \dfrac{1}{1.00} = 5 - 1 = 4$$

Therefore:

$$V_{PFR} = \dfrac{10}{0.5 \times 2} \times 4 = 10 \times 4 = 40\ \text{L}$$

**Part (b) — CSTR Volume:**

For a CSTR:

$$V_{CSTR} = \dfrac{\nu_0\,C_{A0}\,X_A}{-r_{A,\text{exit}}} = \dfrac{\nu_0\,C_{A0}\,X_A}{k\,C_{A0}^2(1-X_A)^2}$$

Substituting the numbers:

$$V_{CSTR} = \dfrac{10 \times 2 \times 0.80}{0.5 \times 4 \times (0.20)^2} = \dfrac{16}{0.5 \times 4 \times 0.04} = \dfrac{16}{0.08} = 200\ \text{L}$$

**Comparison:**

$$\dfrac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} = \dfrac{200}{40} = 5$$

The CSTR requires **5 times** the volume of the PFR for the same conversion — a classic result that the PFR is the **economically preferred** choice for positive-order reactions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reactor Selection Decision Tree (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Identify Reaction] --> nodeB{Production Scale?}
    nodeB -->|Small Volume, High Value| nodeC[Choose Batch Reactor]
    nodeB -->|Large Volume, Continuous| nodeD{Number of Phases?}
    nodeD -->|Single Phase| nodeE{Reaction Order?}
    nodeD -->|Gas-Liquid, Gas-Solid| nodeF[Use CSTR or Packed Bed]
    nodeE -->|Positive Order n greater than 0| nodeG[Choose PFR]
    nodeE -->|Zero Order| nodeH[PFR equals CSTR]
    nodeE -->|Negative Order| nodeI[Choose CSTR]
    nodeE -->|Autocatalytic| nodeJ[Choose CSTR or Recycle PFR]
    nodeG --> nodeK{Heat Removal Critical?}
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeK -->|Yes, Exothermic Sensitive| nodeL[Use CSTR for Stability]
    nodeK -->|No, Endothermic| nodeM[Use PFR with Heat Integration]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Verify with Pilot Plant Data]
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeF --> nodeN
    nodeH --> nodeN
    nodeC --> nodeN
    nodeJ --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeEnd[Final Reactor Selection]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Reactor Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FreshFeed[Fresh Feed Stream A plus B] --> MixerUnit{Mixer Junction}
    RecycleStream[Recycled Outlet Fraction] --> MixerUnit
    MixerUnit --> ReactorInlet[Combined Inlet to Reactor]
    ReactorInlet --> ReactorBlock[Idealized Reactor: PFR or CSTR or BR]
    ReactorBlock --> OutletStream[Outlet Stream to Downstream]
    OutletStream --> SplitterUnit{Outlet Splitter}
    SplitterUnit --> ProductPath[Product Goes to Separation]
    SplitterUnit --> RecycleStream
    ReactorBlock --> ControlLoop[Temperature and Pressure Sensors]
    ControlLoop --> ReactorBlock
```

## 4.3 Performance Comparison Bar Chart (Mermaid Visualization)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Reactor Volume Required for XA equals 0.80 (Second Order Reaction)"
    x-axis ["PFR", "1 CSTR", "2 CSTRs in Series", "3 CSTRs in Series", "4 CSTRs in Series"]
    y-axis "Volume in Liters" 0 --> 250
    bar [40, 200, 116, 88, 76]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the chart:** As the number of CSTRs in series increases, the total volume required to achieve the same conversion **decreases monotonically**, asymptotically approaching the PFR value of 40 L. This visually confirms the theoretical claim that **$N$-CSTRs-in-series $\to$ PFR as $N \to \infty$**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Q: Define space time and space velocity. How are they related to reactor performance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Space time ($\tau$)** is defined as the time required to process one reactor volume of feed at inlet conditions, mathematically expressed as $\tau = V/\nu_0$ where $V$ is the reactor volume and $\nu_0$ is the volumetric feed flow rate. (1 mark)
- **Space velocity ($SV$)** is the reciprocal of space time, $SV = 1/\tau = \nu_0/V$, measured in $\text{s}^{-1}$ or $\text{hr}^{-1}$. (1 mark)
- **Relation to reactor performance:** A **smaller $\tau$** (and hence **larger $SV$**) at a given conversion indicates a **more efficient reactor** (smaller volume for the same throughput). It is a direct measure of reactor productivity. (1 mark)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: For a positive-order reaction, which idealized reactor requires the smallest volume for a given conversion? Justify in one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** requires the smallest volume for a given conversion and feed condition for any **positive-order single reaction** ($n > 0$). (2 marks)

**Justification:** This is because the rate of reaction $-r_A$ in a PFR is at its highest value at the inlet and decreases gradually along the length, whereas in a CSTR, $-r_A$ is at its lowest (exit) value throughout the entire volume. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Derive the design equation for a **PFR with recycle** and show that as the recycle ratio $R \to \infty$, the PFR with recycle behaves as a CSTR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A first-order liquid-phase reaction $\text{A} \to \text{B}$ with $k = 0.25\ \text{min}^{-1}$ is to be carried out in a PFR. The feed flow rate is $50\ \text{L/min}$ with $C_{A0} = 1.5\ \text{mol/L}$. Find the reactor volume required to achieve 75% conversion. If the same conversion is desired in a CSTR, find the volume. Compare the volumes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1 — System Definition:**
Let the fresh molar feed rate of $A$ be $F_{A0}$ and the recycle ratio be $R = F_{R}/F_{A0}$ where $F_R$ is the recycle molar flow.

**Step 2 — Mixed Stream Inlet to Reactor:**
Total molar flow entering the reactor: $F_{A0}' = F_{A0} + F_R = F_{A0}(1+R)$.

Inlet conversion of the mixed stream: $X_{A0}' = X_{Af}/(1+R)$. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Differential Mole Balance on PFR:**
$$F_{A0}'\,dX_A = -r_A\,dV \;\Longrightarrow\; F_{A0}(1+R)\,dX_A = -r_A\,dV$$ (2 marks)

**Step 4 — Integration:**
$$V = F_{A0}(1+R) \int_{X_{A0}'}^{X_{Af}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A} = \nu_0\,C_{A0}(1+R)\int_{X_{Af}/(1+R)}^{X_{Af}} \dfrac{dX_A}{-r_A}$$ (2 marks)

**Step 5 — Limit $R \to \infty$:**
As $R \to \infty$, the lower limit $X_{Af}/(1+R) \to 0$, but the upper limit $X_{Af}$ remains finite. The factor $(1+R)$ in front compensates. In the limit, the composition throughout the reactor becomes uniform at the outlet value, which is the **defining characteristic of a CSTR**. (2 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**PFR Volume — First-Order Reaction:**
$$V_{PFR} = \dfrac{\nu_0}{k}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-X_A}\right) = \dfrac{50}{0.25}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1-0.75}\right) = 200 \times \ln(4) = 200 \times 1.3863$$ (3 marks)

$$V_{PFR} = 277.26\ \text{L} \approx 277.3\ \text{L}$$ (1 mark)

**CSTR Volume — First-Order Reaction:**
$$V_{CSTR} = \dfrac{\nu_0\,C_{A0}\,X_A}{k\,C_{A0}(1-X_A)} = \dfrac{\nu_0\,X_A}{k(1-X_A)} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.75}{0.25 \times 0.25} = \dfrac{37.5}{0.0625} = 600\ \text{L}$$ (2 marks)

**Comparison:**
$$\dfrac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} = \dfrac{600}{277.3} \approx 2.16$$ (1 mark)

The CSTR requires **2.16 times** the volume of the PFR for the same conversion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **concept of recycle in a reactor** and state **two industrial situations** where recycle reactors are preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A second-order reaction $2\text{A} \to \text{B}$ with $k = 0.08\ \text{L/(mol·min)}$ is to be carried out in a PFR. Calculate the volume required for 90% conversion of a feed with $C_{A0} = 1.0\ \text{mol/L}$ and $\nu_0 = 25\ \text{L/min}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Sketch and Concept (4 marks):**
In a recycle reactor, a portion of the **outlet stream** is returned and mixed with the **fresh feed** at the reactor inlet. The recycle ratio $R$ is defined as:

$$R = \dfrac{\text{Volume of stream recycled}}{\text{Volume of fresh feed}}$$

**Industrial Situations (3 marks):**
1. **Reactions limited by equilibrium conversion** (e.g., ammonia synthesis, methanol synthesis) — recycle of unreacted gases improves overall conversion.
2. **Autocatalytic reactions** (e.g., fermentation, enzyme reactions) — product $P$ acts as a catalyst; recycling part of the product stream maintains the catalytic activity throughout the reactor.
3. *(Bonus)* Exothermic reactions with high per-pass conversion → recycling controls temperature rise and selectivity.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

For a second-order reaction $-r_A = k\,C_A^2 = k\,C_{A0}^2(1 - X_A)^2$:

$$V_{PFR} = \nu_0\,C_{A0} \int_0^{0.90} \dfrac{dX_A}{k\,C_{A0}^2(1-X_A)^2}$$ (2 marks)

$$V_{PFR} = \dfrac{\nu_0}{k\,C_{A0}} \int_0^{0.90} \dfrac{dX_A}{(1-X_A)^2}$$ (2 marks)

The integral evaluates to:

$$\int_0^{0.90} \dfrac{dX_A}{(1-X_A)^2} = \left[\dfrac{1}{1-X_A}\right]_0^{0.90} = \dfrac{1}{0.10} - \dfrac{1}{1.00} = 10 - 1 = 9$$ (2 marks)

Substituting the values:

$$V_{PFR} = \dfrac{25}{0.08 \times 1.0} \times 9 = 312.5 \times 9 = 2812.5\ \text{L} \approx 2813\ \text{L}$$ (1 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Sign error in $-r_A$:** Students frequently write $-r_A = -k C_A^2$ which gives negative volume. Always retain $-r_A$ in the **denominator** of the design equation — it is defined as the **rate of disappearance** and is positive.
> 2. **Confusing space time $\tau$ with residence time $t$:** In a CSTR, $\tau = t$ (the mean residence time). In a PFR, $\tau = V/\nu_0$ but the **residence time distribution is not constant**.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units of $k$:** When using $k$ in $\text{L/(mol·s)}$ and feed in $\text{mol/L}$, volumes come out in litres. Mixing units is a top reason for losing 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Improper limits for CSTRs in series:** When $N$ tanks are used, the design equation must be applied **sequentially** — solve tank 1, use its exit as the inlet of tank 2, and so on. Students often apply the single-CSTR formula to the **total** $X_A$, which is **wrong**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **PFR < CSTR volume** for any **positive-order** single reaction; the gap **widens** as $X_A \to 1$.
- [ ] For **zero-order** reactions, $V_{PFR} = V_{CSTR}$ (rate is independent of concentration).
- [ ] For **negative-order or autocatalytic** reactions, **CSTR is the smallest-volume choice**.
- [ ] The **space time** $\tau = V/\nu_0$ is the fundamental performance metric — smaller $\tau$ means better performance for a given conversion.
- [ ] **N-CSTRs in series** approximate a PFR; the approximation improves with $N$ and is **exact only as $N \to \infty$**.
- [ ] The **recycle ratio $R$** acts as a *knob* that smoothly converts a PFR into a CSTR — at $R = 0$ we get PFR, at $R \to \infty$ we get CSTR.
- [ ] **Selectivity** considerations: PFR favors **fast primary reactions**; CSTR favors **slow primary reactions** with consecutive side products.
- [ ] Always quote the **kinetic order** when comparing reactors — saying "PFR is better" without specifying order will lose marks in KTU exams.
- [ ] The **Levenspiel plot** of $\left(\dfrac{1}{-r_A}\right)$ vs $X_A$ is a **graphical** way to find PFR volume (area under curve) and CSTR volume (rectangle to exit point). The **rectangle is always larger than the area for decreasing-rate reactions**.
- [ ] For **gas-phase reactions with changing molar flow**, account for the $\varepsilon$ (expansion factor) parameter in all design equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactor Configurations: Temperature Control
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reactor Configurations: Temperature Control

# Reactor Configurations: Temperature Control

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reactor Temperature Control** in process integration refers to the strategic manipulation of thermal conditions within a chemical reactor network to maximize selectivity, conversion, and yield while ensuring safe operation. It is the deliberate coupling of heat-transfer equipment (intercoolers, interheaters, cold-shots, hot-shots, and heat exchangers) with reactor trains — CSTRs, PFRs, recycle loops, or hybrid networks — to drive the heat of reaction along a path that aligns the *rate–selectivity–equilibrium* trade-off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The focus is on understanding how *reactor network configuration* (number, type, sequence, and connectivity of reactors) combined with *heat-integration schemes* (utilizing hot/cold streams within the reactor section) can be systematically optimized to improve overall plant economics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are cooking a complicated dish — say, a temper-sensitive curry. If you throw all ingredients into a single massive pot and crank the heat to maximum, some spices will burn (decomposition), the meat will overcook on the outside while remaining raw inside, and the flavor profile will be ruined. Now imagine instead a *process line* of smaller pots: the first pot is on high heat to sear the meat, the second is on a gentle simmer to allow collagen to break down, and the third is cooled to let the spices release their aroma without burning. You have **engineered the temperature trajectory** through the "reactor train."

A chemical reactor train works the same way. A **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** operates at the *exit* temperature (uniform), while a **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** operates along a *spatial temperature profile*. By arranging them in series with inter-stage heat exchange, the engineer effectively "programs" the reaction mixture to experience the ideal temperature at every conversion point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** *Reactor temperature control is not about holding a constant temperature — it is about engineering a temperature-vs-conversion trajectory that maximizes the global process objective function.*

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Reference State:** $T_0 = 298.15\ \text{K}$ and $P_0 = 1\ \text{atm}$
- **Activation Energy ($E$):** Typically in the range of $\mathbf{40\ \text{to}\ 200\ \text{kJ/mol}}$
- **Heat of Reaction ($-\Delta H_r$):** Exothermic values range from $\mathbf{30\ \text{to}\ 150\ \text{kJ/mol}}$ for industrial reactions
- **Adiabatic Temperature Rise ($\Delta T_{ad}$):** Defined as $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)C_{A0}}{\rho C_p}$
- **Arrhenius Pre-exponential Factor ($A$):** Units depend on reaction order

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 The Three Pillars of Reactor Temperature Control

1. **Thermodynamic Constraint** (Equilibrium): The reversible-equilibrium conversion $X_e(T)$ decreases with temperature for exothermic reactions.
2. **Kinetic Driver** (Rate): The forward reaction rate $r_f$ generally *increases* with temperature per Arrhenius behavior.
3. **Selectivity Criterion** (Yield): For complex networks (parallel/consecutive), the desired/undesired rate ratio has a temperature-dependent optimum.

> [!TIP]
> For an **exothermic, reversible** reaction, a *high* temperature increases rate but decreases equilibrium conversion. The **Optimum Temperature Progression (OTP)** is the locus where these two opposing effects yield maximum instantaneous rate.

### 2.1.2 Configuration Taxonomy

| Configuration | Temperature Profile | Reactor Type | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Adiabatic CSTR** | Single uniform $T$ (highest of train) | CSTR | Strongly exothermic, low sensitivity |
| **Adiabatic PFR** | Monotonic $T(X)$ along length | PFR | Moderate $\Delta T_{ad}$ |
| **PFR with interstage cooling** | Stepwise decreasing $T$ profile | PFR-Cascade | High $\Delta T_{ad}$ exothermic systems |
| **Cold-Shot Reactor Train** | First reactor hottest, others stepped down | Multi-bed adiabatic | Sulfuric acid, ammonia, methanol synthesis |
| **CSTR Cascade with intercoolers** | Stepwise $T$ profile approaching $T_{opt}$ | CSTR-Cascade | Polymerization, liquid-phase exothermic |
| **Autothermal Reactor (with feed preheat)** | Self-sustaining after ignition | PFR/CSTR | Methane reforming, partial oxidation |
| **PFR with wall cooling / jacketed** | $T(r,z)$ distribution | Tubular | Highly exothermic, narrow temperature tolerance |

### 2.1.3 Operating Modes

- **Adiabatic Operation:** $\sum Q_i = 0$. No heat exchange with surroundings. The temperature rise is governed entirely by conversion and heat of reaction.
- **Isothermal Operation:** Heat is added/removed to maintain $T = T_{set}$ throughout. Achieved via large heat-transfer surface area, internal coils, or large diluent/mass flow.
- **Non-Adiabatic (Polytropic):** $Q \ne 0$ but the temperature trajectory is not held constant. A compromise between rate and equilibrium.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles the **high-yield formulas** most frequently tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations for this topic. All symbols are in standard SI units unless stated.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arrhenius Rate Law | $k = A\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E}{RT}\right)$ | $A$ = pre-exponential factor |
| 2 | Energy Balance — CSTR (adiabatic) | $T - T_0 = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)X}{\sum \theta_i C_{p_i}}$ | $T$ is the exit (and inside) temperature |
| 3 | Adiabatic Temperature Rise | $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)C_{A0}}{\rho C_p}$ | For a single-component feed |
| 4 | Energy Balance — PFR (adiabatic) | $\dfrac{dT}{dX} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{ad}\!\left(\dfrac{r_A}{-r_{A,ref}}\right)}{1 + \varepsilon X}\cdot\dfrac{C_{A0}}{C_A}$ | Coupled ODE with conversion |
| 5 | Optimum Temperature (exothermic reversible) | $T_{opt} = \dfrac{T_e}{1 + \dfrac{RT_e}{E}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_e}{1 - X_e}\cdot\dfrac{1-X}{X}\right)}$ | For $n$-th order kinetics |
| 6 | Levenspiel Plot (1st order, adiabatic) | $\dfrac{T - T_0}{X} = \Delta T_{ad}$ | Slope of the operating line |
| 7 | Multiple Steady States (CSTR) | $\dfrac{Q_r}{V C_A} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)k_0\exp(-E/RT)}{\rho C_p(T - T_c)}$ | Intersection of generation/removal curves |
| 8 | Hot Spot Temperature (Wall-cooled PFR) | $T_{max} = T_w + (T_{ad} - T_w)\exp(-\text{Da}_{wall})$ | Da-Damköhler number coupling |
| 9 | Damköhler Number | $\text{Da} = \dfrac{r_{A,ref}\cdot V}{F_{A0}}$ | Characterizes residence time vs. reaction |
| 10 | Reactor Network Conversion (Series) | $\dfrac{V_{tot}}{F_{A0}} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \int_{X_{i-1}}^{X_i}\dfrac{dX}{-r_A(X,T_i)}$ | Cumulative residence time |
| 11 | Interstage Heat Duty | $Q_{int} = F_t \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | For sensible heat balancing |
| 12 | Equilibrium Constant (Van't Hoff) | $\ln K = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}$ | Decreases with $T$ for exothermic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state the assumptions before writing a formula on the exam. Common assumptions: ideal gas, constant $C_p$, constant $\Delta H_r$ over the temperature range, single reaction.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Adiabatic Energy Balance for a CSTR

### Starting Premise
For a single, irreversible, first-order reaction $A \to B$ occurring in a CSTR operating **adiabatically**, derive the relationship between exit temperature $T$ and conversion $X$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Molar Balance on Species A (steady-state CSTR):**

$$F_{A0} - F_A + r_A V = 0$$

Since $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$ and $r_A = -k C_A$, this becomes:

$$F_{A0}X = k C_A V = k C_{A0}(1 - X)V$$

This is the standard CSTR design equation.

**Step 2 — Energy Balance on the CSTR (steady-state, adiabatic, $Q = 0$):**

$$\sum F_i C_{p,i}(T_0 - T) + (-\Delta H_r) F_{A0} X = 0$$

Rearranging (the reactive term supplies heat that raises the outlet stream):

$$F_{A0}(-\Delta H_r) X = F_t \cdot C_p \cdot (T - T_0)$$

**Step 3 — Introducing the Adiabatic Temperature Rise $\Delta T_{ad}$:**

For a single-component feed at $C_{A0}$ with $F_t \approx F_{A0}$ (dilute solution approximation):

$$\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r) C_{A0}}{\rho C_p} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r) F_{A0}}{F_t C_p}$$

**Step 4 — Final Form (Substitution):**

$$T - T_0 = \Delta T_{ad} \cdot X$$

or equivalently:

$$X = \dfrac{T - T_0}{\Delta T_{ad}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The operating line is a *straight line* on the $T$–$X$ diagram, from $(0, T_0)$ to $(1, T_0 + \Delta T_{ad})$. This is the famous **Levenspiel operating line** for adiabatic CSTRs.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimum Temperature Progression (OTP)

### Starting Premise
For a reversible exothermic reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$ with order $n$ on the forward and $m$ on the reverse, find the temperature $T_{opt}$ that maximizes the net forward rate at conversion $X$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Net Rate Expression:**

$$r_{net} = k_f C_A^n - k_r C_B^m = k_f\!\left(C_A^n - \dfrac{C_B^m}{K}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. Temperature at Fixed X:**

To find the maximum, set $\left(\dfrac{\partial r_{net}}{\partial T}\right)_{X} = 0$:

$$0 = \dfrac{\partial k_f}{\partial T} C_A^n - \dfrac{\partial}{\partial T}\!\left(\dfrac{k_f}{K}\right) C_B^m$$

**Step 3 — Use Arrhenius and Van't Hoff Dependencies:**

$$\dfrac{\partial k_f}{\partial T} = \dfrac{E_f}{RT^2} k_f, \qquad \dfrac{\partial}{\partial T}\!\left(\dfrac{k_f}{K}\right) = \dfrac{k_f}{K}\cdot\dfrac{E_f - \Delta H_r}{RT^2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Resulting Equation:**

After cancellation of common factors, the result is:

$$\dfrac{E_f}{RT_{opt}^2} = \dfrac{(E_f - \Delta H_r)}{RT_{opt}^2}\cdot\dfrac{C_B^m / K}{C_A^n - C_B^m / K}$$

For the special case $m = n = 1$ and $C_{A0} = C_{B0}$ (binary stoichiometry):

$$\boxed{T_{opt}(X) = \dfrac{T_e(X)}{1 + \dfrac{R\,T_e(X)}{E}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - X}{X}\right)}}$$

where $T_e(X)$ is the equilibrium temperature at conversion $X$ obtained by inverting the equilibrium relation.

> **Insight:** The OTP is a *decreasing* function of $X$ for exothermic reactions. This is why interstage cooling (PFR cascade) outperforms a single adiabatic reactor — the temperature profile can be made to *track* the OTP curve.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Multiple Steady States in an Adiabatic CSTR

### Starting Premise
A first-order exothermic reaction $A \to B$ is carried out in an adiabatic CSTR with a coolant at $T_c$. Show that under certain conditions, three steady states exist.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Combine Material and Energy Balances:**

The CSTR design equation gives:

$$\tau = \dfrac{C_{A0}X}{k C_{A0}(1 - X)} = \dfrac{X}{k(1 - X)}$$

The adiabatic operating line gives $T = T_0 + \Delta T_{ad} X$, so:

$$k(T) = A\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E}{R(T_0 + \Delta T_{ad} X)}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Implicit Relation for X:**

$$\tau = \dfrac{X}{A(1 - X)\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E}{R(T_0 + \Delta T_{ad} X)}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — S-shaped Generation Curve:**

When the right-hand side (heat generation rate) and left-hand side (residence-time demand) are plotted, the curve becomes **S-shaped** because $k(T)$ is exponential while the algebraic prefactor is rational.

**Step 4 — Stability Analysis:**

- **Lower steady state** (low $X$, low $T$): stable, kinetics-limited.
- **Middle steady state:** unstable, must be avoided.
- **Upper steady state** (high $X$, high $T$): stable, but often near thermal runaway.

**Step 5 — The Hagan-Sussman-Wiesz Criterion** (for $\beta$–$\kappa$ phase space):

$$\beta = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r) C_{A0}}{\rho C_p T_0}, \qquad \kappa = \dfrac{E}{RT_0}$$

Multiple steady states appear when:

$$\beta \kappa > 4(e^2 + 2e + 1) \approx 17.5$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Configuration Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Decision Matrix Below is essential for KTU 2024 problems involving reactor network selection under heat-integration constraints.**

| Scenario | Best Configuration | Key Trade-Off | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low $\Delta T_{ad}$ (~$<$ 30 K), reversible | Single adiabatic CSTR or PFR | Capital cost vs. conversion | Hydrocarbon isomerization |
| Moderate $\Delta T_{ad}$ (30–150 K) | PFR with 2–3 interstage coolers | Higher $\Delta T$ allowed per stage | Methanol synthesis |
| High $\Delta T_{ad}$ (~$>$ 150 K) | Cold-shot train or multi-bed | Catalyst dilution by cold shot | Ammonia, sulfuric acid |
| Strongly exothermic + side reactions | PFR with wall cooling + axial mixing | Hot spot suppression | Ethylene oxidation, partial oxidation |
| Autothermal required | Recycle with feed-effluent HX | Operability vs. sensitivity | Methane steam reforming |
| High selectivity demands | PFR with temperature programming | Profile engineering complexity | Selective catalytic reduction |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Optimum Temperature Progression (OTP)" for a reversible exothermic reaction. Why is it a monotonically decreasing function of conversion?**

**Model Answer:**

The *Optimum Temperature Progression* is the locus of temperatures $T_{opt}(X)$ at which the *net forward reaction rate* attains its maximum value at every level of conversion $X$. Mathematically:

$$T_{opt}(X) = \dfrac{T_e(X)}{1 + \dfrac{R\,T_e(X)}{E}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - X}{X}\right)}$$

It is *decreasing* in $X$ for exothermic reactions because, as conversion increases, the reaction mixture approaches equilibrium. To sustain a high driving force $(1 - X/X_e)$, the temperature must be progressively lowered to favor the forward reaction. Initially (low $X$), high $T$ accelerates kinetics; later (high $X$), low $T$ protects equilibrium.

*(3 marks: 1 — definition; 1 — formula; 1 — physical reasoning for monotonic decrease.)*

### **Question A.2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between an adiabatic CSTR and an adiabatic PFR with respect to their temperature–conversion trajectories for an exothermic reaction.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Adiabatic CSTR | Adiabatic PFR |
|---|---|---|
| Spatial variation | $T$ is uniform, equal to exit $T$ | $T$ varies with position, equals $T(X)$ |
| Operating line on $T$–$X$ | Vertical jump to $(X, T)$ | Continuous curve from $(0, T_0)$ |
| Maximum $T$ reached | $T_{max} = T_0 + \Delta T_{ad} \cdot X_{exit}$ | Approaches $T_0 + \Delta T_{ad}$ asymptotically |
| Hot spot risk | Lower (single-point) | Higher (distributed) |

For a CSTR, the entire reactor operates at the *final* temperature, which can suppress equilibrium conversion if $X$ is high. For a PFR, the temperature rises *gradually* with conversion, so the reaction begins fast and slows as it nears equilibrium.

*(3 marks: distinction table or equivalent textual contrast.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question contains two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Internal choice is provided between **Question A** and **Question B** (set at the question-paper level).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Adapted)]

**(a) [7 marks]** A first-order irreversible exothermic liquid-phase reaction $A \to B$ is carried out in a single adiabatic CSTR. The feed is pure $A$ at $C_{A0} = 4\ \text{kmol/m}^3$, $T_0 = 310\ \text{K}$. Given $k = 0.001 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-3000}{T}\right)\ \text{s}^{-1}$, $(-\Delta H_r) = 20000\ \text{kJ/kmol}$, and $\rho C_p = 4000\ \text{kJ/(m}^3\cdot\text{K)}$. Calculate:
1. The adiabatic temperature rise $\Delta T_{ad}$.
2. The steady-state exit conversion $X$ and exit temperature $T$.
3. The required residence time $\tau$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the conversion achieved above with that obtained from a **two-stage adiabatic CSTR cascade** with the same total volume and interstage cooling back to $T_0$ between stages. Discuss the qualitative reason for the difference.

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 — Adiabatic Temperature Rise:**

$$\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r) C_{A0}}{\rho C_p} = \dfrac{20000 \times 4}{4000} = 20\ \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance (adiabatic CSTR):**

$$T = T_0 + \Delta T_{ad} \cdot X = 310 + 20X$$

**Step 3 — Design equation coupled with Arrhenius:**

$$\tau = \dfrac{X}{k(T)(1 - X)} = \dfrac{X}{(1 - X)\cdot 0.001 \exp(-3000/(310 + 20X))}$$

For a single steady state, we need additional information. Assuming $\tau = 500\ \text{s}$:

| $X$ | $T$ (K) | $k$ (s$^{-1}$) | $\tau_{req}$ (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.50 | 320 | $1.85\times 10^{-4}$ | 5405 |
| 0.70 | 324 | $1.99\times 10^{-4}$ | 11662 |
| 0.30 | 316 | $1.69\times 10^{-4}$ | 2533 |

Trial-and-error: For $\tau = 500$ s, the only physically realistic $X$ is small. Solving iteratively:

$$500 = \dfrac{X}{(1-X)\cdot 0.001\exp(-3000/(310+20X))}$$

Numerically: $X \approx 0.052$ → $T \approx 311.04\ \text{K}$.

**Step 4 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{X \approx 0.052,\quad T \approx 311\ \text{K},\quad \tau = 500\ \text{s}}$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

For a **2-stage CSTR cascade** with interstage cooling back to $T_0 = 310\ \text{K}$, the volume is split equally: $V_1 = V_2 = V/2$, so $\tau_1 = \tau_2 = 250\ \text{s}$ each.

**Stage 1:** $T_1 = 310 + 20 X_1$, design equation gives:

$$250 = \dfrac{X_1}{(1-X_1)\cdot 0.001 \exp(-3000/(310 + 20X_1))}$$

By iteration: $X_1 \approx 0.028$, $T_1 \approx 310.56\ \text{K}$.

**Stage 2:** Feed is at $310\ \text{K}$ with $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_1) = 4(0.972) = 3.888\ \text{kmol/m}^3$. The design equation *relative to stage-2 feed* is:

$$250 = \dfrac{X_2^{'}}{(1-X_2^{'})\cdot 0.001\exp(-3000/(310 + 20X_2^{'}))}$$

with $X_2^{'} \approx 0.028$ again. So $X_2^{'} \approx 0.028$ relative to stage-2 feed.

**Overall conversion:**

$$X_{overall} = 1 - (1 - X_1)(1 - X_2^{'}) = 1 - (0.972)(0.972) = 1 - 0.9448 = 0.055$$

**Comparison:**

| Configuration | Overall $X$ | $T_{max}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Single CSTR | 0.052 | 311 K |
| 2-stage CSTR with intercooling | 0.055 | 311 K (each stage) |

**Qualitative reason:** Even with modest interstage cooling, the cascade approach prevents the temperature from rising as high per stage. Because the rate constant in the first stage is slightly lower, the *average* rate is higher in the cascade due to the resetting of $T$ between stages, allowing each stage to operate closer to the rate-vs-equilibrium optimum. With strong exotherms, the cascade advantage becomes dramatic.

*(Full marks: 7 for (a) — 2 for $\Delta T_{ad}$, 3 for conversion calculation, 2 for residence time. 7 for (b) — 3 for cascade conversion, 2 for comparison, 2 for qualitative reasoning.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Adapted)]

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a **schematic Levenspiel $T$–$X$ plot**, explain the concept of interstage cooling in a PFR train for an exothermic reaction. Show how this configuration increases overall conversion relative to a single adiabatic PFR.

**(b) [7 marks]** A **cold-shot reactor train** is used for the partial oxidation of an aromatic hydrocarbon. The fresh feed enters at $T_0 = 400\ \text{K}$. The first catalyst bed operates adiabatically, raising the temperature to $T_1 = 720\ \text{K}$ with $X_1 = 0.35$. A cold-shot stream at $T_{cs} = 380\ \text{K}$ is mixed in to bring the second bed inlet to $T_2^{in} = 560\ \text{K}$. Calculate:
1. The cold-shot split fraction $f$.
2. The exit temperature $T_2$ of the second bed if conversion in that bed is $X_2^{'} = 0.20$ (relative to bed-2 feed), given $\Delta T_{ad} = 300\ \text{K}$.

#### Model Solution to (a)

A **Levenspiel plot** is a graph of $T$ vs. $X$ on which:
- The **adiabatic operating line** is a straight line from $(0, T_0)$ with slope $\Delta T_{ad}$.
- The **equilibrium curve** $T_e(X)$ bounds the feasible region (decreasing catenary).
- The **rate curve** $r_A(X, T)$ is a family of isotherms.

For a single adiabatic PFR, the operating line intersects the equilibrium curve at the *maximum achievable* conversion $X_{max,1} < 1$.

When **interstage coolers** are inserted:
- The operating line "drops" vertically to a lower $T$ at the end of stage 1.
- It then continues along a *new* adiabatic line of slope $\Delta T_{ad}$ in stage 2.
- This staircase pattern can be made to *track* the OTP curve, pushing overall conversion toward unity.

The diagram (see Mermaid Section 4.2) shows three parallel lines:

1. **Line 1 — single PFR:** Linear from $(0, T_0)$, hits equilibrium at $X_{max,1}$.
2. **Line 2 — 2-stage PFR:** Two segments with one vertical drop.
3. **Line 3 — OTP curve:** Bounded by the equilibrium curve from above.

> **Quantitative Justification:** Each interstage cooler adds a "reset" of temperature, allowing the system to avoid the equilibrium ceiling. With $N$ stages, the achievable conversion $X_N$ approaches $X_e(T_0)$ as $N \to \infty$.

*(7 marks: 2 — schematic description, 2 — explanation of OTP-tracking, 2 — qualitative argument, 1 — limiting case.)*

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Cold-Shot Split Fraction:**

The cold-shot stream at $T_{cs} = 380\ \text{K}$ mixes with the hot reactor-1 exit at $T_1 = 720\ \text{K}$ to give the bed-2 inlet at $T_2^{in} = 560\ \text{K}$.

Energy balance on the mixer (assuming equal $C_p$ and equal $F$ basis):

$$f \cdot T_{cs} + (1 - f) \cdot T_1 = T_2^{in}$$

Solving for $f$:

$$f \cdot 380 + (1 - f) \cdot 720 = 560$$
$$380 f + 720 - 720 f = 560$$
$$-340 f = 560 - 720 = -160$$
$$f = \dfrac{160}{340} = 0.471$$

**Step 2 — Exit Temperature of Bed 2:**

In an adiabatic PFR, $T = T_{in} + \Delta T_{ad} \cdot X^{'}$:

$$T_2 = 560 + 300 \times 0.20 = 560 + 60 = 620\ \text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Overall Conversion Check:**

If desired, overall $X = 1 - (1 - 0.35)(1 - 0.20) = 1 - 0.65 \times 0.80 = 0.48$.

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.471,\qquad T_2 = 620\ \text{K}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 — mixer energy balance, 1 — split calculation, 2 — adiabatic PFR exit, 2 — overall conversion check.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing "residence time" with "space time":** Space time $\tau$ uses $V / V_0$ (volumetric), not $V / F_{A0}$ (molar). Mark down 0.5–1 point for mixing these up.
> 2. **Forgetting to check units of $k$:** If $k$ is in $\text{s}^{-1}$ and $C$ in $\text{kmol/m}^3$, ensure the rate expression is dimensionally consistent.
> 3. **Cold-shot fraction sign errors:** A common mistake is writing the wrong hot/cold stream temperatures in the mixer balance. The *inlet* temperature to the next bed is the *result* of mixing, not the feed.
> 4. **Skipping the OTP formula derivation:** KTU 2024 Scheme places heavy weight (typically 2–3 marks in Part A) on writing the *formula* itself, not just the *concept*.
> 5. **Neglecting the assumption list:** Always state "assuming constant $C_p$, ideal gas, single reaction" before the energy balance.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Reactor Configurations & Temperature Control**

- [x] **OTP decreases with $X$** for exothermic, reversible reactions — the cornerstone of interstage cooling.
- [x] **Adiabatic operating line** on $T$–$X$ plot has slope $\Delta T_{ad}$ for a PFR; for a CSTR, the line is a *vertical jump* to the exit point.
- [x] **Cold-shot reactor** is the industrial standard for very high $\Delta T_{ad}$ (e.g., $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, $\text{NH}_3$).
- [x] **Multiple steady states** appear in adiabatic CSTRs when $\beta\kappa > 17.5$ — always check stability before reporting a steady-state conversion.
- [x] **Hot spot** in a wall-cooled PFR is controlled by the **Damköhler–heat transfer** interplay: large $\text{Da}$ or low $U$ causes runaway.
- [x] **Cascade > single** for exothermic reactions when conversion is high — the temperature reset between stages keeps the rate high without violating equilibrium.
- [x] **Heat integration** within a reactor network (e.g., feed-effluent HX) reduces *external* utility demand — a key metric in pinch analysis.
- [x] **Industrial benchmarks** to remember:
  - Ammonia synthesis: $T \approx 700\text{–}800\ \text{K}$, $P \approx 200\ \text{bar}$, 3–4 catalyst beds with intercooling.
  - Methanol synthesis: $T \approx 500\text{–}550\ \text{K}$, $P \approx 80\ \text{bar}$, cold-shot or tubular reactor.
  - Sulfuric acid (contact process): $\Delta T_{ad} \approx 200\ \text{K}$ per pass, 4-bed converter.
- [x] **Key formula flashcards:**
  - $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r) C_{A0}}{\rho C_p}$
  - $T_{opt}(X) = \dfrac{T_e}{1 + \dfrac{RT_e}{E}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - X}{X}\right)}$
  - $\tau_{CSTR} = \dfrac{X}{k(1 - X)}$
  - $X_{cascade} = 1 - \displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{N}(1 - X_i)$
- [x] **Examiner's mantra:** *State assumptions → Draw the operating line → Solve algebraically → Interpret physically → Sanity-check the result.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Choice of Reactors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT745 - PROCESS INTEGRATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Choice of Reactors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Process Integration**, the **Choice of Reactors** refers to the systematic selection of the most appropriate reactor type (Batch, Plug Flow Reactor/PFR, Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor/CSTR, or their series/parallel combinations) for a given chemical reaction system, with the explicit objective of optimizing **conversion**, **selectivity**, and **heat integration potential** within the overall process flowsheet.

The reactor choice is made by evaluating the interaction between:
- **Reaction kinetics** (order, reversibility, exothermicity/endothermicity)
- **Operating conditions** (temperature, pressure, concentration)
- **Heat transfer requirements** (heat removal for exothermic, heat supply for endothermic)
- **Process integration constraints** (heat recovery, utility consumption, pinch targets)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The choice of reactor is the first design decision in a chemical process and dictates the entire **Grand Composite Curve (GCC)** shape, the **Hot Utility (QH,min)** and **Cold Utility (QC,min)** demands, and the **ΔTmin** of the overall process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are cooking a large pot of rice for a banquet:
- A **Batch Reactor** is like cooking one pot at a time — total control but slow and labor-intensive.
- A **CSTR** is like a continuously stirred boiling pot where you keep adding raw rice and removing cooked rice — uniform temperature, but cooked and uncooked rice mix in the exit stream.
- A **PFR** is like a long conveyor oven where rice enters raw at one end and comes out fully cooked at the other — each grain spends the same time cooking, giving the highest conversion for a given volume.

**Intuitive Rule of Thumb:**
- For **exothermic, non-autocatalytic reactions** → prefer **CSTR** (better temperature control, easier heat removal).
- For **endothermic, high-order reactions** → prefer **PFR** (higher conversion per unit volume).
- For **small-scale, multi-product, high-value chemicals** → prefer **Batch** (flexibility).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reactor Performance Metrics:**
> - **Space-Time (τ)** = V / ν₀ [units: **s** or **h**]
> - **Conversion (X)** = (F_A0 − F_A) / F_A0 [dimensionless]
> - **Selectivity (S)** = (rate of desired product) / (rate of all products) [dimensionless]
> - **Residence Time Distribution (RTD)** governs reactor type performance.

### 1.3 Classification of Reactors for Process Integration

| Reactor Type | Mixing Pattern | Temperature Profile | Typical Use |
|--------------|----------------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Batch** | Perfect mixing, time-varying | Isothermal at any instant | Pharmaceuticals, fine chemicals |
| **PFR** | No back-mixing | Spatial gradient | High conversion, tubular reactions |
| **CSTR** | Perfect back-mixing | Uniform in space | Exothermic, autocatalytic |
| **CSTRs in Series** | Stepwise gradient | Stepwise | Approximating PFR behavior |
| **PFR with Recycle** | Partial back-mixing | Spatial gradient | Autocatalytic reactions |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Design Equations — The Three Pillars

The choice between PFR and CSTR is fundamentally a comparison of the **integrated rate** required to achieve a given conversion.

**For a PFR (differential, plug flow):**

$$V_{PFR} = F_{A0} \int_{0}^{X} \frac{dX}{-r_A(X, T)}$$

**For a Single CSTR (well-mixed):**

$$V_{CSTR} = \frac{F_{A0} \cdot X}{-r_A(X_{out}, T_{out})}$$

**For a Batch Reactor:**

$$t_{batch} = N_{A0} \int_{0}^{X} \frac{dX}{-r_A(X, T)}$$

### 2.2 Key Decision Criteria (Structured Logic)

1. **Step 1 — Is the reaction exothermic or endothermic?**
   - Exothermic → CSTR preferred (uniform cooling, hot spot avoidance).
   - Endothermic → PFR preferred (high-temperature gradient aligned with reaction rate).

2. **Step 2 — What is the reaction order?**
   - **Positive order (n > 0):** Higher concentration → Higher rate. PFR outperforms CSTR.
   - **Negative order (n < 0):** Higher concentration → Lower rate. CSTR can outperform PFR.
   - **Zero order (n = 0):** Both reactors give identical volume.

3. **Step 3 — Is there autocatalysis?**
   - If yes → PFR with recycle or CSTR in series (to retain product/catalyst).
   - If no → Standard comparison applies.

4. **Step 4 — How many reactions occur?**
   - **Single reaction:** Conversion is the only metric.
   - **Multiple reactions (parallel/consecutive):** Selectivity becomes the deciding factor.

5. **Step 5 — What is the heat integration potential?**
   - A CSTR's isothermal exit stream is **easier to integrate** with downstream heat exchangers.
   - A PFR's gradient exit stream can be **pinch-matched** against cold/hot streams in the composite curves.

### 2.3 Why Reactor Choice Matters for Pinch Analysis

The reactor dictates the **shape of the stream in the composite curves**:

- A **CSTR** produces an outlet stream at a **single, uniform temperature** — appears as a **point** in T-H space.
- A **PFR** produces an outlet stream with a **temperature gradient** — appears as a **curve segment** in T-H space.
- The exit temperature of the reactor directly impacts whether a stream crosses the **pinch**, affecting utility targets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** Moving a stream away from the pinch by adjusting reactor temperature or conversion can dramatically reduce utility consumption. Reactor choice is therefore a **degree of freedom** in pinch targeting.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V_{PFR} = F_{A0} \displaystyle\int_0^X \frac{dX}{-r_A}$ | PFR design volume | m³ |
| 2 | $V_{CSTR} = \dfrac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A(X, T)}$ | Single CSTR design volume | m³ |
| 3 | $\tau_{PFR} = \displaystyle\int_0^X \frac{dX}{-r_A}$ | Space-time for PFR | s |
| 4 | $\tau_{CSTR} = \dfrac{X}{-r_A(X, T)}$ | Space-time for CSTR | s |
| 5 | $\dfrac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} = \dfrac{(1+\alpha) \ln(1/(1-X))}{X(1+\alpha - \alpha X)}$ | Levenspiel ratio for n=1 (1st order) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $r_A = k \cdot C_A^n$ | Power-law kinetics | mol/(L·s) |
| 7 | $k = A \exp(-E/RT)$ | Arrhenius equation | varies |
| 8 | $S = \dfrac{r_{desired}}{r_{total}}$ | Selectivity | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{min}$ | Pinch minimum approach temperature | K or °C |
| 10 | $Q_H^{min}, Q_C^{min}$ | Minimum hot/cold utility | kW |

> [!NOTE]
> For **first-order reaction** (n = 1), the volume ratio is:
> $$V_{CSTR} / V_{PFR} = X / (1-X) / \ln[1/(1-X)]$$
> This ratio is always **≥ 1** for positive-order kinetics, confirming PFR is more volume-efficient.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical industries:** Use PFRs for cracking, reforming (high-temperature, endothermic).
- **Polymerization:** CSTRs dominate for exothermic free-radical polymerization (better heat removal).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Batch reactors for multi-product, GMP-compliant operations.
- **Ammonia synthesis:** Multiple adiabatic PFR beds with inter-stage cooling (integrated heat exchange).

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Volume Ratio of CSTR to PFR for First-Order Reaction

We want to derive the comparison metric for a first-order irreversible reaction:
$$-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0} (1-X)$$

**Step 1 — Set up the PFR design equation.**

$$V_{PFR} = F_{A0} \int_0^X \frac{dX}{-r_A}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the rate law.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{PFR} &= F_{A0} \int_0^X \frac{dX}{k C_{A0}(1-X)} \\
&= \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}} \int_0^X \frac{dX}{1-X}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.**

The integral of dX/(1-X) is −ln(1−X). So:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{PFR} &= \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}} \left[-\ln(1-X)\right]_0^X \\
&= \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}} \left[-\ln(1-X) - 0\right] \\
&= \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}} \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-X}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Recall that F_A0 = ν₀ C_A0, so F_A0 / C_A0 = ν₀.**

$$V_{PFR} = \frac{\nu_0}{k} \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-X}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Set up the CSTR design equation.**

$$V_{CSTR} = \frac{F_{A0} X}{-r_A(X)} = \frac{F_{A0} X}{k C_{A0}(1-X)} = \frac{\nu_0 X}{k (1-X)}$$

**Step 6 — Form the volume ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} &= \frac{\dfrac{\nu_0 X}{k(1-X)}}{\dfrac{\nu_0}{k} \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1-X}\right)} \\
&= \frac{X / (1-X)}{\ln[1/(1-X)]}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Verification of physical limits.**

As X → 0, both numerator and denominator → 0, applying L'Hôpital's rule:
- Numerator: d/dX [X/(1-X)] = 1/(1-X)²
- Denominator: d/dX [ln(1/(1-X))] = 1/(1-X)
- Limit → 1/(1-X) → 1 (as X → 0). ✓

As X → 1, ratio → ∞, confirming PFR superiority at high conversion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final result for first-order reaction:**
> $$\boxed{\dfrac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} = \dfrac{X / (1-X)}{\ln[1/(1-X)]}}$$
> This is one of the most tested formulas in KTU examinations.

### 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Temperature Progression (Exothermic Reversible)

For an **exothermic, reversible** reaction (e.g., A ⇌ B), the equilibrium conversion decreases with temperature, but the rate increases. There is an **optimum temperature** T_opt(X) that maximizes the rate at each conversion.

Starting from the rate law:
$$-r_A = k_1 (C_A - C_B / K_{eq})$$

Using Arrhenius:
$$k_1 = A_1 e^{-E_1/RT}, \quad K_{eq} = K_0 e^{-\Delta H_{rxn}/RT}$$

Setting d(−r_A)/dT = 0 at constant X (Optimum Temperature):

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d \ln(-r_A)}{dT} &= \frac{E_1}{RT^2} - \frac{\Delta H_{rxn}}{RT^2 (1 + \epsilon)} = 0 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Solving:
$$T_{opt}(X) = \frac{E_1 - \Delta H_{rxn}}{R \cdot \ln[\text{composition terms}]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** For exothermic reversible reactions, the reactor should be designed with a **decreasing temperature profile** as conversion increases. This is achieved by **inter-stage cooling** in a PFR, or by **CSTRs in series** at decreasing temperatures.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Scenario | Kinetics | Heat Effect | Best Choice | Reasoning |
|----------|----------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| Simple, positive order, endothermic | n > 0 | Endothermic | PFR | High T ↔ high rate; gradient matches |
| Simple, positive order, exothermic | n > 0 | Exothermic | CSTR (or PFR with cooling) | Heat removal easier, isothermal |
| Autocatalytic | A + B → 2B | Either | PFR with recycle | Need to retain product |
| Exothermic reversible | A ⇌ B | Exothermic | CSTRs in series, decreasing T | Matches T_opt(X) |
| Consecutive: A → B → C | n=1 each | — | PFR or batch with short τ | Maximize B selectivity |
| Parallel: A → B, A → C | n=1,2 | — | CSTR (low conc.) | Suppresses side reaction |
| Polymerization (exothermic, high MW) | Complex | Highly exothermic | CSTR (jacketed) | Heat removal, viscosity |

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reactor Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Reaction] --> B{Exothermic?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Positive Order n > 0?}
    B -- No --> D{Positive Order n > 0?}
    C -- Yes --> E{High Conversion Required?}
    C -- No --> F[Consider CSTR or PFR with recycle]
    D -- Yes --> G[Choose PFR: gradient T matches rate]
    D -- No --> H[Choose CSTR: low conc. helps]
    E -- Yes --> I[Multiple CSTRs in series]
    E -- No --> J[Single CSTR with good cooling]
    G --> K[Consider heat integration with pinch]
    I --> K
    F --> K
    H --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Validate with Composite Curves and GCC]
```

### 4.2 Conversion vs. Reactor Volume Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1[PFR] -->|Linear X vs τ| P1[High conversion with low volume]
    R2[CSTR] -->|Exponential X vs τ| P2[Larger volume needed for high X]
    R3[Batch] -->|Time equivalent| P3[Flexible, multi-product]
    P1 --> OUT[Final Selection]
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Heat Integration Block Diagram (Reactor + Pinch)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph FEED[Feed Streams]
        F1[Cold Feed]:::cold
        F2[Hot Feed]:::hot
    end
    subgraph RX[Reactor Block]
        RX1[PFR with Jacket]:::reactor
        RX2[CSTR with Coil]:::reactor
    end
    subgraph HX[Heat Exchanger Network]
        HX1[Hot Stream H1]:::hot
        HX2[Cold Stream C1]:::cold
    end
    subgraph UT[Utilities]
        U1[Hot Utility QHmin]:::utility
        U2[Cold Utility QCmin]:::utility
    end
    F1 --> RX1
    F2 --> RX2
    RX1 --> HX1
    RX2 --> HX2
    HX1 --> U1
    HX2 --> U2
    classDef cold fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef hot fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc3300,color:#000
    classDef reactor fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009933,color:#000
    classDef utility fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The reactor exit temperature determines the position of stream H1 (or C1) in the composite curves. A 10–20 °C shift in reactor T can move a stream across the pinch, drastically changing Q_H,min and Q_C,min.

### 4.4 Conversion vs. Residence Time Plot (PFR vs. CSTR)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PLOT[Conversion X vs. Residence Time tau]
        direction LR
        A0[X = 0] --- A1[X = 0.5 PFR leads] --- A2[X = 0.9 PFR much higher] --- A3[X approaches 1.0]
        B0[X = 0] --- B1[X = 0.4 CSTR slower] --- B2[X = 0.7 CSTR plateau] --- B3[X asymptotically 1.0]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*For a first-order irreversible liquid-phase reaction, derive the expression for the ratio of volumes of a CSTR to a PFR for the same conversion.*

**Model Answer:**

For a first-order reaction, $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1-X)$.

The PFR design equation gives:
$$V_{PFR} = \frac{\nu_0}{k} \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-X}\right)$$

The CSTR design equation gives:
$$V_{CSTR} = \frac{\nu_0 X}{k(1-X)}$$

Taking the ratio:
$$\frac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} = \frac{X / (1-X)}{\ln[1/(1-X)]} \geq 1$$

This proves that **PFR is always more volume-efficient than a single CSTR** for first-order reactions. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*State and explain the criteria for choosing between a CSTR and a PFR for an exothermic reaction.*

**Model Answer:**

For an **exothermic reaction**:
- **CSTR is preferred** because it operates isothermally, allowing uniform jacket/cooling coil temperature → easier heat removal → safer temperature control → avoids runaway reactions.
- **PFR has a temperature gradient**; the early part of the reactor is hot (high rate), but hot spots may occur → harder to cool.
- **Exception:** If the reaction is highly endothermic, PFR allows the temperature to fall along the length, which can be matched to the optimal T_profile by inter-stage heating.

Therefore, for **exothermic, positive-order** reactions, CSTR (or CSTRs in series) is the safer, more controllable choice. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of suitable design equations, explain the procedure for choosing a reactor for a **single, irreversible, exothermic, second-order reaction** aimed at high conversion. Discuss how the choice affects heat integration in a process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A first-order liquid-phase reaction A → Products has k = 0.05 min⁻¹ at 60 °C. The feed contains 2 mol/L of A, flow rate is 100 L/min. Required conversion is 80%. Compare the volumes of (i) a single CSTR, (ii) a PFR, and (iii) two equal-sized CSTRs in series. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

1. Rate law: $-r_A = k C_A^2 = k C_{A0}^2 (1-X)^2$
2. PFR volume: $V_{PFR} = F_{A0} \int_0^X dX / [k C_{A0}^2 (1-X)^2] = \frac{\nu_0}{k C_{A0}} \cdot \frac{X}{1-X}$
3. CSTR volume: $V_{CSTR} = F_{A0} X / [k C_{A0}^2 (1-X)^2] = \frac{\nu_0 X}{k C_{A0} (1-X)^2}$
4. Ratio: $V_{CSTR}/V_{PFR} = 1/(1-X)$ — confirms PFR superiority, more pronounced at high X.
5. **Heat integration impact:**
   - Exothermic → CSTR (isothermal) gives a clean single-temperature stream for pinch integration.
   - PFR's gradient exit can be split into multiple sub-streams matching the composite curves.

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

Given: k = 0.05 min⁻¹, C_A0 = 2 mol/L, ν₀ = 100 L/min, X = 0.80.

**(i) Single CSTR:**
$$V_{CSTR} = \frac{\nu_0 X}{k(1-X)} = \frac{100 \times 0.80}{0.05 \times 0.20} = \frac{80}{0.01} = 8000 \text{ L}$$

**(ii) PFR:**
$$V_{PFR} = \frac{\nu_0}{k} \ln\left(\frac{1}{1-X}\right) = \frac{100}{0.05} \ln(5) = 2000 \times 1.6094 = 3218.8 \text{ L}$$

**(iii) Two CSTRs in series** (X₁ = 1 − √(1−X_total)):
- X_total = 0.80 → 1 − X_total = 0.20 → √0.20 = 0.4472 → X₁ = 0.5528
- $V_1 = \frac{100 \times 0.5528}{0.05 \times (1-0.5528)} = \frac{55.28}{0.05 \times 0.4472} = \frac{55.28}{0.02236} = 2472 \text{ L}$
- $V_2 = \frac{100 \times (0.80-0.5528)}{0.05 \times (1-0.80)} = \frac{100 \times 0.2472}{0.05 \times 0.20} = \frac{24.72}{0.01} = 2472 \text{ L}$
- Total = **4944 L**

**Comparison Table:**

| Reactor Configuration | Volume (L) |
|------------------------|------------|
| Single CSTR | 8000 |
| Two CSTRs in series | 4944 |
| PFR | 3219 |

**Conclusion:** PFR < 2 CSTRs < 1 CSTR. Two CSTRs in series save ~38% volume over a single CSTR, approaching PFR performance.

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **optimum temperature progression** for exothermic reversible reactions. Explain how a series of CSTRs operating at decreasing temperatures approximates this profile. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an exothermic reversible reaction A ⇌ B with first-order forward and reverse kinetics, derive the condition for the optimum temperature at a given conversion X. Given: k₁ = A₁ e^(−E₁/RT), K_eq = K₀ e^(−ΔH/RT), and the rate expression $-r_A = k_1 C_{A0} [(1-X) - X/K_{eq}]$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Optimum Temperature Progression:**
- For exothermic reversible reactions, the **equilibrium constant K_eq decreases** with temperature (Le Chatelier), so equilibrium conversion drops.
- However, the **forward rate constant k₁ increases** with temperature (Arrhenius).
- There is an **optimum temperature T_opt(X)** for each conversion that maximizes the net rate.
- For an exothermic reaction, **T_opt decreases as X increases**.

**Approximation with CSTRs in Series:**
- Each CSTR operates isothermally at one temperature.
- Setting T₁ > T₂ > T₃ > ... allows stepwise cooling, matching the decreasing T_opt(X) profile.
